# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]

**Q: Define Single Exponential Smoothing. State any two assumptions and two limitations of this method. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
Single Exponential Smoothing (SES) is a forecasting technique that updates the forecast for the next period as a weighted average of the actual demand in the current period and the forecast made for the current period, using the recursive formula $F_{t+1} = \alpha Y_t + (1-\alpha)F_t$, where $\alpha$ is the smoothing constant ($0 < \alpha < 1$).

**Assumptions:**
1. The demand series is **stationary** — that is, it has no trend and no seasonality.
2. The most recent observation carries the **highest informational value** for predicting the next period.

**Limitations:**
1. Cannot capture **trend components** (systematic upward or downward movement).
2. Cannot handle **seasonality** (periodic/cyclical demand patterns).
3. Performance is **highly sensitive** to the choice of $\alpha$; no analytical formula exists to compute the optimal $\alpha$.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]

**Q: Explain the role of the smoothing constant $\alpha$ in Single Exponential Smoothing. What happens when $\alpha = 0$ and $\alpha = 1$? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
The smoothing constant $\alpha$ determines how rapidly the forecast reacts to new information. It is the weight assigned to the most recent actual demand $Y_t$, while $(1-\alpha)$ is the weight retained on the previous forecast $F_t$.

- **When $\alpha = 0$:** The formula reduces to $F_{t+1} = F_t$. The forecast **never updates** beyond the initial value $F_1$. The forecast remains frozen regardless of new demand observations.
- **When $\alpha = 1$:** The formula reduces to $F_{t+1} = Y_t$. This is the **naive forecasting** method — the next period's forecast simply equals the current period's actual demand. There is no smoothing at all; the forecast mirrors the actual data exactly with a one-period lag.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students frequently write "$\alpha = 0$ gives the most accurate forecast" or "$\alpha = 1$ gives smooth forecasts." **Both are incorrect** — the former gives no updating, and the latter gives no smoothing. The optimal $\alpha$ is always strictly between 0 and 1.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style]

**(a) The monthly demand (in units) for a product over the past 8 months is given in the table below. Using Single Exponential Smoothing with $\alpha = 0.4$ and initial forecast $F_1 = 40$, compute the forecasts for periods 2 through 9. Also compute the Mean Absolute Deviation (MAD). (7 Marks)**

| Period $t$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Demand $Y_t$ | 42 | 45 | 48 | 46 | 50 | 52 | 55 | 58 |

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1 — Initialize:** $F_1 = 40$.

**Step 2 — Apply recurrence $F_{t+1} = \alpha Y_t + (1-\alpha) F_t$ with $\alpha = 0.4$:**

$$F_2 = (0.4)(42) + (0.6)(40) = 16.8 + 24.0 = 40.80$$

$$F_3 = (0.4)(45) + (0.6)(40.80) = 18.0 + 24.48 = 42.48$$

$$F_4 = (0.4)(48) + (0.6)(42.48) = 19.2 + 25.488 = 44.688$$

$$F_5 = (0.4)(46) + (0.6)(44.688) = 18.4 + 26.8128 = 45.2128$$

$$F_6 = (0.4)(50) + (0.6)(45.2128) = 20.0 + 27.12768 = 47.12768$$

$$F_7 = (0.4)(52) + (0.6)(47.12768) = 20.8 + 28.27661 = 49.07661$$

$$F_8 = (0.4)(55) + (0.6)(49.07661) = 22.0 + 29.44597 = 51.44597$$

$$F_9 = (0.4)(58) + (0.6)(51.44597) = 23.2 + 30.86758 = 54.06758$$

**Step 3 — Errors and Absolute Errors:**

| $t$ | $Y_t$ | $F_t$ | $e_t$ | $\vert e_t \vert$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 2 | 45 | 40.80 | +4.20 | 4.20 |
| 3 | 48 | 42.48 | +5.52 | 5.52 |
| 4 | 46 | 44.688 | +1.312 | 1.312 |
| 5 | 50 | 45.2128 | +4.7872 | 4.7872 |
| 6 | 52 | 47.12768 | +4.87232 | 4.87232 |
| 7 | 55 | 49.07661 | +5.92339 | 5.92339 |
| 8 | 58 | 51.44597 | +6.55403 | 6.55403 |

**Step 4 — MAD Calculation:**

$$\text{MAD} = \frac{4.20 + 5.52 + 1.312 + 4.7872 + 4.87232 + 5.92339 + 6.55403}{7} = \frac{33.16894}{7} = 4.738$$

$$\boxed{F_9 \approx 54.07 \text{ units}, \quad \text{MAD} \approx 4.74 \text{ units}}$$

---

**(b) If the smoothing constant is changed to $\alpha = 0.7$, recalculate the forecast for period 9 and MAD. Compare both MAD values and recommend the better $\alpha$. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1 — Recalculate using $\alpha = 0.7$:**

$$F_2 = (0.7)(42) + (0.3)(40) = 29.4 + 12.0 = 41.40$$

$$F_3 = (0.7)(45) + (0.3)(41.4) = 31.5 + 12.42 = 43.92$$

$$F_4 = (0.7)(48) + (0.3)(43.92) = 33.6 + 13.176 = 46.776$$

$$F_5 = (0.7)(46) + (0.3)(46.776) = 32.2 + 14.0328 = 46.2328$$

$$F_6 = (0.7)(50) + (0.3)(46.2328) = 35.0 + 13.86984 = 48.86984$$

$$F_7 = (0.7)(52) + (0.3)(48.86984) = 36.4 + 14.66095 = 51.06095$$

$$F_8 = (0.7)(55) + (0.3)(51.06095) = 38.5 + 15.31829 = 53.81829$$

$$F_9 = (0.7)(58) + (0.3)(53.81829) = 40.6 + 16.14549 = 56.74549$$

**Step 2 — Absolute Errors:**

| $t$ | $Y_t$ | $F_t$ | $\vert e_t \vert$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 2 | 45 | 41.40 | 3.60 |
| 3 | 48 | 43.92 | 4.08 |
| 4 | 46 | 46.776 | 0.776 |
| 5 | 50 | 46.2328 | 3.7672 |
| 6 | 52 | 48.86984 | 3.13016 |
| 7 | 55 | 51.06095 | 3.93905 |
| 8 | 58 | 53.81829 | 4.18171 |

$$\text{MAD}_{\alpha=0.7} = \frac{3.60 + 4.08 + 0.776 + 3.7672 + 3.13016 + 3.93905 + 4.18171}{7} = \frac{23.47412}{7} = 3.353$$

**Step 3 — Comparison & Recommendation:**

| Metric | $\alpha = 0.4$ | $\alpha = 0.7$ |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| MAD | 4.738 | **3.353** |
| $F_9$ | 54.07 | **56.75** |

Since $\text{MAD}_{\alpha=0.7} < \text{MAD}_{\alpha=0.4}$, the **smoothing constant $\alpha = 0.7$** yields more accurate forecasts. The recommended forecast for period 9 is **56.75 units**.

---

### Question B [Alternate Module Choice — 14 Marks]

**(a) State and prove the equivalence of the two forms of the Single Exponential Smoothing recurrence relation. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer:

**Form 1 (Weighted form):**

$$F_{t+1} = \alpha Y_t + (1-\alpha) F_t$$

**Form 2 (Error-correction form):**

$$F_{t+1} = F_t + \alpha(Y_t - F_t)$$

**Proof:**

Starting from Form 1:

$$F_{t+1} = \alpha Y_t + (1-\alpha) F_t$$

Expanding the second term:

$$F_{t+1} = \alpha Y_t + F_t - \alpha F_t$$

Rearranging:

$$F_{t+1} = F_t + \alpha Y_t - \alpha F_t = F_t + \alpha(Y_t - F_t)$$

This is precisely Form 2. $\blacksquare$

**Engineering Interpretation:** Form 2 explicitly shows that the new forecast equals the old forecast plus a fraction $\alpha$ of the previous period's forecast error. This is computationally convenient in adaptive control systems and ERP dashboards where the *error signal* is monitored in real-time.

---

**(b) Expand the SES recurrence to show that the forecast $F_{t+1}$ is a weighted moving average with geometrically decaying weights summing to unity. For $\alpha = 0.3$, list the weights assigned to the past 5 observations ($Y_t, Y_{t-1}, Y_{t-2}, Y_{t-3}, Y_{t-4}$). (7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer:

**Step 1 — Recursive Substitution:**

$$F_{t+1} = \alpha Y_t + (1-\alpha) F_t$$

Substituting $F_t = \alpha Y_{t-1} + (1-\alpha) F_{t-1}$:

$$F_{t+1} = \alpha Y_t + \alpha(1-\alpha) Y_{t-1} + (1-\alpha)^2 F_{t-1}$$

Continuing this process indefinitely yields:

$$F_{t+1} = \alpha Y_t + \alpha(1-\alpha) Y_{t-1} + \alpha(1-\alpha)^2 Y_{t-2} + \alpha(1-\alpha)^3 Y_{t-3} + \dots$$

**Step 2 — Verification of unit sum:**

$$\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \alpha(1-\alpha)^k = \frac{\alpha}{1-(1-\alpha)} = \frac{\alpha}{\alpha} = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

This confirms SES is a valid **convex combination** (weighted average) of past observations.

**Step 3 — Weights for $\alpha = 0.3$:**

| Observation | Weight Formula | Computed Value |
| :--- | :--- | :---: |
| $Y_t$ (most recent) | $\alpha(1-\alpha)^0 = 0.3$ | **0.3000** |
| $Y_{t-1}$ | $\alpha(1-\alpha)^1 = 0.3 \times 0.7$ | **0.2100** |
| $Y_{t-2}$ | $\alpha(1-\alpha)^2 = 0.3 \times 0.49$ | **0.1470** |
| $Y_{t-3}$ | $\alpha(1-\alpha)^3 = 0.3 \times 0.343$ | **0.1029** |
| $Y_{t-4}$ | $\alpha(1-\alpha)^4 = 0.3 \times 0.2401$ | **0.0720** |

**Sum of these 5 weights:** $0.3000 + 0.2100 + 0.1470 + 0.1029 + 0.0720 = 0.8319$
**Tail weight (all remaining observations):** $1 - 0.8319 = 0.1681$

This confirms that the **most recent observation receives the highest weight (30%)**, while the influence of older observations decays geometrically.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting $F_1 = Y_1$ initialization:** Many students begin computing from $t = 1$ without setting an initial value. This cascades errors through every subsequent forecast.
> 2. **Off-by-one in MAD calculation:** MAD denominator must be $(n - 1)$, not $n$, because $F_1$ has no associated error.
> 3. **Using $\alpha$ outside the range $[0, 1]$:** This is mathematically invalid and will not earn marks even if the arithmetic looks plausible.
> 4. **Confusing SES with Moving Averages:** SES uses *all* past data with decaying weights; Simple Moving Average uses *only the last $n$ periods* with equal weights. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 5. **Not comparing MAD values when $\alpha$ optimization is asked:** Students often compute two MADs but fail to state *which is better* and *why*. Always conclude with a comparison statement.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core Formula:** $F_{t+1} = \alpha Y_t + (1-\alpha) F_t \equiv F_t + \alpha(Y_t - F_t)$.
- **Initialization:** Always begin with $F_1 = Y_1$ (or mean of first few observations).
- **Range of $\alpha$:** Strictly between 0 and 1; typical search range is 0.1 to 0.9 in steps of 0.1.
- **Boundary behaviour:** $\alpha = 0 \Rightarrow$ frozen forecast; $\alpha = 1 \Rightarrow$ naive forecast (no smoothing).
- **Weight structure:** Geometric decay — recent data dominates, but no past observation is ever completely ignored.
- **Applicability:** Only for **stationary** time series — no trend, no seasonality.
- **Error metrics:** MAD (Mean Absolute Deviation) and MSE (Mean Squared Error) are used to validate and optimize $\alpha$.
- **Computational efficiency:** SES requires storing only **two scalars** ($F_t$ and $Y_t$) regardless of historical length — making it ideal for real-time embedded forecasting systems.
- **KTU-specific trap:** When asked to "compare two $\alpha$ values," always end with a **declarative recommendation** based on the **lower MAD/MSE**, never just present the numbers.
- **Industrial relevance:** Used as the default forecasting module in ERP systems for spare parts, raw materials, and consumables with stable consumption patterns.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Double Exponential Smoothing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET601 - OPERATIONS RESEARCH AND PRODUCTION MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Double Exponential Smoothing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Double Exponential Smoothing (DES)**, also known as **Holt's Linear Trend Method** (developed by Charles C. Holt in 1957), is an extension of simple exponential smoothing designed to forecast time series data that exhibit a **linear trend component** in addition to a level component. Unlike simple exponential smoothing, which maintains only a single smoothed statistic, DES simultaneously tracks **two recursive smoothing equations** — one for the **level** ($L_t$) of the series and one for the **trend** ($b_t$) — using two independent smoothing parameters, namely $\alpha$ (alpha) for the level and $\beta$ (beta) for the trend.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 3 (Forecasting):**
> Double Exponential Smoothing is officially classified as a **trend-adjusted (second-order) exponential smoothing technique**. It is prescribed in the PCPET601 syllabus under the unit *Forecasting Models* and is a high-weightage topic for Part B (14-mark) derivations and numerical problems in the End Semester Examination (ESE).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car on a highway. Your **speedometer** tells you how fast you are going *right now* — this is the **current speed (level)**. But to predict *where you will be in 10 minutes*, you also need to know whether you are **accelerating, decelerating, or cruising at a constant speed** — this is the **trend**.

Simple exponential smoothing is like knowing only your current speed; it assumes you will continue at the same pace forever. Double exponential smoothing is like having both a speedometer and an **accelerometer** — it tracks both how fast you are going *and* whether you are speeding up or slowing down, so it can project your future position along the road more intelligently.

In forecasting language:
- The **level** $L_t$ answers: *"What is the baseline value of the series at time $t$?"*
- The **trend** $b_t$ answers: *"By how much is the series rising or falling per period?"*

### 1.3 Key Components and Boundary Conditions

| Component | Symbol | Range / Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Smoothing constant (level) | $\alpha$ | $0 < \alpha < 1$ | Weight given to the most recent observation |
| Smoothing constant (trend) | $\beta$ | $0 < \beta < 1$ | Weight given to the most recent trend estimate |
| Forecast horizon | $m$ | $m \geq 1$ | Number of periods ahead being forecasted |
| Level estimate | $L_t$ | — | Smoothed value of the series at time $t$ |
| Trend estimate | $b_t$ | — | Smoothed slope of the series at time $t$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Convention:** Higher $\alpha$ makes the level react faster to recent shocks; higher $\beta$ makes the trend react faster to changes in slope. In KTU board problems, $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are typically supplied as decimals in the question itself.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logical Structure of Holt's Method

Double Exponential Smoothing operates through **three coupled recursive equations** that must be evaluated sequentially for each time period:

**Step 1 — Level Update Equation:**
The smoothed level at time $t$ is a weighted average of:
- The actual observation $Y_t$ (weighted by $\alpha$), and
- The previous level plus the previous trend $(L_{t-1} + b_{t-1})$, which represents the **projected value** from the prior period (weighted by $1 - \alpha$).

**Step 2 — Trend Update Equation:**
The smoothed trend at time $t$ is a weighted average of:
- The change between the current and previous level $(L_t - L_{t-1})$ (weighted by $\beta$), and
- The previous trend estimate $b_{t-1}$ (weighted by $1 - \beta$).

**Step 3 — Forecast Equation:**
To forecast $m$ periods into the future from origin time $t$, the model extrapolates the line defined by the current level and trend.

> [!TIP]
> **The "Why" Behind the Recursion:** Each equation is *self-correcting*. If the actual demand $Y_t$ deviates from what was forecast, the level equation absorbs the error; if the slope of the series changes, the trend equation absorbs it. Together, they prevent the systematic under- or over-forecasting that plagues simple exponential smoothing when trends exist.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Purpose / When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Level Update | $L_t = \alpha Y_t + (1 - \alpha)(L_{t-1} + b_{t-1})$ | Update smoothed level at every period |
| 2 | Trend Update | $b_t = \beta (L_t - L_{t-1}) + (1 - \beta) b_{t-1}$ | Update smoothed trend at every period |
| 3 | m-Step Ahead Forecast | $F_{t+m} = L_t + m \cdot b_t$ | Forecast $m$ periods ahead from origin $t$ |
| 4 | Initial Level | $L_1 = Y_1$ | First-period initialization (common choice) |
| 5 | Initial Trend | $b_1 = Y_2 - Y_1$ | First-period initialization (common choice) |
| 6 | Alternative Initial Trend | $b_1 = \dfrac{Y_n - Y_1}{n - 1}$ | Average slope over full series |
| 7 | MAD (in-sample error) | $\text{MAD} = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{t=1}^{n} \vert Y_t - F_t \vert$ | Measure of forecast accuracy |
| 8 | MSE (in-sample error) | $\text{MSE} = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{t=1}^{n} (Y_t - F_t)^2$ | Penalizes large errors more heavily |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units & Boundary Conditions:**
> - Both $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are **dimensionless constants** lying strictly in the open interval $(0, 1)$.
> - $L_t$ carries the same units as $Y_t$.
> - $b_t$ carries the units of $Y_t$ **per period** (i.e., the slope of the trend line).
> - The forecast $F_{t+m}$ is valid only for the same data series; extrapolating $m$ beyond a few periods is risky because trend uncertainty compounds.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering & Management Utility

Double Exponential Smoothing is the workhorse forecasting model in production and operations management for scenarios such as:
- **Demand forecasting** of products showing steady growth (e.g., smartphone sales, electric vehicle uptake).
- **Inventory planning** where ignoring trend leads to chronic stockouts or overstocking.
- **Sales budgeting** for businesses experiencing linear expansion across regions.
- **Capacity planning** in manufacturing facilities whose workload rises predictably.
- **Financial revenue forecasting** for companies in growth phase.

It is also the **conceptual foundation** for advanced models like Holt-Winters (triple exponential smoothing, which adds seasonality) and the damped trend method used in modern supply-chain software.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Three Core Equations

The Double Exponential Smoothing model is derived by assuming that the underlying data series can be represented locally by a **straight line**:

$$
\hat{Y}_{t} = L_{t-1} + b_{t-1}
$$

This represents the one-step-ahead forecast made at the end of period $t-1$. We seek to "smooth" this forecast using observed data.

**Step A — Forecast Error Definition:**
At period $t$, the forecast error (the surprise) is:
$$
e_t = Y_t - (L_{t-1} + b_{t-1})
$$

**Step B — Level Correction:**
We correct the previous forecast by a fraction $\alpha$ of the error:
$$
L_t = L_{t-1} + b_{t-1} + \alpha \, e_t
$$

Substituting $e_t$:
$$
L_t = L_{t-1} + b_{t-1} + \alpha \bigl[Y_t - (L_{t-1} + b_{t-1})\bigr]
$$

Simplifying:
$$
L_t = \alpha Y_t + (1 - \alpha)(L_{t-1} + b_{t-1}) \qquad \text{(Level Equation)}
$$

**Step C — Trend Correction:**
Similarly, the change in trend from $b_{t-1}$ to $b_t$ is corrected by a fraction $\beta$ of the most recent level change $(L_t - L_{t-1})$:
$$
b_t = b_{t-1} + \beta \bigl[(L_t - L_{t-1}) - b_{t-1}\bigr]
$$

Simplifying:
$$
b_t = \beta (L_t - L_{t-1}) + (1 - \beta) b_{t-1} \qquad \text{(Trend Equation)}
$$

**Step D — Multi-Step Forecast:**
Because the level and trend are assumed to evolve linearly, the forecast $m$ periods ahead from origin $t$ is the line extrapolation:
$$
F_{t+m} = L_t + m \cdot b_t \qquad \text{(Forecast Equation)}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem Statement (Adapted for PCPET601):**
A production manager records the monthly demand (in hundreds of units) for a new product over 6 months as follows:

| Period $t$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Demand $Y_t$ | 100 | 110 | 125 | 140 | 155 | 168 |

Use Double Exponential Smoothing with **$\alpha = 0.3$** and **$\beta = 0.2$** to:
1. Compute the smoothed level and trend for each period.
2. Compute the one-step-ahead forecast for periods 2 through 6.
3. Forecast demand for period 7 and period 8.

**Step 0 — Initialize:**
$$
L_1 = Y_1 = 100
$$
$$
b_1 = Y_2 - Y_1 = 110 - 100 = 10
$$

**Step 1 — Period 2:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
L_2 &= \alpha Y_2 + (1 - \alpha)(L_1 + b_1) \\
&= 0.3(110) + 0.7(100 + 10) \\
&= 33.0 + 77.0 = 110.0
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_2 &= \beta(L_2 - L_1) + (1 - \beta) b_1 \\
&= 0.2(110.0 - 100) + 0.8(10) \\
&= 2.0 + 8.0 = 10.0
\end{aligned}
$$

Forecast made at end of period 1 for period 2:
$$
F_2 = L_1 + 1 \cdot b_1 = 100 + 10 = 110.0
$$

**Step 2 — Period 3:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
L_3 &= 0.3(125) + 0.7(110.0 + 10.0) \\
&= 37.5 + 84.0 = 121.5
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_3 &= 0.2(121.5 - 110.0) + 0.8(10.0) \\
&= 2.3 + 8.0 = 10.3
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
F_3 = L_2 + b_2 = 110.0 + 10.0 = 120.0
$$

**Step 3 — Period 4:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
L_4 &= 0.3(140) + 0.7(121.5 + 10.3) \\
&= 42.0 + 92.26 = 134.26
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_4 &= 0.2(134.26 - 121.5) + 0.8(10.3) \\
&= 2.552 + 8.24 = 10.792
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
F_4 = L_3 + b_3 = 121.5 + 10.3 = 131.8
$$

**Step 4 — Period 5:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
L_5 &= 0.3(155) + 0.7(134.26 + 10.792) \\
&= 46.5 + 101.536 = 148.036
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_5 &= 0.2(148.036 - 134.26) + 0.8(10.792) \\
&= 2.7552 + 8.6336 = 11.3888
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
F_5 = L_4 + b_4 = 134.26 + 10.792 = 145.052
$$

**Step 5 — Period 6:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
L_6 &= 0.3(168) + 0.7(148.036 + 11.3888) \\
&= 50.4 + 111.5974 = 161.9974
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_6 &= 0.2(161.9974 - 148.036) + 0.8(11.3888) \\
&= 2.79228 + 9.11104 = 11.90332
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
F_6 = L_5 + b_5 = 148.036 + 11.3888 = 159.4248
$$

**Step 6 — Multi-Step Future Forecasts:**

Forecast for period 7 ($m = 1$):
$$
F_7 = L_6 + 1 \cdot b_6 = 161.9974 + 11.90332 = 173.90072
$$

Forecast for period 8 ($m = 2$):
$$
F_8 = L_6 + 2 \cdot b_6 = 161.9974 + 23.80664 = 185.80404
$$

### 3.3 Consolidated Results Table

| Period $t$ | $Y_t$ | $L_t$ | $b_t$ | $F_t$ (one-step ahead) | Error $(Y_t - F_t)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 100 | 100.000 | 10.000 | — | — |
| 2 | 110 | 110.000 | 10.000 | 110.000 | 0.000 |
| 3 | 125 | 121.500 | 10.300 | 120.000 | 5.000 |
| 4 | 140 | 134.260 | 10.792 | 131.800 | 8.200 |
| 5 | 155 | 148.036 | 11.389 | 145.052 | 9.948 |
| 6 | 168 | 161.997 | 11.903 | 159.425 | 8.575 |
| **7** | — | — | — | **173.901** | — |
| **8** | — | — | — | **185.804** | — |

### 3.4 In-Sample Accuracy Check

Mean Absolute Deviation (MAD):
$$
\text{MAD} = \frac{0 + 5.0 + 8.2 + 9.948 + 8.575}{5} = \frac{31.723}{5} = 6.3446
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation for Production Planning:** The MAD of **6.34 (hundred units)** suggests that on average, the one-step-ahead forecast deviates by about 634 units from actual demand. The production manager may set a safety stock equal to or exceeding the MAD to maintain desired service levels.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Holt's DES Algorithm

The diagram below maps the information flow inside one iteration of Double Exponential Smoothing, showing how the two smoothing equations exchange information recursively.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START OF PERIOD t] --> B[Retrieve Previous Level L sub t-1]
    A --> C[Retrieve Previous Trend b sub t-1]
    A --> D[Observe Actual Demand Y sub t]
    B --> E[Compute Projected Value L sub t-1 PLUS b sub t-1]
    C --> E
    E --> F[Compute Forecast Error e sub t equals Y sub t MINUS projected]
    D --> F
    F --> G[LEVEL EQUATION L sub t equals alpha Y sub t PLUS 1 minus alpha times projected]
    G --> H[TREND EQUATION b sub t equals beta times L sub t MINUS L sub t-1 PLUS 1 minus beta times b sub t-1]
    H --> I[Store New Level L sub t]
    H --> J[Store New Trend b sub t]
    I --> K{Decision m greater than 0 ?}
    J --> K
    K -- YES --> L[Compute Forecast F sub t+m equals L sub t PLUS m times b sub t]
    K -- NO --> M[Return to START OF PERIOD t plus 1]
    L --> M
    M --> A
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Flow of the Forecast Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Historical Series Y1 to Yn] --> R2[Initialization L1 equals Y1 and b1 equals Y2 minus Y1]
    R2 --> R3[Choose alpha and beta in 0 to 1]
    R3 --> R4[Iterate t equals 2 to n using Level and Trend Equations]
    R4 --> R5[Generate Forecasts F sub t plus m equals L sub t PLUS m times b sub t]
    R5 --> R6[Evaluate MAD or MSE]
    R6 --> R7[Accept or Tune alpha and beta]
```

### 4.3 Comparative Schematic — SES vs DES vs TES

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SES[Simple Exponential Smoothing] -->|Single alpha| SES1[Only Level L sub t captured]
    DES[Double Exponential Smoothing Holt Method] -->|Two constants alpha and beta| DES1[Level L sub t PLUS Trend b sub t captured]
    TES[Triple Exponential Smoothing Holt Winters Method] -->|Three constants alpha beta and gamma| TES1[Level PLUS Trend PLUS Seasonality captured]
    SES1 -.Underestimates data with trend.-> NOTE[Limitation]
    DES1 -.Handles Linear Trend Data.-> NOTE
    TES1 -.Handles Trend plus Seasonal Data.-> NOTE
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each box represents a computational block. The arrows depict data flow direction. The decision diamond in the first diagram represents the conditional branch that decides whether a future forecast is required at the current step.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*State the three recursive equations used in Double Exponential Smoothing (Holt's method). Explain the role of the two smoothing constants $\alpha$ and $\beta$.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The three recursive equations are:
$$
L_t = \alpha Y_t + (1 - \alpha)(L_{t-1} + b_{t-1})
$$
$$
b_t = \beta(L_t - L_{t-1}) + (1 - \beta) b_{t-1}
$$
$$
F_{t+m} = L_t + m \cdot b_t
$$
Here $\alpha$ is the **level smoothing constant** ($0 < \alpha < 1$) controlling the weight assigned to the most recent observation. **$\beta$** is the **trend smoothing constant** ($0 < \beta < 1$) controlling how rapidly the model adapts to changes in the slope of the series. Both constants must lie between 0 and 1, with higher values producing more reactive forecasts.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Why is simple exponential smoothing inadequate for time series with a linear trend? How does Double Exponential Smoothing overcome this limitation?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Simple exponential smoothing uses only one smoothing constant $\alpha$ and produces forecasts of the form $F_{t+1} = L_t$, which is **constant across all future horizons**. When the data exhibits a trend, the constant forecast systematically **lags behind** the actual values, producing persistent positive or negative forecast errors.

Double Exponential Smoothing overcomes this by maintaining **two components** — a level $L_t$ and a trend $b_t$ — and forecasting using $F_{t+m} = L_t + m \cdot b_t$. The trend term $b_t$ allows the forecast to extrapolate linearly into the future, capturing the upward or downward slope of the series. This makes DES suitable for trended data while preserving the simplicity of exponential weighting.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Derive the level and trend update equations for Double Exponential Smoothing from the principle of forecast error correction. State the multi-step forecast equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The annual sales (in lakhs of rupees) of a manufacturing firm over the last 6 years are given below:

| Year $t$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sales $Y_t$ | 50 | 56 | 64 | 72 | 80 | 88 |

Using Double Exponential Smoothing with $\alpha = 0.4$ and $\beta = 0.3$, determine the smoothed level and trend for each year. Also forecast the sales for year 7 and year 8. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Assume the one-step-ahead forecast at the end of period $t-1$ is:
$$
\hat{Y}_t = L_{t-1} + b_{t-1}
$$

The forecast error at time $t$ is:
$$
e_t = Y_t - \hat{Y}_t = Y_t - (L_{t-1} + b_{t-1})
$$

To correct the level, add a fraction $\alpha$ of the error to the previous projection:
$$
L_t = L_{t-1} + b_{t-1} + \alpha e_t
$$

Expanding:
$$
\begin{aligned}
L_t &= L_{t-1} + b_{t-1} + \alpha \bigl[Y_t - (L_{t-1} + b_{t-1})\bigr] \\
&= \alpha Y_t + (1 - \alpha)(L_{t-1} + b_{t-1})
\end{aligned}
$$

To correct the trend, add a fraction $\beta$ of the discrepancy between the latest level change and the previous trend:
$$
b_t = b_{t-1} + \beta \bigl[(L_t - L_{t-1}) - b_{t-1}\bigr]
$$

Expanding:
$$
b_t = \beta (L_t - L_{t-1}) + (1 - \beta) b_{t-1}
$$

The forecast $m$ periods ahead:
$$
F_{t+m} = L_t + m \cdot b_t
$$

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Initialize:
$$
L_1 = Y_1 = 50, \quad b_1 = Y_2 - Y_1 = 56 - 50 = 6
$$

**Period 2:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
L_2 &= 0.4(56) + 0.6(50 + 6) = 22.4 + 33.6 = 56.0 \\
b_2 &= 0.3(56 - 50) + 0.7(6) = 1.8 + 4.2 = 6.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Period 3:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
L_3 &= 0.4(64) + 0.6(56 + 6) = 25.6 + 37.2 = 62.8 \\
b_3 &= 0.3(62.8 - 56) + 0.7(6) = 2.04 + 4.2 = 6.24
\end{aligned}
$$

**Period 4:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
L_4 &= 0.4(72) + 0.6(62.8 + 6.24) = 28.8 + 41.424 = 70.224 \\
b_4 &= 0.3(70.224 - 62.8) + 0.7(6.24) = 2.2272 + 4.368 = 6.5952
\end{aligned}
$$

**Period 5:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
L_5 &= 0.4(80) + 0.6(70.224 + 6.5952) = 32 + 46.09152 = 78.09152 \\
b_5 &= 0.3(78.09152 - 70.224) + 0.7(6.5952) = 2.360256 + 4.61664 = 6.976896
\end{aligned}
$$

**Period 6:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
L_6 &= 0.4(88) + 0.6(78.09152 + 6.976896) = 35.2 + 51.041054 = 86.241054 \\
b_6 &= 0.3(86.241054 - 78.09152) + 0.7(6.976896) = 2.44486 + 4.883827 = 7.328687
\end{aligned}
$$

**Forecasts:**
$$
F_7 = L_6 + 1 \cdot b_6 = 86.241054 + 7.328687 \approx 93.57
$$
$$
F_8 = L_6 + 2 \cdot b_6 = 86.241054 + 14.657374 \approx 100.90
$$

**Summary Table:**

| Period $t$ | $Y_t$ | $L_t$ | $b_t$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 50 | 50.000 | 6.000 |
| 2 | 56 | 56.000 | 6.000 |
| 3 | 64 | 62.800 | 6.240 |
| 4 | 72 | 70.224 | 6.595 |
| 5 | 80 | 78.092 | 6.977 |
| 6 | 88 | 86.241 | 7.329 |
| **Forecast 7** | — | — | **93.57** |
| **Forecast 8** | — | — | **100.90** |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Discuss the limitations of simple exponential smoothing. Explain how Double Exponential Smoothing addresses these limitations, and state the assumptions underlying Holt's method. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Quarterly production output (in tonnes) of a process industry for 6 quarters is:

| Quarter $t$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Output $Y_t$ | 200 | 220 | 245 | 265 | 290 | 310 |

Apply Double Exponential Smoothing with $\alpha = 0.5$ and $\beta = 0.4$. Find the smoothed level and trend. Compute the forecast for quarter 7 and quarter 8. Also compute MAD. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) Theoretical Discussion (7 marks):**

**Limitations of Simple Exponential Smoothing:**
1. It maintains only a single smoothed statistic (the level), so it cannot model **trended data**.
2. The forecast is **flat** ($F_{t+1} = L_t$), causing systematic lag when the series rises or falls.
3. It is unsuitable for series with **systematic pattern** beyond a constant mean.

**How DES Overcomes These:**
1. DES adds a **trend component** $b_t$ that updates recursively with parameter $\beta$.
2. The forecast $F_{t+m} = L_t + m b_t$ **extrapolates linearly**, eliminating the flat-forecast lag.
3. Two parameters $\alpha$ and $\beta$ give flexibility in separately tuning responsiveness to level changes and slope changes.

**Assumptions of Holt's Method:**
1. The series can be locally approximated by a **straight line**.
2. Errors are random and the trend is **stable or slowly changing**.
3. Both $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are **constant** across all periods (no adaptive switching).
4. No **seasonality** is present (handled by triple smoothing).
5. Initial values $L_1$ and $b_1$ are reasonable approximations of the true starting level and trend.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Initialize:
$$
L_1 = 200, \quad b_1 = 220 - 200 = 20
$$

**Quarter 2:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
L_2 &= 0.5(220) + 0.5(200 + 20) = 110 + 110 = 220 \\
b_2 &= 0.4(220 - 200) + 0.6(20) = 8 + 12 = 20
\end{aligned}
$$

**Quarter 3:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
L_3 &= 0.5(245) + 0.5(220 + 20) = 122.5 + 120 = 242.5 \\
b_3 &= 0.4(242.5 - 220) + 0.6(20) = 9 + 12 = 21
\end{aligned}
$$

**Quarter 4:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
L_4 &= 0.5(265) + 0.5(242.5 + 21) = 132.5 + 131.75 = 264.25 \\
b_4 &= 0.4(264.25 - 242.5) + 0.6(21) = 8.7 + 12.6 = 21.3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Quarter 5:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
L_5 &= 0.5(290) + 0.5(264.25 + 21.3) = 145 + 142.775 = 287.775 \\
b_5 &= 0.4(287.775 - 264.25) + 0.6(21.3) = 9.41 + 12.78 = 22.19
\end{aligned}
$$

**Quarter 6:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
L_6 &= 0.5(310) + 0.5(287.775 + 22.19) = 155 + 154.9825 = 309.9825 \\
b_6 &= 0.4(309.9825 - 287.775) + 0.6(22.19) = 8.883 + 13.314 = 22.197
\end{aligned}
$$

**Forecasts:**
$$
F_7 = 309.9825 + 1(22.197) = 332.18
$$
$$
F_8 = 309.9825 + 2(22.197) = 354.38
$$

**MAD Computation:**
One-step-ahead forecasts for quarters 2–6:
$$
\begin{aligned}
F_2 &= 200 + 20 = 220, \quad e_2 = 220 - 220 = 0 \\
F_3 &= 220 + 20 = 240, \quad e_3 = 245 - 240 = 5 \\
F_4 &= 242.5 + 21 = 263.5, \quad e_4 = 265 - 263.5 = 1.5 \\
F_5 &= 264.25 + 21.3 = 285.55, \quad e_5 = 290 - 285.55 = 4.45 \\
F_6 &= 287.775 + 22.19 = 309.965, \quad e_6 = 310 - 309.965 = 0.035
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\text{MAD} = \frac{0 + 5 + 1.5 + 4.45 + 0.035}{5} = \frac{10.985}{5} = 2.197
$$

**Summary Table:**

| Quarter $t$ | $Y_t$ | $L_t$ | $b_t$ | $F_t$ | $\vert e_t \vert$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 200 | 200.000 | 20.000 | — | — |
| 2 | 220 | 220.000 | 20.000 | 220.000 | 0.000 |
| 3 | 245 | 242.500 | 21.000 | 240.000 | 5.000 |
| 4 | 265 | 264.250 | 21.300 | 263.500 | 1.500 |
| 5 | 290 | 287.775 | 22.190 | 285.550 | 4.450 |
| 6 | 310 | 309.983 | 22.197 | 309.965 | 0.035 |
| **7** | — | — | — | **332.180** | — |
| **8** | — | — | — | **354.380** | — |

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Verified from KTU Valuation Keys):**
> 1. **Initialization Error:** Students frequently set $L_1 = Y_1$ but then forget that the first **forecast** $F_1$ is undefined (or zero), which propagates into MAD calculations. Always start MAD from period 2 onward.
> 2. **Trend Calculation Slip:** A very common error is computing $b_2 = b_1$ (i.e., forgetting that the trend equation must also be applied at $t = 2$). The trend equation is **active at every period $t \geq 2$**, not just $t \geq 3$.
> 3. **Confusion Between $Y_t$ and $F_t$:** When computing MAD, students sometimes use $L_t$ in place of $F_t$ for the error term. The correct definition is $e_t = Y_t - F_t$ where $F_t = L_{t-1} + b_{t-1}$.
> 4. **Forecast Horizon Mix-up:** When asked to forecast period 7 from period 6 data, students mistakenly use $m = 0$ or $m = 2$. The correct offset is $m = 1$ for the next period, $m = 2$ for the one after, etc.
> 5. **Rounding Discipline:** KTU examiners usually accept answers correct to **two decimal places**. Carrying 6 decimal places wastes time; rounding to 2 places (or 3 for intermediate steps) is the safe KTU convention.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- **Name:** Double Exponential Smoothing = **Holt's Linear Trend Method** (1957).
- **When to Use:** Data has a **linear trend** but no seasonality.
- **Number of Parameters:** Two — $\alpha$ (level) and $\beta$ (trend), each in $(0, 1)$.
- **Three Core Equations:**
  - Level: $L_t = \alpha Y_t + (1 - \alpha)(L_{t-1} + b_{t-1})$
  - Trend: $b_t = \beta (L_t - L_{t-1}) + (1 - \beta) b_{t-1}$
  - Forecast: $F_{t+m} = L_t + m \cdot b_t$
- **Initialization Convention:** $L_1 = Y_1$ and $b_1 = Y_2 - Y_1$ (most common in KTU problems).
- **Forecast Error:** $e_t = Y_t - F_t = Y_t - (L_{t-1} + b_{t-1})$.
- **Accuracy Metrics:** MAD = mean of $\vert e_t \vert$; MSE = mean of $e_t^2$.
- **Limitation:** Cannot handle **seasonality** — extend to Holt-Winters for that.
- **Tuning:** Optimal $\alpha, \beta$ minimize MAD/MSE; solvers iterate over a grid in $(0, 1) \times (0, 1)$.
- **Edge Cases:** If $\beta = 0$, DES collapses to Simple Exponential Smoothing (trend frozen at $b_1$). If $\alpha = 1$, the level always equals $Y_t$ (no smoothing).
- **Difference from SES:** SES has only $L_t$; DES has $L_t + m \cdot b_t$ (trend-aware).
- **Difference from Holt-Winters:** DES has 2 components; Holt-Winters (Triple ES) has 3 — level, trend, **and** seasonal index.
- **Order of Computation:** Always compute $L_t$ **first**, then $b_t$, since $b_t$ depends on $L_t$.
- **Formula Sheet Storage:** Memorize the three equations in the exact order shown; KTU valuation keys accept the equations in any equivalent algebraic form, but the standard form above is the most commonly expected.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Regression Analysis** is a statistical modeling technique used to investigate and quantify the relationship between a dependent variable (response/outcome variable) and one or more independent variables (predictors/regressors). It serves as a cornerstone of inferential statistics, predictive analytics, and operations research, enabling engineers and managers to forecast outcomes, optimize processes, and identify causal relationships in production and management systems.

Mathematically, the general regression model can be expressed as:

$$Y = f(X_1, X_2, \dots, X_k) + \varepsilon$$

Where:
- **$Y$** = Dependent (response) variable
- **$X_1, X_2, \dots, X_k$** = Independent (predictor) variables
- **$f(\cdot)$** = Systematic function describing the relationship
- **$\varepsilon$** = Random error term (residual), assumed to be normally distributed with **zero mean** and **constant variance** $\sigma^2$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** For PCPET601 Module 3, the primary emphasis is on **Linear Regression (Simple and Multiple)** with derivations based on the **Method of Least Squares (OLS)**. Non-linear regression concepts may be touched upon only at a conceptual level.

## 1.2 Types of Regression Techniques Covered in Module 3

| S.No | Regression Type | Number of Predictors | Functional Form | Primary Use |
|------|-----------------|---------------------|-----------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Simple Linear Regression** | 1 ($X$) | Straight line | Trend analysis, basic forecasting |
| 2 | **Multiple Linear Regression** | 2 or more ($X_1, X_2, \dots, X_k$) | Hyperplane | Multi-factor production analysis |
| 3 | **Polynomial Regression** | 1 (with powers) | Curve of degree $n$ | Non-linear trend fitting |
| 4 | **Non-Linear Regression** | 1 or more | Exponential, logarithmic | Specialized engineering applications |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bouncing Ball of Production"

Imagine you are a quality control engineer observing how the **speed of a conveyor belt** ($X$) affects the **number of defective items produced per hour** ($Y$) in a manufacturing plant.

- You cannot predict the exact number of defects for any given speed because of random fluctuations (electrical noise, worker fatigue, micro-vibrations). However, you **observe a clear trend**: as speed increases, defects rise in a roughly linear pattern.
- **Regression Analysis** is like drawing the *single best-fitting straight line* through a scatter of points on a graph. This line does not pass through every point exactly (because of randomness), but it captures the underlying *systematic trend*.
- Once the line is drawn, you can **predict** defects for any new belt speed, **quantify uncertainty**, and **measure the strength** of the speed-defect relationship.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction:** *Correlation* measures the **strength and direction** of an association between two variables, while *Regression* provides a **predictive equation** quantifying *how* one variable changes as the other changes. Regression is inherently directional ($X \rightarrow Y$), while correlation is symmetric.

## 1.4 Significance in Production Management (Engineering Context)

In the context of **Operations Research and Production Management**, regression is indispensable for:

- **Demand Forecasting:** Predicting future sales ($Y$) based on advertising spend, price, and seasonality ($X_i$).
- **Quality Control:** Modeling defect rates as a function of machine temperature, pressure, or operator skill.
- **Cost Analysis:** Estimating total cost ($Y$) from production volume ($X$) using cost-volume-profit models.
- **Workforce Planning:** Predicting labor hours required based on order quantity.
- **Inventory Optimization:** Establishing safety stock levels via lead-time demand regression.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Simple Linear Regression Model — Theoretical Foundation

The **Simple Linear Regression (SLR)** model postulates that the relationship between two variables $X$ and $Y$ is captured by:

$$Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_i + \varepsilon_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, n$$

Where:
- **$Y_i$** = Observed value of the dependent variable for the $i^{th}$ observation
- **$X_i$** = Known value of the independent variable
- **$\beta_0$** = **Population intercept** (value of $Y$ when $X = 0$)
- **$\beta_1$** = **Population slope** (change in $Y$ per unit change in $X$)
- **$\varepsilon_i$** = Random error component for the $i^{th}$ observation

The corresponding **estimated (fitted) regression equation** is:

$$\hat{Y}_i = b_0 + b_1 X_i$$

Where $b_0$ and $b_1$ are the **sample estimates** of $\beta_0$ and $\beta_1$ obtained via the **Ordinary Least Squares (OLS)** method.

## 2.2 The Method of Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) — Logic Steps

**Step 1 — Define the Residual:**
The vertical deviation between the observed value $Y_i$ and the predicted value $\hat{Y}_i$:

$$e_i = Y_i - \hat{Y}_i = Y_i - (b_0 + b_1 X_i)$$

**Step 2 — Construct the Minimization Objective:**
Minimize the **Sum of Squared Errors (SSE)**:

$$S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} e_i^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (Y_i - b_0 - b_1 X_i)^2$$

**Step 3 — Apply First-Order Optimality Conditions:**
Partial derivatives of $S$ with respect to $b_0$ and $b_1$ must equal **zero** at the optimum:

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial b_0} = -2 \sum (Y_i - b_0 - b_1 X_i) = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial b_1} = -2 \sum X_i(Y_i - b_0 - b_1 X_i) = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve the Resulting Normal Equations:**

$$n b_0 + b_1 \sum X_i = \sum Y_i$$

$$b_0 \sum X_i + b_1 \sum X_i^2 = \sum X_i Y_i$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These two **normal equations** are the cornerstone of every KTU regression problem. Memorize the structure: the coefficient matrix is built from sums of $1$, $X$, and $X^2$ — always symmetric.

## 2.3 Solving the Normal Equations (Closed-Form Solutions)

From the two normal equations, solving simultaneously using Cramer's rule or elimination yields:

$$b_1 = \frac{n \sum X_i Y_i - \sum X_i \sum Y_i}{n \sum X_i^2 - (\sum X_i)^2} = \frac{\sum (X_i - \bar{X})(Y_i - \bar{Y})}{\sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2}$$

$$b_0 = \bar{Y} - b_1 \bar{X}$$

Where $\bar{X} = \dfrac{\sum X_i}{n}$ and $\bar{Y} = \dfrac{\sum Y_i}{n}$ are the sample means.

## 2.4 Goodness of Fit — Coefficient of Determination ($R^2$)

The **coefficient of determination** $R^2$ quantifies the proportion of total variation in $Y$ explained by the regression model:

$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{SSE}{SST} = \frac{SSR}{SST}$$

Where:
- **$SST = \sum (Y_i - \bar{Y})^2$** = Total Sum of Squares
- **$SSR = \sum (\hat{Y}_i - \bar{Y})^2$** = Regression Sum of Squares (explained)
- **$SSE = \sum (Y_i - \hat{Y}_i)^2$** = Error Sum of Squares (unexplained)

**Identity:** $SST = SSR + SSE$ (fundamental decomposition)

**Range:** $0 \leq R^2 \leq 1$. A value closer to **1** indicates superior fit.

## 2.5 Standard Error of Estimate

$$S_e = \sqrt{\frac{SSE}{n-2}} = \sqrt{\frac{\sum (Y_i - \hat{Y}_i)^2}{n-2}}$$

This measures the **average vertical scatter** of observed points around the regression line.

## 2.6 Multiple Linear Regression (Brief Overview)

For $k$ independent variables:

$$Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_{1i} + \beta_2 X_{2i} + \dots + \beta_k X_{ki} + \varepsilon_i$$

In matrix notation: $\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$, and the OLS estimator is:

$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{Y}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Questions on multiple regression in Module 3 typically ask for the matrix formulation only. Numerical computation is restricted to **simple linear regression** due to the time constraint of a 14-mark question.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Regression Techniques

| S.No | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Remarks |
|------|---------|----------------------|------------------|
| 1 | SLR Model | $Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_i + \varepsilon_i$ | $\varepsilon_i \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$ |
| 2 | Fitted Model | $\hat{Y}_i = b_0 + b_1 X_i$ | Point predictor |
| 3 | Residual | $e_i = Y_i - \hat{Y}_i$ | Observed minus predicted |
| 4 | SSE Objective | $S = \sum (Y_i - b_0 - b_1 X_i)^2$ | Minimize over $b_0, b_1$ |
| 5 | Normal Eqn 1 | $n b_0 + b_1 \sum X_i = \sum Y_i$ | From $\partial S/\partial b_0 = 0$ |
| 6 | Normal Eqn 2 | $b_0 \sum X_i + b_1 \sum X_i^2 = \sum X_i Y_i$ | From $\partial S/\partial b_1 = 0$ |
| 7 | Slope Estimator | $b_1 = \dfrac{n\sum X_i Y_i - \sum X_i \sum Y_i}{n\sum X_i^2 - (\sum X_i)^2}$ | Always compute denominator first |
| 8 | Intercept Estimator | $b_0 = \bar{Y} - b_1 \bar{X}$ | Use sample means |
| 9 | Alternate Slope Form | $b_1 = \dfrac{\sum (X_i - \bar{X})(Y_i - \bar{Y})}{\sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2}$ | Numerically equivalent |
| 10 | Total SS | $SST = \sum (Y_i - \bar{Y})^2$ | Total variation in $Y$ |
| 11 | Regression SS | $SSR = \sum (\hat{Y}_i - \bar{Y})^2$ | Explained variation |
| 12 | Error SS | $SSE = \sum (Y_i - \hat{Y}_i)^2$ | Unexplained variation |
| 13 | Coefficient of Determination | $R^2 = 1 - \dfrac{SSE}{SST}$ | $0 \leq R^2 \leq 1$ |
| 14 | Standard Error | $S_e = \sqrt{\dfrac{SSE}{n-2}}$ | Degrees of freedom $= n-2$ |
| 15 | Correlation Coeff. | $r = b_1 \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2}}{\sqrt{\sum (Y_i - \bar{Y})^2}}$ | $-1 \leq r \leq 1$ |
| 16 | Multiple Reg. (Matrix) | $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{Y}$ | Requires $(p+1) \times (p+1)$ inverse |
| 17 | Adjusted $R^2$ | $\bar{R}^2 = 1 - (1-R^2)\dfrac{n-1}{n-k-1}$ | For multiple regression |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of OLS Estimators from First Principles

We begin with the minimization objective:

$$S(b_0, b_1) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (Y_i - b_0 - b_1 X_i)^2$$

**Step 1 — First Partial Derivative with respect to $b_0$:**

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial b_0} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} 2(Y_i - b_0 - b_1 X_i)(-1) = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} (Y_i - b_0 - b_1 X_i)$$

**Step 2 — Set to Zero (Optimality Condition for $b_0$):**

$$-2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} (Y_i - b_0 - b_1 X_i) = 0 \implies \sum_{i=1}^{n} Y_i - n b_0 - b_1 \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i = 0$$

This simplifies directly to **Normal Equation 1**:

$$\boxed{\,n b_0 + b_1 \sum X_i = \sum Y_i\,}$$

**Step 3 — First Partial Derivative with respect to $b_1$:**

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial b_1} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} 2(Y_i - b_0 - b_1 X_i)(-X_i) = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i(Y_i - b_0 - b_1 X_i)$$

**Step 4 — Set to Zero (Optimality Condition for $b_1$):**

$$-2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i(Y_i - b_0 - b_1 X_i) = 0$$

Expanding:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i Y_i - b_0 \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i - b_1 \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i^2 = 0$$

This simplifies to **Normal Equation 2**:

$$\boxed{\,b_0 \sum X_i + b_1 \sum X_i^2 = \sum X_i Y_i\,}$$

**Step 5 — Solve via Elimination:**

From Normal Equation 1: $b_0 = \dfrac{\sum Y_i - b_1 \sum X_i}{n}$

Substituting into Normal Equation 2:

$$\left(\frac{\sum Y_i - b_1 \sum X_i}{n}\right) \sum X_i + b_1 \sum X_i^2 = \sum X_i Y_i$$

Multiplying through by $n$:

$$\sum X_i \sum Y_i - b_1 (\sum X_i)^2 + n b_1 \sum X_i^2 = n \sum X_i Y_i$$

Rearranging for $b_1$:

$$b_1 \left[ n \sum X_i^2 - (\sum X_i)^2 \right] = n \sum X_i Y_i - \sum X_i \sum Y_i$$

$$\boxed{\,b_1 = \frac{n \sum X_i Y_i - \sum X_i \sum Y_i}{n \sum X_i^2 - (\sum X_i)^2}\,}$$

**Step 6 — Recover $b_0$ using Means:**

Since $\bar{X} = \dfrac{\sum X_i}{n}$ and $\bar{Y} = \dfrac{\sum Y_i}{n}$, dividing Normal Equation 1 by $n$:

$$b_0 + b_1 \bar{X} = \bar{Y} \implies \boxed{\,b_0 = \bar{Y} - b_1 \bar{X}\,}$$

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example — KTU Numerical Pattern

**Problem Statement:** A production manager wishes to study the relationship between **machine operating temperature ($X$ in °C)** and the **number of defective items produced per shift ($Y$)**. The following data were collected over 6 shifts:

| Shift ($i$) | Temperature $X_i$ (°C) | Defects $Y_i$ |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| 1 | 30 | 5 |
| 2 | 40 | 8 |
| 3 | 50 | 10 |
| 4 | 60 | 13 |
| 5 | 70 | 16 |
| 6 | 80 | 20 |

**Required:**
(a) Fit a simple linear regression model $\hat{Y} = b_0 + b_1 X$ using the method of least squares.
(b) Compute the coefficient of determination $R^2$ and interpret it.
(c) Predict the number of defects when the temperature is **$75$°C**.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step A — Construct the Computation Table:**

| $i$ | $X_i$ | $Y_i$ | $X_i^2$ | $Y_i^2$ | $X_i Y_i$ |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| 1 | 30 | 5 | 900 | 25 | 150 |
| 2 | 40 | 8 | 1600 | 64 | 320 |
| 3 | 50 | 10 | 2500 | 100 | 500 |
| 4 | 60 | 13 | 3600 | 169 | 780 |
| 5 | 70 | 16 | 4900 | 256 | 1120 |
| 6 | 80 | 20 | 6400 | 400 | 1600 |
| **Sum** | **330** | **72** | **19900** | **1014** | **4470** |

So we have:
- $n = 6$
- $\sum X_i = 330$
- $\sum Y_i = 72$
- $\sum X_i^2 = 19900$
- $\sum X_i Y_i = 4470$
- $\bar{X} = 330/6 = 55$
- $\bar{Y} = 72/6 = 12$

**Step B — Compute the Slope $b_1$:**

Applying the formula:

$$b_1 = \frac{n \sum X_i Y_i - \sum X_i \sum Y_i}{n \sum X_i^2 - (\sum X_i)^2}$$

Substituting numerical values:

$$b_1 = \frac{6 \times 4470 - 330 \times 72}{6 \times 19900 - (330)^2}$$

Compute the numerator:

$$6 \times 4470 = 26820, \quad 330 \times 72 = 23760$$

$$\text{Numerator} = 26820 - 23760 = 3060$$

Compute the denominator:

$$6 \times 19900 = 119400, \quad (330)^2 = 108900$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 119400 - 108900 = 10500$$

Therefore:

$$b_1 = \frac{3060}{10500} = 0.2914 \text{ defects per °C}$$

**Interpretation:** For every 1°C increase in machine temperature, the number of defective items increases by approximately **0.29 units**, on average.

**Step C — Compute the Intercept $b_0$:**

$$b_0 = \bar{Y} - b_1 \bar{X} = 12 - (0.2914)(55)$$

$$b_0 = 12 - 16.0286 = -4.0286$$

**Step D — Write the Fitted Regression Equation:**

$$\boxed{\,\hat{Y} = -4.0286 + 0.2914 X\,}$$

**Step E — Predict Defects at $X = 75$°C:**

$$\hat{Y} = -4.0286 + 0.2914 \times 75$$

$$\hat{Y} = -4.0286 + 21.857 = 17.83 \approx 18 \text{ defects}$$

**Step F — Compute the Coefficient of Determination $R^2$:**

First compute $SST$:

$$SST = \sum Y_i^2 - \frac{(\sum Y_i)^2}{n} = 1014 - \frac{(72)^2}{6} = 1014 - 864 = 150$$

Compute $SSR$:

$$SSR = b_1 \left[ \sum X_i Y_i - \frac{\sum X_i \sum Y_i}{n} \right]$$

$$SSR = 0.2914 \times \left[ 4470 - \frac{330 \times 72}{6} \right] = 0.2914 \times [4470 - 3960]$$

$$SSR = 0.2914 \times 510 = 148.63$$

Compute $SSE$:

$$SSE = SST - SSR = 150 - 148.63 = 1.37$$

Therefore:

$$R^2 = \frac{SSR}{SST} = \frac{148.63}{150} = 0.9909 \text{ or } 99.09\%$$

**Interpretation:** Approximately **99.09%** of the variation in the number of defective items is explained by the linear relationship with machine temperature. This indicates an **excellent fit**.

**Step G — Standard Error of Estimate:**

$$S_e = \sqrt{\frac{SSE}{n-2}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.37}{4}} = \sqrt{0.3425} = 0.585 \text{ defects}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Verifying the Normal Equations

To verify the result, substitute $b_0$ and $b_1$ back into the two normal equations:

**Verification of Normal Equation 1:**

$$n b_0 + b_1 \sum X_i = 6(-4.0286) + (0.2914)(330) = -24.172 + 96.162 = 71.99 \approx 72 = \sum Y_i \quad \checkmark$$

**Verification of Normal Equation 2:**

$$b_0 \sum X_i + b_1 \sum X_i^2 = (-4.0286)(330) + (0.2914)(19900)$$

$$= -1329.438 + 5798.86 = 4469.42 \approx 4470 = \sum X_i Y_i \quad \checkmark$$

Both normal equations are satisfied within rounding tolerance, confirming the correctness of the estimates.

## 4.1 Regression Analysis Workflow — Process Block Diagram

The following block diagram illustrates the standard **KTU-recommended procedure** for executing a regression analysis from raw data to validated model:
