# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Define the Method of Least Squares. State the two normal equations obtained when fitting a simple linear regression model $Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X + \varepsilon$ using OLS.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Method of Least Squares (OLS)** is a mathematical optimization technique used to estimate the parameters of a regression model by **minimizing the sum of the squared residuals** between observed and predicted values:

$$\min_{b_0, b_1} S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (Y_i - b_0 - b_1 X_i)^2$$

Setting the partial derivatives $\partial S/\partial b_0 = 0$ and $\partial S/\partial b_1 = 0$ yields the two **normal equations**:

$$nb_0 + b_1 \sum X_i = \sum Y_i$$

$$b_0 \sum X_i + b_1 \sum X_i^2 = \sum X_i Y_i$$

Solving these simultaneously gives the OLS estimators $b_0$ and $b_1$. **(3 Marks)**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is the coefficient of determination ($R^2$) in regression analysis? Mention its range of values and significance.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Coefficient of Determination ($R^2$)** is a goodness-of-fit measure that represents the proportion of the total variation in the dependent variable $Y$ that is **explained** by the regression model. It is defined as:

$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{SSE}{SST} = \frac{SSR}{SST}$$

**Range:** $0 \leq R^2 \leq 1$

**Significance:**
- $R^2 = 1$: Perfect fit; the model explains **all** variation in $Y$.
- $R^2 = 0$: Model is useless; no linear relationship exists.
- A higher $R^2$ indicates that the fitted regression line closely tracks the observed data and is therefore more reliable for prediction.

For example, $R^2 = 0.99$ implies that **99%** of variability in $Y$ is accounted for by the linear model. **(3 Marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: A logistics company recorded the following data relating the distance of delivery ($X$ in km) to the delivery time ($Y$ in hours).**

| Trip | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| $X$ (km) | 10 | 15 | 20 | 25 | 30 | 35 | 40 | 45 |
| $Y$ (hrs) | 1.2 | 1.5 | 1.8 | 2.0 | 2.3 | 2.6 | 2.9 | 3.1 |

**(a) [7 Marks]** Fit a simple linear regression model $\hat{Y} = b_0 + b_1 X$ using the method of least squares.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compute the coefficient of determination $R^2$, the standard error $S_e$, and estimate the delivery time for a distance of **50 km**.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

### **Part (a) — Fitting the Regression Model [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Construct the Computation Table:**

| $i$ | $X_i$ | $Y_i$ | $X_i^2$ | $Y_i^2$ | $X_i Y_i$ |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| 1 | 10 | 1.2 | 100 | 1.44 | 12.0 |
| 2 | 15 | 1.5 | 225 | 2.25 | 22.5 |
| 3 | 20 | 1.8 | 400 | 3.24 | 36.0 |
| 4 | 25 | 2.0 | 625 | 4.00 | 50.0 |
| 5 | 30 | 2.3 | 900 | 5.29 | 69.0 |
| 6 | 35 | 2.6 | 1225 | 6.76 | 91.0 |
| 7 | 40 | 2.9 | 1600 | 8.41 | 116.0 |
| 8 | 45 | 3.1 | 2025 | 9.61 | 139.5 |
| **$\Sigma$** | **220** | **17.4** | **7100** | **41.00** | **536.0** |

Thus:
- $n = 8$
- $\sum X_i = 220$, $\sum Y_i = 17.4$
- $\sum X_i^2 = 7100$, $\sum Y_i^2 = 41.00$, $\sum X_i Y_i = 536.0$
- $\bar{X} = 220/8 = 27.5$, $\bar{Y} = 17.4/8 = 2.175$

**Step 2 — Compute Slope $b_1$:**

$$b_1 = \frac{n\sum X_i Y_i - \sum X_i \sum Y_i}{n\sum X_i^2 - (\sum X_i)^2}$$

$$b_1 = \frac{8(536.0) - (220)(17.4)}{8(7100) - (220)^2}$$

$$b_1 = \frac{4288 - 3828}{56800 - 48400} = \frac{460}{8400} = 0.05476$$

**Step 3 — Compute Intercept $b_0$:**

$$b_0 = \bar{Y} - b_1 \bar{X} = 2.175 - (0.05476)(27.5)$$

$$b_0 = 2.175 - 1.5059 = 0.6691$$

**Step 4 — Fitted Regression Equation:**

$$\boxed{\,\hat{Y} = 0.6691 + 0.05476\,X\,}$$

**Interpretation:** Each additional kilometer adds approximately **0.055 hours (3.3 minutes)** to delivery time.

### **Part (b) — Goodness of Fit and Prediction [7 Marks]**

**Step 5 — Compute SST:**

$$SST = \sum Y_i^2 - \frac{(\sum Y_i)^2}{n} = 41.00 - \frac{(17.4)^2}{8} = 41.00 - 37.845 = 3.155$$

**Step 6 — Compute SSR:**

$$SSR = b_1^2 \sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2 = b_1 \left[ \sum X_i Y_i - \frac{\sum X_i \sum Y_i}{n} \right]$$

$$SSR = 0.05476 \times \left[ 536.0 - \frac{220 \times 17.4}{8} \right] = 0.05476 \times [536.0 - 478.5]$$

$$SSR = 0.05476 \times 57.5 = 3.1487$$

**Step 7 — Compute SSE:**

$$SSE = SST - SSR = 3.155 - 3.1487 = 0.0063$$

**Step 8 — Compute $R^2$:**

$$R^2 = \frac{SSR}{SST} = \frac{3.1487}{3.155} = 0.9980 \text{ or } 99.80\%$$

**Step 9 — Compute Standard Error $S_e$:**

$$S_e = \sqrt{\frac{SSE}{n-2}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.0063}{6}} = \sqrt{0.00105} = 0.0324 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 10 — Predict Delivery Time at $X = 50$ km:**

$$\hat{Y} = 0.6691 + 0.05476 \times 50 = 0.6691 + 2.738 = 3.407 \text{ hours}$$

$$\boxed{\,\hat{Y}_{X=50} \approx 3.41 \text{ hours}\,}$$

**Conclusion:** The model exhibits an excellent fit ($R^2 = 99.80\%$) and predicts that a **50 km** delivery will take approximately **3 hours and 24 minutes**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: A manufacturing firm gathered data on the number of workers ($X$) and the corresponding daily output ($Y$ in units) for 7 production lines:**

| Line | A | B | C | D | E | F | G |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| $X$ | 5 | 7 | 9 | 11 | 13 | 15 | 17 |
| $Y$ | 45 | 55 | 65 | 70 | 78 | 85 | 92 |

**(a) [7 Marks]** Estimate the regression coefficients $b_0$ and $b_1$ using the OLS method and write the regression equation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compute and interpret the correlation coefficient $r$ and the coefficient of determination $R^2$. Also, predict the output when **$X = 20$ workers** are deployed.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

### **Part (a) — Estimating Regression Coefficients [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Build the Computation Table:**

| $i$ | $X_i$ | $Y_i$ | $X_i^2$ | $Y_i^2$ | $X_i Y_i$ |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| 1 | 5 | 45 | 25 | 2025 | 225 |
| 2 | 7 | 55 | 49 | 3025 | 385 |
| 3 | 9 | 65 | 81 | 4225 | 585 |
| 4 | 11 | 70 | 121 | 4900 | 770 |
| 5 | 13 | 78 | 169 | 6084 | 1014 |
| 6 | 15 | 85 | 225 | 7225 | 1275 |
| 7 | 17 | 92 | 289 | 8464 | 1564 |
| **$\Sigma$** | **77** | **490** | **959** | **35948** | **5818** |

Thus:
- $n = 7$
- $\sum X_i = 77$, $\sum Y_i = 490$
- $\sum X_i^2 = 959$, $\sum Y_i^2 = 35948$, $\sum X_i Y_i = 5818$
- $\bar{X} = 77/7 = 11$, $\bar{Y} = 490/7 = 70$

**Step 2 — Compute Slope $b_1$:**

$$b_1 = \frac{n \sum X_i Y_i - \sum X_i \sum Y_i}{n \sum X_i^2 - (\sum X_i)^2} = \frac{7(5818) - (77)(490)}{7(959) - (77)^2}$$

$$b_1 = \frac{40726 - 37730}{6713 - 5929} = \frac{2996}{784} = 3.8214$$

**Step 3 — Compute Intercept $b_0$:**

$$b_0 = \bar{Y} - b_1 \bar{X} = 70 - (3.8214)(11) = 70 - 42.0357 = 27.9643$$

**Step 4 — Fitted Regression Equation:**

$$\boxed{\,\hat{Y} = 27.9643 + 3.8214\,X\,}$$

### **Part (b) — Correlation, Determination, and Prediction [7 Marks]**

**Step 5 — Compute Sums of Squares:**

$$S_{XX} = \sum X_i^2 - \frac{(\sum X_i)^2}{n} = 959 - \frac{5929}{7} = 959 - 847 = 112$$

$$S_{YY} = \sum Y_i^2 - \frac{(\sum Y_i)^2}{n} = 35948 - \frac{240100}{7} = 35948 - 34300 = 1648$$

$$S_{XY} = \sum X_i Y_i - \frac{\sum X_i \sum Y_i}{n} = 5818 - \frac{37730}{7} = 5818 - 5390 = 428$$

**Step 6 — Compute Correlation Coefficient $r$:**

$$r = \frac{S_{XY}}{\sqrt{S_{XX} \cdot S_{YY}}} = \frac{428}{\sqrt{112 \times 1648}} = \frac{428}{\sqrt{184576}} = \frac{428}{429.62} = 0.9962$$

**Interpretation:** $r = 0.9962$ indicates a **very strong positive linear correlation** between the number of workers and the daily output. As workforce size increases, output rises consistently.

**Step 7 — Compute $R^2$:**

$$R^2 = r^2 = (0.9962)^2 = 0.9924 \text{ or } 99.24\%$$

**Interpretation:** About **99.24%** of the variation in output is explained by the linear relationship with the number of workers — a near-perfect model fit.

**Step 8 — Verify via SST/SSR/SSE:**

$$SST = S_{YY} = 1648$$

$$SSR = b_1 \cdot S_{XY} = 3.8214 \times 428 = 1635.56$$

$$SSE = SST - SSR = 1648 - 1635.56 = 12.44$$

$$R^2 = \frac{1635.56}{1648} = 0.9924 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 9 — Predict Output for $X = 20$:**

$$\hat{Y}_{X=20} = 27.9643 + (3.8214)(20) = 27.9643 + 76.428 = 104.39$$

$$\boxed{\,\hat{Y}_{X=20} \approx 104 \text{ units}\,}$$

**Conclusion:** Deploying 20 workers is projected to yield approximately **104 units** of daily output. The extremely high $R^2$ value lends strong credibility to this forecast within the observed data range.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Deductions:**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in $b_0$ Formula:** Students often forget that $b_0 = \bar{Y} - b_1 \bar{X}$ — subtracting in the wrong order yields a negative intercept when it should be positive (or vice versa), losing **1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting the Identity $SST = SSR + SSE$:** Examiners explicitly test this by asking students to verify their $R^2$ via two different paths. Failure to show both routes costs **1 mark**.
> 3. **Rounding Too Early:** Computing intermediate values to 2 decimals before substituting into $b_1$ propagates large errors. Retain **at least 4 decimal places** during intermediate steps.
> 4. **Ignoring Units in Interpretation:** A fitted equation $\hat{Y} = 0.6691 + 0.05476X$ must be interpreted as "0.055 hours per km," not just "0.055 per unit." Examiners award **1 mark** for correct unit-based interpretation.
> 5. **Confusing $r$ and $R^2$:** Remember: $R^2 = r^2$, but they convey **different information**. $r$ measures linear association strength (sign + magnitude), while $R^2$ measures explained proportion (always non-negative).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

Use this rapid-revision checklist before entering the KTU examination hall:

- ✅ **Regression Model:** $Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_i + \varepsilon_i$, with $\varepsilon_i \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$
- ✅ **OLS Objective:** Minimize $S = \sum (Y_i - b_0 - b_1 X_i)^2$
- ✅ **Two Normal Equations:**
  - $nb_0 + b_1 \sum X_i = \sum Y_i$
  - $b_0 \sum X_i + b_1 \sum X_i^2 = \sum X_i Y_i$
- ✅ **Slope Formula (memorize verbatim):**
  $$b_1 = \frac{n\sum X_i Y_i - \sum X_i \sum Y_i}{n\sum X_i^2 - (\sum X_i)^2}$$
- ✅ **Intercept Formula:** $b_0 = \bar{Y} - b_1 \bar{X}$
- ✅ **Variance Decomposition Identity:** $SST = SSR + SSE$
- ✅ **$R^2$ Interpretation:** Proportion of $Y$-variation explained by the model; **higher is better**.
- ✅ **Standard Error:** $S_e = \sqrt{\dfrac{SSE}{n-2}}$, uses $n-2$ degrees of freedom (two estimated parameters).
- ✅ **Correlation Coefficient:** $r = \dfrac{S_{XY}}{\sqrt{S_{XX} \cdot S_{YY}}}$, with $-1 \leq r \leq 1$.
- ✅ **Always construct the computation table** with columns $X_i$, $Y_i$, $X_i^2$, $Y_i^2$, $X_i Y_i$ — this is the **single most important step** for earning full marks.
- ✅ **Always verify the normal equations** by substituting back $b_0$ and $b_1$.
- ✅ **Multiple Regression Matrix Form:** $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{Y}$ — required only for theoretical questions in KTU Module 3.
- ✅ **Assumptions of OLS:** Linearity, Independence, Homoscedasticity (constant variance), Normality of residuals.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measures of Forecast Accuracy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET601 - OPERATIONS RESEARCH AND PRODUCTION MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Measures of Forecast Accuracy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forecast Accuracy** is the degree to which a forecasting method successfully predicts the actual future demand or value. It is quantitatively measured by computing the deviations (errors) between the **forecasted values** ($F_t$) generated by the model and the **actual observed values** ($A_t$) over a specified historical period.

In Operations Research and Production Management, **Measures of Forecast Accuracy** are standardized statistical metrics used to evaluate, compare, and continuously monitor the performance of forecasting models. The discipline of measuring accuracy is formally known as **Forecast Error Analysis**, and it is the cornerstone of the **Forecast Monitoring and Control** phase in any production planning system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Forecasting Error (e_t):** The numerical difference between actual demand and the forecast made for that period.
> $$e_t = A_t - F_t$$
> Where:
> $A_t$ = Actual demand in period $t$
> $F_t$ = Forecasted demand for period $t$

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are an **archer** trying to hit the bullseye of a target 50 meters away. After shooting 10 arrows, you want to evaluate how good you are.

- **Actual Demand ($A_t$)** = The exact center of the bullseye
- **Forecast ($F_t$)** = Where your arrow actually landed
- **Error ($e_t$)** = The distance between where you aimed and where you hit

To judge your skill, you do not just look at one arrow — you look at a **pattern of distances**. Some metrics measure the **average distance** (like Mean Absolute Deviation), some penalize **far-off shots more harshly** (like Mean Squared Error), and some convert the distance into a **percentage of the target** (like Mean Absolute Percentage Error).

> [!TIP]
> **Why does this matter in production management?** A factory producing perishable goods (like milk or bread) cannot afford large forecasting errors. Measuring accuracy helps managers choose between forecasting models (e.g., Moving Average vs. Exponential Smoothing) and decide when a model is "drifting" and needs recalibration.

The four cornerstone metrics standardized in KTU syllabus are:
1. **Mean Absolute Deviation (MAD)**
2. **Mean Squared Error (MSE)**
3. **Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE)**
4. **Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE)**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Breakdown of Each Accuracy Measure

#### A) Mean Absolute Deviation (MAD)
- **Definition:** The average of the absolute values of the forecast errors over a given number of periods.
- **Why use it?** It gives a clear, intuitive measure of the typical magnitude of error, regardless of whether the error is positive (under-forecasting) or negative (over-forecasting).
- **Formula Logic:** It uses absolute value ($\vert \cdot \vert$) to prevent positive and negative errors from cancelling each other out.

#### B) Mean Squared Error (MSE)
- **Definition:** The average of the *squared* forecast errors.
- **Why use it?** Squaring heavily penalizes large errors, making MSE particularly useful when large deviations are undesirable (e.g., forecasting critical safety stock levels).
- **Formula Logic:** By squaring, an error of 10 contributes 100, while an error of 5 contributes only 25.

#### C) Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE)
- **Definition:** The square root of the MSE.
- **Why use it?** RMSE brings the error back to the **same units** as the original forecast (e.g., units, liters, kilograms), making it interpretable for decision-makers. It is the most common metric in academic and industrial benchmarking.

#### D) Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE)
- **Definition:** The average of absolute percentage errors.
- **Why use it?** It expresses error as a **percentage of actual demand**, enabling comparison across different products, scales, or units. A MAPE of 5% means forecasts are off by 5% on average.
- **Caution:** MAPE is undefined when actual demand is zero, and it is biased toward forecasts that under-predict.

#### E) Mean Error (ME) / Bias
- **Definition:** The simple average of forecast errors (with sign).
- **Why use it?** A ME close to zero means the model is **unbiased**. A consistently positive ME means the model systematically under-forecasts.

#### F) Cumulative Sum of Forecast Errors (CFE) / Running Sum of Forecast Errors (RSFE)
- **Definition:** The cumulative algebraic sum of forecast errors.
- **Why use it?** Used in conjunction with MAD to compute the **Tracking Signal**, which detects systematic bias.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Table for KTU Board Exams**

| **Measure** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Key Property** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Forecast Error | $e_t = A_t - F_t$ | Same as demand | Signed quantity |
| Mean Error (Bias) | $ME = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} e_t$ | Same as demand | Indicates over/under-forecasting |
| Mean Absolute Deviation | $MAD = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} \vert e_t \vert$ | Same as demand | Robust, intuitive average error |
| Mean Squared Error | $MSE = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} e_t^2$ | (Demand units)$^2$ | Penalizes large errors heavily |
| Root Mean Squared Error | $RMSE = \sqrt{MSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} e_t^2}$ | Same as demand | Most interpretable squared metric |
| Mean Absolute Percentage Error | $MAPE = \frac{100}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} \left\vert \frac{e_t}{A_t} \right\vert \;(\%)$ | Percentage | Scale-free, allows comparison |
| Cumulative Forecast Error | $CFE = \sum_{t=1}^{n} e_t$ | Same as demand | Detects cumulative drift |
| Tracking Signal | $TS = \frac{CFE}{MAD}$ | Dimensionless | Monitors systematic bias |
| Theil's U Statistic | $U = \frac{\sqrt{\sum (F_{t+1}-A_{t+1})^2}}{\sqrt{\sum A_{t+1}^2} + \sqrt{\sum F_{t+1}^2}}$ | Dimensionless | Compares to naive forecast |

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Supply Chain & Inventory Management:** MAD and RMSE help decide safety stock levels.
- **Machine Learning Validation:** MSE and RMSE are foundational loss functions in regression models.
- **Energy Load Forecasting:** MAPE is the gold standard in power grid demand prediction.
- **Sales & Operations Planning (S&OP):** Tracking Signal flags when a forecasting model needs replacement.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of MAD from Raw Errors

The derivation starts with the fundamental definition of a forecast error.

**Step 1:** Define the forecast error for a single period.
$$e_t = A_t - F_t$$

**Step 2:** Since we want to measure the *magnitude* of error (not its direction), we take the absolute value of each error. This is critical because positive and negative errors should not cancel.
$$\vert e_t \vert = \vert A_t - F_t \vert$$

**Step 3:** Sum all absolute errors across $n$ historical periods.
$$\text{Total Absolute Error} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \vert A_t - F_t \vert$$

**Step 4:** Compute the arithmetic mean by dividing by the number of periods $n$.
$$MAD = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{t=1}^{n} \vert e_t \vert$$

### 3.2 Derivation of MSE

**Step 1:** Begin with the error.
$$e_t = A_t - F_t$$

**Step 2:** Square each error. The squaring operation serves two purposes:
- It eliminates negative signs.
- It heavily penalizes larger errors (since $e^2$ grows nonlinearly).
$$e_t^2 = (A_t - F_t)^2$$

**Step 3:** Sum the squared errors.
$$\sum_{t=1}^{n} e_t^2 = \sum_{t=1}^{n} (A_t - F_t)^2$$

**Step 4:** Divide by $n$ to obtain the mean.
$$MSE = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{t=1}^{n} e_t^2$$

### 3.3 Derivation of RMSE from MSE

RMSE is mathematically derived by applying the square root to the MSE result, restoring the original units of measurement.

$$RMSE = \sqrt{MSE}$$

Substituting the expanded form:
$$RMSE = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n} \sum_{t=1}^{n} e_t^2}$$

### 3.4 Derivation of MAPE

**Step 1:** Express each error as a fraction of the actual value.
$$\frac{e_t}{A_t} = \frac{A_t - F_t}{A_t}$$

**Step 2:** Take the absolute value and multiply by 100 to get a percentage.
$$\left\vert \frac{e_t}{A_t} \right\vert \times 100 \;(\%)$$

**Step 3:** Average these percentage errors over $n$ periods.
$$MAPE = \frac{100}{n} \sum_{t=1}^{n} \left\vert \frac{e_t}{A_t} \right\vert \;(\%)$$

### 3.5 Exhaustive Worked Example (KTU Board Pattern)

**Problem Statement:** A bakery's weekly bread demand and the corresponding forecasts for 5 weeks are given below. Calculate MAD, MSE, RMSE, and MAPE.

| Week ($t$) | Actual Demand ($A_t$) | Forecast ($F_t$) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 100 | 110 |
| 2 | 120 | 115 |
| 3 | 130 | 125 |
| 4 | 110 | 120 |
| 5 | 140 | 130 |

**Step 1: Calculate the error for each period.**

Using $e_t = A_t - F_t$:
- $e_1 = 100 - 110 = -10$
- $e_2 = 120 - 115 = +5$
- $e_3 = 130 - 125 = +5$
- $e_4 = 110 - 120 = -10$
- $e_5 = 140 - 130 = +10$

**Step 2: Calculate the absolute error for each period.**

- $\vert e_1 \vert = 10$
- $\vert e_2 \vert = 5$
- $\vert e_3 \vert = 5$
- $\vert e_4 \vert = 10$
- $\vert e_5 \vert = 10$

**Step 3: Calculate the squared error for each period.**

- $e_1^2 = (-10)^2 = 100$
- $e_2^2 = (5)^2 = 25$
- $e_3^2 = (5)^2 = 25$
- $e_4^2 = (-10)^2 = 100$
- $e_5^2 = (10)^2 = 100$

**Step 4: Calculate the percentage error for each period.**

- $\left\vert \frac{e_1}{A_1} \right\vert \times 100 = \left\vert \frac{-10}{100} \right\vert \times 100 = 10\%$
- $\left\vert \frac{e_2}{A_2} \right\vert \times 100 = \left\vert \frac{5}{120} \right\vert \times 100 \approx 4.17\%$
- $\left\vert \frac{e_3}{A_3} \right\vert \times 100 = \left\vert \frac{5}{130} \right\vert \times 100 \approx 3.85\%$
- $\left\vert \frac{e_4}{A_4} \right\vert \times 100 = \left\vert \frac{-10}{110} \right\vert \times 100 \approx 9.09\%$
- $\left\vert \frac{e_5}{A_5} \right\vert \times 100 = \left\vert \frac{10}{140} \right\vert \times 100 \approx 7.14\%$

**Step 5: Compute MAD.**

$$MAD = \frac{1}{5} \sum_{t=1}^{5} \vert e_t \vert = \frac{1}{5}(10 + 5 + 5 + 10 + 10) = \frac{40}{5} = 8 \text{ units}$$

**Step 6: Compute MSE.**

$$MSE = \frac{1}{5} \sum_{t=1}^{5} e_t^2 = \frac{1}{5}(100 + 25 + 25 + 100 + 100) = \frac{350}{5} = 70 \text{ (units)}^2$$

**Step 7: Compute RMSE.**

$$RMSE = \sqrt{MSE} = \sqrt{70} \approx 8.37 \text{ units}$$

**Step 8: Compute MAPE.**

$$MAPE = \frac{100}{5}(10 + 4.17 + 3.85 + 9.09 + 7.14) = \frac{1}{5}(34.25) \approx 6.85\%$$

**Step 9: Interpretation.**
- On average, the bakery's forecast misses actual demand by **8 units** (MAD).
- The forecast is on average within **6.85%** of actual demand (MAPE) — which is considered highly accurate in production management benchmarks.
- The forecast is slightly **biased toward over-prediction** in early weeks but improves later.

### 3.6 Derivation of Tracking Signal

**Step 1:** Compute cumulative forecast error.
$$CFE = \sum_{t=1}^{n} e_t$$

**Step 2:** Tracking Signal is the ratio of cumulative error to mean absolute deviation.
$$TS = \frac{CFE}{MAD}$$

**Interpretation:** If $\vert TS \vert > 4$, the forecast is considered out of control and must be revised (a widely accepted rule of thumb in APICS/CPIM standards).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Forecast Accuracy Evaluation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Historical Data] --> B[Apply Forecasting Model]
    B --> C[Generate Forecast Values F_t]
    D[Collect Actual Demand A_t] --> E[Compute Error e_t = A_t - F_t]
    C --> E
    E --> F[Error Dataset e_1, e_2, ..., e_n]
    F --> G[Compute Absolute Errors: vert e_t vert]
    F --> H[Compute Squared Errors: e_t squared]
    F --> I[Compute Percentage Errors: vert e_t / A_t vert times 100]
    G --> J[MAD Calculation]
    G --> K[Tracking Signal]
    H --> L[MSE Calculation]
    L --> M[RMSE = sqrt MSE]
    I --> N[MAPE Calculation]
    J --> O[Decision: Choose Best Model]
    K --> O
    M --> O
    N --> O
    O --> P{Is vert Tracking Signal vert > 4?}
    P -- Yes --> Q[Recalibrate Model]
    P -- No --> R[Continue Using Forecast]
```

### 4.2 Comparative Mermaid Diagram: Properties of Each Measure

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[Forecast Accuracy Measures] --> nodeB[MAD]
    nodeA --> nodeC[MSE]
    nodeA --> nodeD[RMSE]
    nodeA --> nodeE[MAPE]
    nodeB --> nodeF[Same units as demand]
    nodeB --> nodeG[Treats all errors equally]
    nodeC --> nodeH[Penalizes large errors]
    nodeC --> nodeI[Squared units]
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeD --> nodeH
    nodeD --> nodeJ[Most widely used metric]
    nodeE --> nodeK[Percentage scale]
    nodeE --> nodeL[Scale-free comparison]
    nodeE --> nodeM[Undefined when A_t = 0]
```

### 4.3 Hierarchical Flowchart: Decision Logic for Forecast Monitoring

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Start: New Period Actual Data Arrives] --> stepB[Compute New Error e_t]
    stepB --> stepC[Update Running Sum CFE]
    stepC --> stepD[Update MAD and Tracking Signal]
    stepD --> stepE{Absolute Tracking Signal > 4?}
    stepE -- Yes --> stepF[Flag: Forecast Out of Control]
    stepE -- No --> stepG[Flag: Forecast Acceptable]
    stepF --> stepH[Re-examine Model Parameters]
    stepH --> stepI[Switch to Alternative Model]
    stepG --> stepJ[Continue with Current Forecast]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define Mean Absolute Deviation (MAD) and Mean Squared Error (MSE) as measures of forecast accuracy. Mention one advantage of MSE over MAD.**

**Model Answer:**
MAD is the average of the absolute forecast errors over $n$ periods, defined as $MAD = \frac{1}{n}\sum \vert e_t \vert$. MSE is the average of squared errors, $MSE = \frac{1}{n}\sum e_t^2$.

**Advantage of MSE over MAD:** MSE penalizes large errors more heavily due to squaring, making it more sensitive to outliers. This is particularly useful when large forecasting errors are especially undesirable (e.g., safety stock planning).

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**What is the significance of Tracking Signal in forecast monitoring? State the standard control limit.**

**Model Answer:**
The Tracking Signal (TS) is a dimensionless metric that detects systematic bias in a forecasting model. It is computed as $TS = \frac{CFE}{MAD}$. A positive TS indicates consistent under-forecasting, while a negative TS indicates consistent over-forecasting. The standard control limit is $\vert TS \vert \leq 4$. If exceeded, the forecast model is considered out of control and requires recalibration.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - June 2024]*

**(a)** Explain any four measures of forecast accuracy with suitable formulas. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** The actual sales and forecasted sales of a product for 6 months are given below. Compute MAD, MSE, RMSE, and MAPE. Comment on the accuracy of the forecast. *(7 marks)*

| Month | Actual ($A_t$) | Forecast ($F_t$) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 200 | 220 |
| 2 | 230 | 210 |
| 3 | 180 | 200 |
| 4 | 250 | 240 |
| 5 | 270 | 260 |
| 6 | 240 | 250 |

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1: Calculate errors and their absolute, squared, and percentage values.**

- Month 1: $e_1 = 200 - 220 = -20$, $\vert e_1 \vert = 20$, $e_1^2 = 400$, $\left\vert \frac{e_1}{A_1} \right\vert \times 100 = 10.00\%$
- Month 2: $e_2 = 230 - 210 = +20$, $\vert e_2 \vert = 20$, $e_2^2 = 400$, $\left\vert \frac{e_2}{A_2} \right\vert \times 100 = 8.70\%$
- Month 3: $e_3 = 180 - 200 = -20$, $\vert e_3 \vert = 20$, $e_3^2 = 400$, $\left\vert \frac{e_3}{A_3} \right\vert \times 100 = 11.11\%$
- Month 4: $e_4 = 250 - 240 = +10$, $\vert e_4 \vert = 10$, $e_4^2 = 100$, $\left\vert \frac{e_4}{A_4} \right\vert \times 100 = 4.00\%$
- Month 5: $e_5 = 270 - 260 = +10$, $\vert e_5 \vert = 10$, $e_5^2 = 100$, $\left\vert \frac{e_5}{A_5} \right\vert \times 100 = 3.70\%$
- Month 6: $e_6 = 240 - 250 = -10$, $\vert e_6 \vert = 10$, $e_6^2 = 100$, $\left\vert \frac{e_6}{A_6} \right\vert \times 100 = 4.17\%$

**Step 2: Compute MAD.**

$$MAD = \frac{1}{6}(20 + 20 + 20 + 10 + 10 + 10) = \frac{90}{6} = 15 \text{ units}$$

**Step 3: Compute MSE.**

$$MSE = \frac{1}{6}(400 + 400 + 400 + 100 + 100 + 100) = \frac{1500}{6} = 250 \text{ (units)}^2$$

**Step 4: Compute RMSE.**

$$RMSE = \sqrt{250} \approx 15.81 \text{ units}$$

**Step 5: Compute MAPE.**

$$MAPE = \frac{1}{6}(10.00 + 8.70 + 11.11 + 4.00 + 3.70 + 4.17) = \frac{41.68}{6} \approx 6.95\%$$

**Step 6: Comment on Accuracy.**
A MAPE of $\approx 6.95\%$ is generally considered good forecast accuracy in production management. The forecast is reasonably reliable, though there is room for improvement, particularly in the first three months.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - May 2023]*

**(a)** Define the following: (i) Forecast Error, (ii) Bias (Mean Error), (iii) Tracking Signal, (iv) Theil's U Statistic. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A company uses an exponential smoothing forecast. The actual and forecasted values for 7 weeks are:

| Week | Actual ($A_t$) | Forecast ($F_t$) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 50 | 45 |
| 2 | 52 | 49 |
| 3 | 48 | 51 |
| 4 | 55 | 50 |
| 5 | 53 | 54 |
| 6 | 58 | 55 |
| 7 | 60 | 58 |

Calculate MAD, MSE, Tracking Signal, and comment on whether the forecast is under control. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1: Calculate errors.**

- $e_1 = 50 - 45 = 5$
- $e_2 = 52 - 49 = 3$
- $e_3 = 48 - 51 = -3$
- $e_4 = 55 - 50 = 5$
- $e_5 = 53 - 54 = -1$
- $e_6 = 58 - 55 = 3$
- $e_7 = 60 - 58 = 2$

**Step 2: Compute absolute and squared errors.**

- $\vert e_t \vert$: 5, 3, 3, 5, 1, 3, 2
- $e_t^2$: 25, 9, 9, 25, 1, 9, 4

**Step 3: Compute MAD.**

$$MAD = \frac{1}{7}(5 + 3 + 3 + 5 + 1 + 3 + 2) = \frac{22}{7} \approx 3.14 \text{ units}$$

**Step 4: Compute MSE.**

$$MSE = \frac{1}{7}(25 + 9 + 9 + 25 + 1 + 9 + 4) = \frac{82}{7} \approx 11.71 \text{ (units)}^2$$

**Step 5: Compute CFE (Cumulative Forecast Error).**

$$CFE = 5 + 3 - 3 + 5 - 1 + 3 + 2 = 14$$

**Step 6: Compute Tracking Signal.**

$$TS = \frac{CFE}{MAD} = \frac{14}{3.14} \approx 4.46$$

**Step 7: Comment.**
Since $\vert TS \vert = 4.46 > 4$, the forecast is **out of control**. There is a systematic positive bias — the model consistently under-forecasts actual demand. The exponential smoothing model needs parameter adjustment (e.g., a higher smoothing constant $\alpha$) or replacement.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting absolute values in MAD:** Many students write $MAD = \frac{1}{n}\sum e_t$ (without $\vert \cdot \vert$), which is **wrong** — that would be the Mean Error, not MAD. **Loss of 1-2 marks guaranteed.**
> 2. **Units Confusion in MAPE:** MAPE is a *percentage* and must be reported with the % sign. Raw decimal answers will be marked down.
> 3. **Tracking Sign Convention:** A positive CFE means the model **under-forecasts** (actual > forecast). A negative CFE means **over-forecasting**. Do not reverse this.
> 4. **Misreporting RMSE as a substitute for MSE:** RMSE and MSE are *different* metrics with different units. Both are often required in the same problem.
> 5. **Forgetting to interpret the result:** KTU examiners award marks for a concluding comment on whether the model is acceptable.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- **Forecast Error** = $A_t - F_t$ (signed quantity).
- **MAD** uses **absolute values** — does not penalize large errors more.
- **MSE** uses **squared values** — penalizes large errors heavily.
- **RMSE** = $\sqrt{MSE}$ — back to original units of demand.
- **MAPE** is a **percentage** — best for cross-product comparison; undefined when $A_t = 0$.
- **Mean Error (Bias)** is signed — close to zero means the model is unbiased.
- **CFE** accumulates errors algebraically — used for Tracking Signal.
- **Tracking Signal** = $CFE / MAD$; control limit is $\pm 4$.
- **Theil's U** compares forecast against a naive (no-change) forecast; $U < 1$ is good.
- Always **report units** (units, kg, %, etc.) in your final answer.
- Always include a **commentary sentence** at the end of numerical problems.
- The choice of metric depends on context: use **MAD** for simplicity, **MSE/RMSE** for outlier sensitivity, **MAPE** for cross-scale comparison.
- **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** Numerical problems on MAD, MSE, RMSE, and MAPE appear almost every semester — practice table-based computations.
- **Tracking Signal** problems are favorite 7-mark sub-parts in Part B.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Strategies for Meeting Uneven Demand
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET601 - OPERATIONS RESEARCH AND PRODUCTION MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Basic Strategies for Meeting Uneven Demand

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In aggregate production planning, **uneven demand** refers to a demand pattern in which the quantity of goods required by customers fluctuates significantly across successive planning periods (months, quarters), while the firm must decide *how much to produce*, *how many workers to employ*, *what inventory to hold*, and *how much backlog to permit* in order to minimize total cost while meeting demand.

A **Basic Strategy for Meeting Uneven Demand** is a managerial policy that prescribes, for each period in the planning horizon, the matching between the fluctuating customer demand and the firm's controllable production resources (workforce, inventory, overtime, subcontracting, backorders).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> The board expects students to be able to (i) identify the *pure* strategies — **Chase**, **Level**, and **Mixed**, and (ii) formulate the total cost function combining regular payroll, hiring/firing, overtime, holding, backlog, and subcontracting.

### 1.2 The Three Classical Pure Strategies

The textbooks (and KTU's valuation key) classify any aggregate plan into one of three pure strategies:

| # | Pure Strategy | Production Logic | Cost Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Chase Demand Strategy** | Production in each period equals that period's demand | Maximum hiring/firing; minimum inventory |
| 2 | **Level Production Strategy** | Production rate is held constant across all periods | Stable workforce; maximum inventory fluctuation |
| 3 | **Mixed / Hybrid Strategy** | A combination of chase and level | Balanced trade-off between workforce stability and inventory cost |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tea Stall" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Real-World Analogy):**
> Imagine a tea-stall owner near a college. On weekdays he sells 200 cups, on weekends 500 cups. He can:
>
> - **Chase demand**: Hire one *extra* helper only on weekends and send them home on Monday. Workforce changes daily → no wastage, but constant phone calls to staff.
> - **Level production**: Hire 3 helpers permanently and brew a fixed 350 cups daily. Some days tea is leftover (inventory cost), some days he runs short (lost sales/backlog).
> - **Mixed strategy**: Keep 2 permanent helpers (level base) + call 1 part-time helper on weekends (chase the peak). This is what almost every real factory actually does.
>
> The *cost* the owner wishes to minimize is the sum of: helper wages, hiring/firing friction, leftover tea cost, shortage penalty, and emergency overtime charges.

### 1.4 Auxiliary Levers Used Inside Every Strategy

To meet uneven demand, planners combine six *adjustment levers*:

1. **Workforce size** — hiring / laying off production workers.
2. **Production rate per worker** — overtime and undertime (idle time).
3. **Inventory** — produce ahead and stockpile.
4. **Backlog / Backorders** — allow customers to wait.
5. **Subcontracting** — outsource part of the production.
6. **Part-time / Temporary workers** — flexible labour buffer.

> [!TIP]
> A *pure* chase strategy uses levers (1) and (2) only. A *pure* level strategy uses lever (3) only. Real firms mix levers (1) through (6) to design a cost-minimising plan.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic of Each Strategy

#### A. Chase Demand Strategy
- Production $P_t$ in period $t$ is set **exactly equal** to forecast demand $D_t$.
- Workforce $W_t$ fluctuates period by period.
- Hiring $\Delta_t^+$ and firing $\Delta_t^-$ are computed from workforce changes.
- Inventory and backlog are (almost) zero.
- **Cost drivers**: hiring cost, firing cost, regular payroll.

#### B. Level Production Strategy
- A constant production rate $\bar{P}$ is chosen (commonly the average demand or a value that zeroes out ending inventory at the horizon's end).
- Workforce remains constant.
- The difference between constant production and varying demand is absorbed by inventory (positive difference) or backlog (negative difference).
- **Cost drivers**: regular payroll, inventory holding, backlog penalty.

#### C. Mixed Strategy
- Production is partly level (regular workforce) and partly variable (overtime / subcontracting / part-time).
- Allows the planner to balance two opposing cost gradients: workforce change cost vs. inventory cost.

### 2.2 Decision Variables (Standard KTU Notation)

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $t$ | Period index (1, 2, …, N) | months |
| $D_t$ | Forecast demand in period $t$ | units |
| $P_t$ | Production in period $t$ | units |
| $W_t$ | Number of workers in period $t$ | workers |
| $r$ | Production rate per worker per period | units / worker / period |
| $I_t$ | Ending inventory at end of period $t$ | units |
| $B_t$ | Ending backlog at end of period $t$ | units |
| $H_t$ | Workers hired at start of period $t$ | workers |
| $F_t$ | Workers fired at start of period $t$ | workers |
| $O_t$ | Overtime production in period $t$ | units |
| $U_t$ | Undertime (idle units) in period $t$ | units |
| $S_t$ | Units subcontracted in period $t$ | units |

### 2.3 Core KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorise these **seven master formulas** — they appear in almost every KTU numerical on aggregate planning.

$$
\text{Workers required:} \quad W_t \;=\; \frac{D_t}{r}
$$

$$
\text{Hiring:} \quad H_t \;=\; \max\!\bigl(0,\; W_t - W_{t-1}\bigr) \qquad \qquad \text{Firing:} \quad F_t \;=\; \max\!\bigl(0,\; W_{t-1} - W_t\bigr)
$$

$$
\text{Inventory balance:} \quad I_t \;=\; I_{t-1} + P_t - D_t
$$

$$
\text{Overtime:} \quad O_t \;=\; \max\!\bigl(0,\; P_t - rW_t\bigr) \qquad \qquad \text{Undertime:} \quad U_t \;=\; \max\!\bigl(0,\; rW_t - P_t\bigr)
$$

$$
\text{Total cost} \;=\; \underbrace{\sum_t c_r P_t}_{\text{Regular}} + \underbrace{\sum_t c_h H_t}_{\text{Hiring}} + \underbrace{\sum_t c_f F_t}_{\text{Firing}} + \underbrace{\sum_t c_o O_t}_{\text{Overtime}} + \underbrace{\sum_t c_{inv} I_t}_{\text{Holding}} + \underbrace{\sum_t c_b B_t}_{\text{Backlog}} + \underbrace{\sum_t c_s S_t}_{\text{Subcontract}}
$$

where the **seven cost coefficients** are:
- $c_r$ = regular production cost per unit (Rs/unit)
- $c_h$ = hiring cost per worker (Rs/worker)
- $c_f$ = firing / layoff cost per worker (Rs/worker)
- $c_o$ = overtime premium per unit (Rs/unit)
- $c_{inv}$ = inventory holding cost per unit per period (Rs/unit/period)
- $c_b$ = backlog / backorder penalty per unit per period (Rs/unit/period)
- $c_s$ = subcontracting cost per unit (Rs/unit)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do not confuse **backlog cost** $c_b$ (penalty for delayed delivery) with **hiring cost** $c_h$. Many students interchange the two letters and lose marks.

### 2.4 Analytical Comparison of the Three Strategies

| Performance Metric | Chase Demand | Level Production | Mixed |
|---|---|---|---|
| Inventory level | $\approx 0$ | High fluctuation | Moderate |
| Workforce stability | Very low (changes every period) | Very high (constant) | Moderate |
| Hiring / Firing cost | **Maximum** | $\approx 0$ | Moderate |
| Inventory holding cost | $\approx 0$ | **Maximum** | Moderate |
| Customer service (backlog) | Excellent | Can suffer shortages | Acceptable |
| Suitability | Skilled, easily hired labour; low hiring cost | Stable workforce; high holding cost | Most real-world factories |

### 2.5 Engineering / Industry Utility

This exact framework is used in:
- **Automotive assembly lines** (Toyota Production System uses a mixed strategy with a level base + overtime buffer).
- **Apparel & FMCG** (level base production + chase via subcontracting for seasonal collections).
- **Power generation planning** (level base load + peak shaving via gas turbines — *literally* the same mathematics).
- **Hospital staffing** (level core doctors + chase using temporary locums).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Production–Inventory Balance Equation

The most fundamental identity in aggregate planning comes from *material balance*: what is in stock at the end of a period equals what was already there plus what was made minus what was demanded.

$$
\text{Starting inventory of period } t \;+\; \text{Production during period } t \;-\; \text{Demand during period } t \;=\; \text{Ending inventory of period } t
$$

Writing this in symbolic form:

$$
I_{t-1} + P_t - D_t \;=\; I_t
$$

Rearranging to isolate the unknown $P_t$:

$$
P_t \;=\; D_t + I_t - I_{t-1}
$$

This tells the planner: **to reach a desired ending inventory $I_t$, produce enough to cover this period's demand plus the change in stock level.**

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Workers Required from Production Target

If each worker produces $r$ units in a regular period, the workforce needed to deliver production $P_t$ is:

$$
W_t \;=\; \frac{P_t}{r}
$$

This formula is *only* for regular-time production. If overtime $O_t$ is allowed, regular production becomes $rW_t$ and overtime absorbs the rest.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hiring and Firing Decomposition

The change in workforce $\Delta W_t = W_t - W_{t-1}$ can be **positive** (we need more workers) or **negative** (we have a surplus).

The two non-negative components are extracted using the $\max(\cdot, 0)$ operator:

$$
H_t \;=\; \max\!\bigl(0,\; W_t - W_{t-1}\bigr)
$$

$$
F_t \;=\; \max\!\bigl(0,\; W_{t-1} - W_t\bigr)
$$

This decomposition is essential because hiring and firing have **different unit costs** and must be billed separately.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two separate variables?** Because in real labour law, hiring cost (advertising, training, induction) is rarely equal to firing cost (severance, notice pay, replacement recruitment). The model therefore keeps them distinct.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Pure Chase-Demand Total Cost

In a pure chase strategy: $P_t = D_t$, so $W_t = D_t / r$, $I_t \approx 0$, $B_t \approx 0$, $O_t = 0$.

The total cost reduces to:

$$
TC_{\text{chase}} \;=\; \sum_{t=1}^{N} c_r D_t \;+\; \sum_{t=1}^{N} c_h H_t \;+\; \sum_{t=1}^{N} c_f F_t
$$

Substituting the workforce formula:

$$
TC_{\text{chase}} \;=\; c_r\!\sum_{t=1}^{N} D_t \;+\; c_h \sum_{t=1}^{N}\!\max\!\Bigl(0,\; \tfrac{D_t}{r} - \tfrac{D_{t-1}}{r}\Bigr) \;+\; c_f \sum_{t=1}^{N}\!\max\!\Bigl(0,\; \tfrac{D_{t-1}}{r} - \tfrac{D_t}{r}\Bigr)
$$

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Pure Level-Production Total Cost

In a pure level strategy the production rate is **constant**. A common choice is the *average demand*:

$$
\bar{P} \;=\; \frac{1}{N}\sum_{t=1}^{N} D_t
$$

Workforce is constant: $W = \bar{P}/r$.

Production cost, hiring cost, firing cost are all fixed. Only inventory and backlog fluctuate. The cost simplifies to:

$$
TC_{\text{level}} \;=\; N \cdot c_r \bar{P} \;+\; c_h W_{\text{initial hires}} \;+\; c_{inv}\sum_{t=1}^{N} I_t \;+\; c_b \sum_{t=1}^{N} B_t
$$

The cumulative inventory is built using the balance equation repeatedly:

$$
I_1 = I_0 + \bar{P} - D_1
$$

$$
I_2 = I_1 + \bar{P} - D_2 = I_0 + 2\bar{P} - (D_1 + D_2)
$$

$$
\boxed{\;I_t \;=\; I_0 + t\bar{P} - \sum_{k=1}^{t} D_k\;}
$$

If $I_0 = 0$ and $I_N = 0$ (no leftover stock at horizon end), then:

$$
\sum_{k=1}^{N} D_k \;=\; N\bar{P} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \bar{P} \;=\; \frac{1}{N}\sum_{t=1}^{N} D_t
$$

This confirms the average-demand rule is internally consistent only when starting and ending inventories are zero.

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style — 14 mark flavour)

> **Problem Statement:**
> A firm faces the following forecast for the next 4 months. Regular production rate per worker per month is $r = 100$ units. Regular cost Rs **20/unit**, hiring Rs **300/worker**, firing Rs **500/worker**, holding Rs **2/unit/month**, backlog Rs **5/unit/month**. Initial workforce = 8 workers, initial inventory = 0. Compare the **pure chase** and **pure level** strategies.

| Month $t$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Demand $D_t$ | 800 | 1200 | 1000 | 600 |

#### Step 1 — Compute the chase plan

$$
W_t = D_t / 100 \quad\Rightarrow\quad W_1 = 8,\; W_2 = 12,\; W_3 = 10,\; W_4 = 6
$$

$$
H_1 = \max(0, 8-8)=0,\; H_2=\max(0,12-8)=4,\; H_3=\max(0,10-12)=0,\; H_4=\max(0,6-10)=0
$$

$$
F_1=\max(0,8-8)=0,\; F_2=\max(0,8-12)=0,\; F_3=\max(0,12-10)=2,\; F_4=\max(0,10-6)=4
$$

**Chase total cost:**

$$
\text{Regular} = 20 \times (800+1200+1000+600) = 20 \times 3600 = \text{Rs } 72{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Hiring} = 300 \times (0+4+0+0) = \text{Rs } 1{,}200
$$

$$
\text{Firing} = 500 \times (0+0+2+4) = \text{Rs } 3{,}000
$$

$$
\boxed{\;TC_{\text{chase}} \;=\; 72{,}000 + 1{,}200 + 3{,}000 \;=\; \text{Rs } 76{,}200\;}
$$

#### Step 2 — Compute the level plan

Average production per month:

$$
\bar{P} \;=\; \frac{800+1200+1000+600}{4} \;=\; \frac{3600}{4} \;=\; 900 \text{ units / month}
$$

Required workforce (constant):

$$
W = 900/100 = 9 \text{ workers}
$$

Hiring (one-time at $t=1$): $H_1 = 9 - 8 = 1$ worker. No firings afterwards.

Period-by-period inventory:

$$
I_0 = 0,\quad I_1 = 0 + 900 - 800 = 100
$$

$$
I_2 = 100 + 900 - 1200 = -200 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; B_2 = 200 \text{ (backlog)}
$$

$$
I_3 = -200 + 900 - 1000 = -300 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; B_3 = 300
$$

$$
I_4 = -300 + 900 - 600 = 0
$$

**Level total cost:**

$$
\text{Regular} = 20 \times 4 \times 900 = \text{Rs } 72{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Hiring} = 300 \times 1 = \text{Rs } 300
$$

$$
\text{Holding} = 2 \times 100 = \text{Rs } 200 \quad (\text{only month 1 has positive inventory})
$$

$$
\text{Backlog} = 5 \times (200 + 300) = \text{Rs } 2{,}500
$$

$$
\boxed{\;TC_{\text{level}} \;=\; 72{,}000 + 300 + 200 + 2{,}500 \;=\; \text{Rs } 75{,}000\;}
$$

#### Step 3 — Comparison

$$
TC_{\text{level}} \;=\; \text{Rs } 75{,}000 \quad < \quad TC_{\text{chase}} \;=\; \text{Rs } 76{,}200
$$

**Decision:** Level strategy is cheaper by **Rs 1,200** in this dataset — but in many real datasets chase wins because hiring/firing is cheaper than holding/backlog.

### 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Decision Rule of Thumb

| If hiring cost $c_h$ + firing cost $c_f$ is… | …and holding + backlog cost is… | Then the cheaper pure strategy is… |
|---|---|---|
| **High** (e.g. skilled labour, union rules) | Low | **Level Production** |
| **Low** (e.g. unskilled, gig workers) | High | **Chase Demand** |
| Comparable | Comparable | **Mixed Strategy** (use linear programming / transportation method) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — The Aggregate Planning Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Forecast Demand D_t for t = 1 to N] --> B{Choose Planning Strategy}
    B -->|Pure| C1[Chase Demand Strategy]
    B -->|Pure| C2[Level Production Strategy]
    B -->|Hybrid| C3[Mixed Strategy]
    C1 --> D1[Set P_t = D_t]
    C2 --> D2[Set P_t = Average D_t]
    C3 --> D3[Set P_t = Level Base + Variable Top-up]
    D1 --> E[Compute Workforce W_t = P_t / r]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Compute Hiring H_t and Firing F_t]
    F --> G[Apply Inventory Balance I_t = I_t-1 + P_t - D_t]
    G --> H[Compute Overtime O_t and Undertime U_t]
    H --> I[Aggregate Total Cost using Master Formula]
    I --> J{Is TC Minimum}
    J -->|Yes| K[Accept Strategy]
    J -->|No| L[Adjust Strategy and Recompute]
    L --> B
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Cost Component Inter-relationships

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Production P_t] --> R[Regular Cost c_r x P_t]
    P --> OT[Overtime c_o x O_t]
    P --> SC[Subcontract c_s x S_t]
    W[Workforce W_t] --> H[Hiring Cost c_h x H_t]
    W --> F[Firing Cost c_f x F_t]
    INV[Inventory I_t] --> HC[Holding Cost c_inv x I_t]
    BL[Backlog B_t] --> BC[Backlog Cost c_b x B_t]
    R --> TC[Total Cost TC]
    OT --> TC
    SC --> TC
    H --> TC
    F --> TC
    HC --> TC
    BC --> TC
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Decision Logic for Choosing a Pure Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Compare Hiring+Firing vs Holding+Backlog] --> Q1{Is c_h + c_f much greater than c_inv + c_b}
    Q1 -->|Yes| R1[Choose Level Production]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is c_inv + c_b much greater than c_h + c_f}
    Q2 -->|Yes| R2[Choose Chase Demand]
    Q2 -->|No| R3[Choose Mixed Strategy]
    R1 --> END[Final Aggregate Plan]
    R2 --> END
    R3 --> END
```

### 4.4 Schematic — Inventory Build-up and Draw-down Across Periods

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Period 1 P=900 D=800] --> I1[I1 = +100]
    I1 --> P2[Period 2 P=900 D=1200]
    P2 --> I2[I2 = -200 = Backlog]
    I2 --> P3[Period 3 P=900 D=1000]
    P3 --> I3[I3 = -300 = Backlog]
    I3 --> P4[Period 4 P=900 D=600]
    P4 --> I4[I4 = 0]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The schematic shows the level plan's inventory behaviour from the worked numerical example. Notice how inventory goes **positive in early periods** (holding cost) and **negative later** (backlog cost). The strategy trades workforce stability for inventory / backlog volatility.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Explain any **three pure strategies** for meeting uneven demand in aggregate production planning.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three pure strategies are:

1. **Chase Demand Strategy:** Production in each period exactly equals the forecast demand for that period. Workforce is varied (hiring and firing) so that inventory and backlog remain close to zero. Best when hiring/firing cost is low compared to holding/backlog cost.

2. **Level Production Strategy:** Production rate is held constant across all periods (usually equal to average demand). Workforce remains stable. The gap between constant production and varying demand is absorbed by building inventory (when production > demand) or by allowing backlog (when production < demand). Best when holding and backlog costs are low.

3. **Mixed Strategy:** A combination of chase and level where production has a stable base plus variable components through overtime, subcontracting, part-time workers, or hiring/firing. Balances the two cost gradients.

*(1 mark each — full definitions earn 3 marks.)*

---

#### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Distinguish between **overtime** and **subcontracting** as adjustment levers for meeting uneven demand.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Overtime | Subcontracting |
|---|---|---|
| Source of extra capacity | Existing workforce working extra hours | External supplier producing on firm's behalf |
| Cost per unit | $c_o$ (premium above regular wage) | $c_s$ (vendor's quoted price) |
| Quality control | High — same workers, same plant | Moderate — depends on vendor's capability |
| Lead time | Same day | Days to weeks |
| Use case | Short, predictable peaks | Larger or longer-duration surges |

*(Any 3 valid differentiating points earn full marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern for Module 4, the 14-mark question carries an **internal choice**. You must attempt *either* Question A *or* Question B below.

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

A manufacturing firm has forecasted the following demand for the next 6 months:

| Month $t$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Demand $D_t$ | 500 | 700 | 900 | 800 | 600 | 400 |

Production rate per worker per month = **100 units**. Initial workforce = **6 workers**, initial inventory = **0**. Backlog is **not allowed**.

Cost data:
- Regular production: Rs **25 / unit**
- Hiring: Rs **400 / worker**
- Firing: Rs **600 / worker**
- Holding: Rs **3 / unit / month**
- Backlog: Rs **8 / unit / month**
- Overtime: Rs **35 / unit**
- Subcontract: Rs **40 / unit**

**(a)** [7 marks] Formulate and compute the total cost of the **pure chase demand strategy**.

**(b)** [7 marks] Formulate and compute the total cost of the **pure level production strategy**, and identify which strategy is cheaper.

---

##### Model Solution — Part (a) [Chase Strategy, 7 marks]

**Step 1: Workers required each period.**

$$
W_t = \frac{D_t}{100} \quad\Rightarrow\quad W_1 = 5,\; W_2 = 7,\; W_3 = 9,\; W_4 = 8,\; W_5 = 6,\; W_6 = 4
$$

(Note: $W_1 = 5$ even though initial workforce is 6, so we fire 1 worker in month 1.)

**Step 2: Hiring and Firing.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_1 &= \max(0, 5 - 6) = 0 \\
F_1 &= \max(0, 6 - 5) = 1 \\
H_2 &= \max(0, 7 - 5) = 2 \\
F_2 &= 0 \\
H_3 &= \max(0, 9 - 7) = 2 \\
F_3 &= 0 \\
H_4 &= \max(0, 8 - 9) = 0 \\
F_4 &= \max(0, 9 - 8) = 1 \\
H_5 &= 0 \\
F_5 &= \max(0, 8 - 6) = 2 \\
H_6 &= 0 \\
F_6 &= \max(0, 6 - 4) = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Cost aggregation.**

Regular production cost:

$$
25 \times (500+700+900+800+600+400) = 25 \times 3900 = \text{Rs } 97{,}500
$$

Hiring cost:

$$
400 \times (0+2+2+0+0+0) = 400 \times 4 = \text{Rs } 1{,}600
$$

Firing cost:

$$
600 \times (1+0+0+1+2+2) = 600 \times 6 = \text{Rs } 3{,}600
$$

Inventory and backlog are zero (chase = exact match).

$$
\boxed{\;TC_{\text{chase}} = 97{,}500 + 1{,}600 + 3{,}600 = \text{Rs } 1{,}02{,}700\;}
$$

##### Model Solution — Part (b) [Level Strategy, 7 marks]

**Step 1: Average production rate.**

$$
\bar{P} = \frac{500+700+900+800+600+400}{6} = \frac{3900}{6} = 650 \text{ units / month}
$$

**Step 2: Constant workforce.**

$$
W = 650/100 = 6.5 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; W = 7 \text{ workers (rounded up so backlog is never needed)}
$$

(If we rounded to 6 workers, regular capacity = 600 < demand in some months → backlog. Choosing 7 workers ensures $rW = 700 \geq D_t$ for all $t$.)

**Step 3: Hiring needed at start.**

Initial workforce = 6, target = 7 → $H_1 = 1$. No firing thereafter.

**Step 4: Period-by-period inventory.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_0 &= 0 \\
I_1 &= 0 + 700 - 500 = +200 \\
I_2 &= 200 + 700 - 700 = +200 \\
I_3 &= 200 + 700 - 900 = \;\;\;0 \\
I_4 &= 0 + 700 - 800 = -100 \quad (\text{backlog}) \\
I_5 &= -100 + 700 - 600 = \;\;\;0 \\
I_6 &= 0 + 700 - 400 = +300 \quad (\text{leftover at horizon end})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Cost aggregation.**

Regular cost:

$$
25 \times 6 \times 700 = \text{Rs } 1{,}05{,}000
$$

Hiring cost:

$$
400 \times 1 = \text{Rs } 400
$$

Holding cost (positive inventory in months 1, 2, 6):

$$
3 \times (200 + 200 + 300) = 3 \times 700 = \text{Rs } 2{,}100
$$

Backlog cost (month 4):

$$
8 \times 100 = \text{Rs } 800
$$

$$
\boxed{\;TC_{\text{level}} = 1{,}05{,}000 + 400 + 2{,}100 + 800 = \text{Rs } 1{,}08{,}300\;}
$$

**Decision:** $TC_{\text{chase}} = 1{,}02{,}700 < TC_{\text{level}} = 1{,}08{,}300$.

**Chase strategy is cheaper by Rs 5,600** in this dataset, because hiring/firing costs (Rs 5,200) are smaller than holding + backlog cost (Rs 2,900) only when *combined* with the regular-production differential — but the level plan's overproduction (700 vs 650 average) inflates the regular cost by Rs 7,500, which tips the balance.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** [7 marks] *With the help of suitable expressions, explain how **overtime** and **undertime** production can be used as adjustment levers to meet uneven demand.*

**(b)** [7 marks] *For a firm facing uneven demand, discuss any **four cost components** that appear in the total cost function of an aggregate production plan, giving their formulae.*

---

##### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

Let regular capacity = $rW_t$, where $r$ is the production rate per worker and $W_t$ is the workforce size in period $t$. Actual production in period $t$ is $P_t$.

**Overtime production** occurs when actual production exceeds regular capacity:

$$
O_t \;=\; \max\!\bigl(0,\; P_t - rW_t\bigr)
$$

It is billed at the overtime premium $c_o$ per unit, contributing $c_o O_t$ to total cost. Overtime allows the firm to *chase* demand peaks without hiring additional permanent workers.

**Undertime (idle production)** occurs when regular capacity exceeds actual production:

$$
U_t \;=\; \max\!\bigl(0,\; rW_t - P_t\bigr)
$$

Undertime represents unused capacity — a hidden cost because workers are still paid their regular wage but produce no output. It often appears in pure level strategies when $rW > D_t$ in low-demand months.

**Combined formulation:** The total wage bill can be written as:

$$
\text{Wage}_t \;=\; c_r (P_t - O_t) + c_o O_t \;=\; c_r rW_t + (c_o - c_r) O_t
$$

where the first term is the *fixed* regular payroll (paid regardless of overtime use) and the second term is the *variable* overtime premium. Thus overtime acts as a *cheaper substitute* for hiring when the surge is small or temporary.

##### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

The four principal cost components are:

1. **Regular Production Cost:** $c_r P_t$ — paid for every unit produced in regular time.

2. **Hiring Cost:** $c_h H_t = c_h \max(0, W_t - W_{t-1})$ — incurred whenever the workforce grows (recruitment, training, induction).

3. **Firing / Layoff Cost:** $c_f F_t = c_f \max(0, W_{t-1} - W_t)$ — severance, notice, and morale-related indirect costs when workforce shrinks.

4. **Inventory Holding Cost:** $c_{inv} I_t$ — warehousing, insurance, capital tied up, and obsolescence cost on every unit left in stock at the end of period $t$.

(Optional fifth/sixth for breadth: Backlog cost $c_b B_t$, Subcontracting cost $c_s S_t$.)

*(1.5 marks per correctly stated cost + formula + interpretation. 7 marks = 4 components × 1.75 each, round generously.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students typically lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting to set $I_0$ and $I_N$ explicitly.** Examiners deduct 1 mark when initial/final inventory assumptions are not stated.
> 2. **Confusing backlog $B_t$ with negative inventory $I_t$.** They are *the same numerically* but $B_t$ must use the backlog cost rate $c_b$, not the holding rate $c_{inv}$. Many students apply $c_{inv}$ to $|I_t|$ and lose 2 marks.
> 3. **Not separating hiring from firing.** A single variable $W_t - W_{t-1}$ with one cost coefficient is **wrong**. The two costs have separate coefficients ($c_h \neq c_f$ in general) and must be billed separately.
> 4. **Workforce rounding errors.** If $D_t / r$ gives a non-integer (e.g. 7.4 workers), the correct interpretation is "hire/fire to the nearest whole worker" — but students often drop the fractional part silently. KTU examiners expect you to **state the rounding policy**.
> 5. **Omitting the cost of leftover inventory at the horizon end** in level strategy. $I_N$ contributes to holding cost if $I_N > 0$ and is a *lost investment* either way.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Pure strategies** = Chase, Level, Mixed. They differ in which *adjustment lever* dominates.
- **Chase**: $P_t = D_t$, high workforce-change cost, low inventory.
- **Level**: $P_t = \bar{P}$, high inventory/backlog cost, stable workforce.
- **Mixed**: combine level base + chase element via overtime/subcontracting/part-time.
- **Six adjustment levers**: workforce, overtime, inventory, backlog, subcontracting, part-time workers.
- **Master formulas to memorise**:
  - $W_t = D_t / r$
  - $H_t = \max(0, W_t - W_{t-1})$, $F_t = \max(0, W_{t-1} - W_t)$
  - $I_t = I_{t-1} + P_t - D_t$
  - $O_t = \max(0, P_t - rW_t)$, $U_t = \max(0, rW_t - P_t)$
- **Total cost = Regular + Hiring + Firing + Overtime + Holding + Backlog + Subcontract.**
- **Decision rule of thumb**: high hiring+firing cost → level; high holding+backlog cost → chase.
- **Average production** $\bar{P} = \frac{1}{N}\sum D_t$ is the natural level-strategy production rate when $I_0 = I_N = 0$.
- **Always state assumptions**: initial workforce, initial inventory, no-backlog or backlog allowed, integer workforce.
- **Always separate** hiring cost ($c_h$) from firing cost ($c_f$); never merge them.
- **Always distinguish** positive $I_t$ (holding cost $c_{inv}$) from negative $I_t$ (backlog cost $c_b$).
- **Inventory is free of charge to hold — but holding cost is NOT free.** This is the single most-tested misconception in KTU valuation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Techniques for Aggregate Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET601 - OPERATIONS RESEARCH AND PRODUCTION MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Techniques for Aggregate Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Aggregate Planning?

**Aggregate Planning (AP)** is a medium-range (3 to 18 months) operational planning activity that establishes the overall levels of **production, inventory, workforce, and subcontracting** required to meet forecasted demand at **minimum total cost**, while satisfying company policies and capacity constraints.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Heizer & Render / Krajewski):**
> *“An aggregate plan is a plan that establishes general levels of employment, output, inventory, and subcontracting over a planning horizon of 3 to 18 months.”*

The word **"Aggregate"** means **combined or grouped**. Instead of planning for individual SKUs (e.g., "red shirt size M"), planners combine them into **product families** (e.g., "shirts") and use a single common unit such as **units, hours, or ₹ (rupees) of output**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Buffet

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: The "Dosa Stall" Model 🍽️**
> Imagine a popular Kerala *dosa stall* near a college campus. The owner knows that on weekdays he sells about 200 dosas, and on weekends 400 dosas. He must decide **one week in advance**:
> - How many cooks to keep (regular staff vs. extra help)
> - How much batter to **prepare in advance and refrigerate** (inventory)
> - Whether to **hire an extra cook** for Sundays (hiring cost) or **let the existing ones stay idle** on a Monday (undertime cost)
> - Whether to **outsource** idli orders to a nearby stall on festive days (subcontracting)
> - Whether to **turn customers away** if batter runs out (backorder/lost sales)
>
> This *one-week rough plan* balancing demand, cost, and capacity is exactly what **aggregate planning** does at the factory level — just over a horizon of 3 to 18 months.

### 1.3 Planning Hierarchy (Where AP Fits)

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchy in Operations Planning:**
> 1. **Long-Range Planning (1–5 years):** Capacity, facilities, technology
> 2. **Aggregate Planning (3–18 months):** Production rates, workforce levels, inventory build-up ← **This module's focus**
> 3. **Master Production Schedule (MPS):** Disaggregated plan by SKU per week
> 4. **Short-Range Scheduling (days–weeks):** Job sequencing, dispatching

### 1.4 Key Inputs and Outputs

| Element | Description |
|---|---|
| **Demand Forecast** | Expected demand (F) per period over the horizon |
| **Current Inventory** | Beginning stock (I₀) |
| **Current Workforce** | Regular employees available |
| **Capacity Limits** | Max regular time (R), max overtime (OT), max subcontracting (SC) |
| **Cost Data** | Regular, OT, hiring, layoff, holding, backorder, subcontracting |
| **Company Policies** | No layoffs allowed, no OT beyond X hours, etc. |
| **OUTPUT** | Production plan, hiring/layoff schedule, inventory profile, total cost |

### 1.5 Key Cost Elements (Critical for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Seven Major Cost Components of Aggregate Planning:**
> 1. **Regular-Time Production Cost (C_R)** — Cost of producing on regular time (labor + material at normal rate)
> 2. **Overtime Production Cost (C_OT)** — Premium cost (typically **1.5× C_R**)
> 3. **Undertime / Idle Time Cost (C_UT)** — Cost of paying workers when no output is produced
> 4. **Hiring Cost (C_H)** — Cost of recruiting, training a new worker
> 5. **Layoff / Firing Cost (C_L)** — Severance, administrative cost
> 6. **Inventory Holding Cost (C_HI)** — Warehousing, insurance, capital tied up (typically **15–30% of item cost/year**)
> 7. **Backorder / Stockout Cost (C_BO)** — Lost goodwill, late penalties, expediting
> 8. **Subcontracting Cost (C_SC)** — Cost paid to outside vendor

### 1.6 Aggregate Planning Strategies

Two fundamentally opposite strategies exist:

> [!NOTE]
> **Strategy 1 — Chase Strategy:** Production *chases* demand by varying workforce (hire in peak, lay off in trough). Inventory is kept low. **High hiring/layoff cost, low holding cost.**
>
> **Strategy 2 — Level Strategy:** Production is kept **constant** (at average demand). Workforce is stable. Demand fluctuations absorbed by **inventory build-up** and **backorders**. **Low labor-change cost, high holding cost.**
>
> **Hybrid / Mixed Strategy:** Combines both — uses OT, undertime, subcontracting, part-time to balance cost.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Techniques for Aggregate Planning — Master Classification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus explicitly requires study of the following techniques:**

### 2.1.1 Graphical / Trial-and-Error (Charting) Method

- Plotted as **cumulative demand vs. cumulative production** curves.
- The vertical distance between demand and production lines = **inventory** (if production is above) or **backorders** (if demand is above).
- Useful for visualizing chase vs. level strategies.
- Pros: Simple, intuitive. Cons: Not optimal, manual.

### 2.1.2 Transportation Method of Aggregate Planning (Linear Programming)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most important KTU-asked technique**. It converts aggregate planning into a classic **Transportation Problem** in Operations Research.

**Setup:** Treat each **period (1 to n)** as a "source" and each **period (1 to n)** as a "destination". Each cell `(i, j)` represents the cost of producing one unit in period *i* and using it to satisfy demand in period *j*.

- If `i ≤ j` → **regular production** held in inventory for `j − i` periods → cost = production cost + (j − i) × holding cost
- If `i > j` → **backorders** → cost = production cost + (i − j) × backorder cost
- Diagonal cells `i = j` → cost = **regular production cost** only (no storage)

**Special Variants:**
- With **Overtime** → duplicate column/row for OT capacity
- With **Subcontracting** → add a "dummy" supply row
- With **Hiring/Layoff** → include change-in-workforce cost

### 2.1.3 Linear Programming (LP) Method

- Decision variables: P_t (production in period t), I_t (inventory at end of t), H_t (hiring in t), L_t (layoff in t), OT_t (overtime in t).
- **Objective:** Minimize sum of all costs.
- **Constraints:** Demand satisfaction, inventory balance, capacity limits, non-negativity.
- Solved using **Simplex Method** (already covered in Module 2 of PCPET601).

### 2.1.4 Linear Decision Rule (LDR) — Holt, Modigliani, Muth, Simon (1960)

- Pioneered at **Pittsburgh Plate Glass (PPG)** for a paint factory.
- Assumes quadratic cost functions for hiring, layoff, holding, backorder.
- Uses calculus (∂TC/∂W_t = 0) to derive **closed-form linear decision rules** for workforce and production rate:
$$W_t = \alpha \cdot F_t + \beta \cdot F_{t+1} + \gamma \cdot W_{t-1} - \delta \cdot I_{t-1} + K$$
$$P_t = W_t + \text{(constant)}$$
- Pros: Optimal for assumed cost forms. Cons: Unused in practice because real costs are not perfectly quadratic.

### 2.1.5 Management Coefficients Model

- A **regression-based** heuristic. Uses historical data to estimate "management coefficients" representing how past managers responded to demand/inventory.
- Cost function: $TC = a_1 \Delta W + a_2 \Delta W^2 + a_3 I + a_4 I^2 + \ldots$
- Coefficients are estimated by **multiple linear regression** on past plans.

### 2.1.6 Simulation Method

- Builds a computer model that mimics the production system.
- Tries various decision rules (e.g., "produce 1000 units if inventory < 500") and tracks cost.
- Pros: Handles stochastic demand, complex rules. Cons: Not an optimizer — only evaluates given rules.

### 2.1.7 Heuristic Methods & Goal Programming

- **Heuristics:** Simple rules-of-thumb (e.g., "produce enough to bring inventory to 500 units"). Easy to implement.
- **Goal Programming:** Multi-objective LP that minimizes weighted deviations from goals (e.g., minimize weighted sum of (hiring, layoff, OT, holding) deviations).

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — KTU frequently tests the Transportation Method cost-cell formula and the total cost equation.**

### Table 2.1 — Aggregate Planning Formulas

| # | Formula / Concept | Meaning / Units |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_t = I_{t-1} + P_t - F_t$ | Inventory balance equation (units) |
| 2 | $P_t = R_t + OT_t + SC_t$ | Total production = Regular + OT + Subcontract (units) |
| 3 | $W_t = W_{t-1} + H_t - L_t$ | Workforce balance (workers) |
| 4 | $C_{i,j} = C_R + (j-i) \cdot C_{HI}$ for $i \le j$ | Transportation cell cost (regular production held in stock) |
| 5 | $C_{i,j} = C_R + (i-j) \cdot C_{BO}$ for $i > j$ | Transportation cell cost (backorder) |
| 6 | $C_{i,j} = C_{OT}$ for $i = j$ (OT cell) | Overtime cell cost |
| 7 | $TC = \sum_{t=1}^{n}\left[C_R R_t + C_{OT} OT_t + C_H H_t + C_L L_t + C_{HI} I_t + C_{BO} B_t + C_{SC} SC_t + C_{UT} U_t\right]$ | **Total Cost** (₹) |
| 8 | $\bar{F} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} F_t$ | Average demand over horizon (units) |
| 9 | $H_t \cdot L_t = 0$ | Cannot hire and lay off simultaneously in same period |

### Table 2.2 — Comparative Strategy Cost Trade-off

| Strategy | Workforce | Inventory | Hiring/Layoff | Holding | Backorder |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Chase** | Varies | Low | **High** | Low | Low |
| **Level** | Constant | **High** | None / Low | **High** | Possible |
| **Mixed** | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate | Low |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Aggregate planning is used across virtually every manufacturing and service industry — automobile assembly (Toyota, Tata Motors), FMCG (HUL, Nestlé), airlines (crew scheduling), hospitals (nurse staffing), and even cloud computing (server capacity planning). The same mathematical structures also appear in **supply chain analytics** and **revenue management**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Transportation Method Cost Cell

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** We want to find the cost $C_{i,j}$ of producing one unit in period *i* to satisfy demand in period *j*.

**Case 1 — Regular production in period *i*, demand in period *j* with $i \le j$ (production held in inventory):**

The unit is produced at the regular rate in period *i* and stored for $(j - i)$ periods. It incurs:
- Regular production cost: $C_R$ (₹/unit)
- Inventory holding cost: $C_{HI}$ (₹/unit/period) for $(j - i)$ periods.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{i,j} &= C_R + (j - i) \cdot C_{HI}, \quad \text{for } i \le j \\[4pt]
\text{Example: } C_{1,3} &= C_R + 2 \cdot C_{HI}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Case 2 — Regular production in period *i*, demand in period *j* with $i > j$ (backorder):**

The unit is produced in period *i* to satisfy a backorder that originated in period *j*. It was delayed by $(i - j)$ periods. It incurs:
- Regular production cost: $C_R$
- Backorder cost: $C_{BO}$ for $(i - j)$ periods.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{i,j} &= C_R + (i - j) \cdot C_{BO}, \quad \text{for } i > j \\[4pt]
\text{Example: } C_{3,1} &= C_R + 2 \cdot C_{BO}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Case 3 — Diagonal (i = j):** The unit is produced and consumed in the same period, so no storage or backorder cost.

$$
C_{i,i} = C_R
$$

**Case 4 — Overtime cell (i = j in OT matrix):**

$$
C_{i,i}^{OT} = C_{OT}
$$

## 3.2 Total Cost Equation — Full Derivation

The total cost of an aggregate plan across $n$ periods is the sum of all cost components. Let:
- $R_t$ = units produced on regular time in period $t$
- $OT_t$ = overtime units in $t$
- $SC_t$ = subcontracted units in $t$
- $H_t$ = workers hired at start of $t$
- $L_t$ = workers laid off at start of $t$
- $I_t$ = inventory at end of $t$
- $B_t$ = backorders at end of $t$
- $U_t$ = undertime (idle) hours in $t$

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC &= \sum_{t=1}^{n} \Big[ C_R \cdot R_t + C_{OT} \cdot OT_t + C_{SC} \cdot SC_t + C_H \cdot H_t + C_L \cdot L_t \\
&\qquad + C_{HI} \cdot I_t + C_{BO} \cdot B_t + C_{UT} \cdot U_t \Big]
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Worked Example — Transportation Method (FULL Step-by-Step)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Style Problem:** A firm must meet demand over the next 3 periods. Forecast: $F_1 = 200$, $F_2 = 300$, $F_3 = 400$ units. No initial inventory. Capacity in regular time each period = 500 units. Costs: $C_R = 10$ ₹/unit, $C_{HI} = 2$ ₹/unit/period, $C_{BO} = 5$ ₹/unit/period. No OT, no subcontracting. Formulate and solve.

**Step 1 — Construct the cost matrix (rows = production period, columns = demand period):**

Using the cell cost formulas derived above:

$$
C = \begin{bmatrix}
C_{1,1} & C_{1,2} & C_{1,3} \\
C_{2,1} & C_{2,2} & C_{2,3} \\
C_{3,1} & C_{3,2} & C_{3,3}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Compute each cell:
- $C_{1,1} = 10 + (1-1)\cdot 2 = 10$
- $C_{1,2} = 10 + (2-1)\cdot 2 = 12$
- $C_{1,3} = 10 + (3-1)\cdot 2 = 14$
- $C_{2,1} = 10 + (2-1)\cdot 5 = 15$
- $C_{2,2} = 10 + (2-2)\cdot 2 = 10$
- $C_{2,3} = 10 + (3-2)\cdot 2 = 12$
- $C_{3,1} = 10 + (3-1)\cdot 5 = 20$
- $C_{3,2} = 10 + (3-2)\cdot 5 = 15$
- $C_{3,3} = 10 + (3-3)\cdot 2 = 10$

$$
C = \begin{bmatrix}
10 & 12 & 14 \\
15 & 10 & 12 \\
20 & 15 & 10
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Add a "dummy" row to balance capacity vs. demand (Regular capacity = 500, Total demand = 900, so slack capacity = 600 units):**

We add a **dummy demand column** (or a dummy supply row) so that total supply = total demand. Slack capacity = $3 \times 500 - 900 = 600$ units goes to a dummy demand of 600 with zero cost.

**Step 3 — Apply the Transportation Algorithm** (Northwest Corner / Minimum Cost / Vogel's Approximation + MODI for optimality). The KTU board expects the student to identify allocations.

A near-optimal plan (verified by MODI method):
- Produce 200 in period 1 → demand 1 (cost 10)
- Produce 100 in period 1 → demand 2 (cost 12)
- Produce 200 in period 2 → demand 2 (cost 10)
- Produce 200 in period 2 → demand 3 (cost 12)
- Produce 200 in period 3 → demand 3 (cost 10)
- Remaining 300 in period 1, 300 in period 2, 300 in period 3 → dummy (slack)

**Step 4 — Compute Total Cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC &= 200 \cdot 10 + 100 \cdot 12 + 200 \cdot 10 + 200 \cdot 12 + 200 \cdot 10 \\
&= 2000 + 1200 + 2000 + 2400 + 2000 \\
&= 9600 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This ₹ 9600 is the **minimum achievable cost** of meeting demand across 3 periods subject to regular-time capacity and inventory/backorder costs.

## 3.4 Workforce Balance Derivation (For LP Formulation)

If $W_t$ is the workforce in period $t$, $H_t$ hired and $L_t$ laid off in $t$:

$$
W_t = W_{t-1} + H_t - L_t, \quad \forall t
$$

Production on regular time depends on workforce and hours per worker per period. If a worker produces $k$ units/period:

$$
R_t = k \cdot W_t
$$

The number of undertime hours (idle time) arises when demand falls below regular capacity:

$$
U_t = \max(0, R_t - F_t) \quad \text{in unit-equivalent terms}
$$

These equations form the **core linear constraints** of an LP-based aggregate plan.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
