<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define Aggregate Planning. List any four aggregate planning strategies with one-line descriptions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Aggregate Planning is a medium-range (3–18 months) planning process that determines overall production rates, inventory levels, workforce size, and subcontracting requirements to satisfy forecasted demand at minimum total cost (1 mark).

**Four Strategies (2 marks):**
1. **Chase Strategy:** Production matches demand by varying workforce through hiring/layoffs.
2. **Level Strategy:** Production rate is kept constant; inventory absorbs demand fluctuation.
3. **Mixed/Hybrid Strategy:** Combines level production with OT, subcontracting, part-time, backorders.
4. **Subcontracting-Heavy Strategy:** Uses external vendors to absorb demand peaks.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is the cost cell formula used in the Transportation Method of Aggregate Planning? Explain with one example for holding and one for backorder.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The transportation method uses a cost cell matrix where each cell $C_{i,j}$ represents the cost of producing in period $i$ to satisfy demand in period $j$ (1 mark).

**Holding case (i ≤ j):** Unit is produced in *i* and held in inventory until *j*.
$$C_{i,j} = C_R + (j - i) \cdot C_{HI}$$
Example: $C_{1,3} = 10 + 2 \times 2 = 14$ ₹/unit (1 mark).

**Backorder case (i > j):** Unit is produced late in *i* to clear a backorder from *j*.
$$C_{i,j} = C_R + (i - j) \cdot C_{BO}$$
Example: $C_{3,1} = 10 + 2 \times 5 = 20$ ₹/unit (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Choice-Based)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Transportation Method Full Problem**

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style Question]**
>
> A manufacturing firm must meet the demand for the next 4 periods. Forecasts (units): $F_1 = 600$, $F_2 = 700$, $F_3 = 800$, $F_4 = 500$. Regular-time capacity = 700 units/period. No initial inventory. Costs: Regular production = ₹ 10/unit, Holding = ₹ 2/unit/period, Backorder = ₹ 4/unit/period. Overtime is NOT allowed.
>
> **(a) [7 marks]** Formulate the problem as a transportation model. Construct the cost matrix.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** Solve the transportation problem using Vogel's Approximation Method (VAM) and compute the total cost of the aggregate plan.

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Cost Matrix Construction**

Total demand = $600 + 700 + 800 + 500 = 2600$ units.
Total regular-time capacity = $4 \times 700 = 2800$ units.
Slack capacity = $2800 - 2600 = 200$ units → introduced as a dummy demand column with cost = 0.

Cost cell formula:
- $C_{i,j} = 10 + (j-i)\cdot 2$ for $i \le j$ (holding)
- $C_{i,j} = 10 + (i-j)\cdot 4$ for $i > j$ (backorder)
- $C_{i,i} = 10$ (diagonal)

**Cost Matrix (rows = production period 1–4, cols = demand 1–4, plus dummy):**

$$
C = \begin{bmatrix}
10 & 12 & 14 & 16 & 0 \\
14 & 10 & 12 & 14 & 0 \\
18 & 14 & 10 & 12 & 0 \\
22 & 18 & 14 & 10 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

| | D1=600 | D2=700 | D3=800 | D4=500 | Dummy=200 | Supply |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **P1=700** | 10 | 12 | 14 | 16 | 0 | 700 |
| **P2=700** | 14 | 10 | 12 | 14 | 0 | 700 |
| **P3=700** | 18 | 14 | 10 | 12 | 0 | 700 |
| **P4=700** | 22 | 18 | 14 | 10 | 0 | 700 |
| **Demand** | 600 | 700 | 800 | 500 | 200 | 3300 |

**Part (b) — VAM Solution**

**Step 1 — Compute penalties (difference between two smallest costs in each row/column):**

Row penalties:
- P1: $12 - 10 = 2$
- P2: $12 - 10 = 2$
- P3: $12 - 10 = 2$
- P4: $14 - 10 = 4$

Column penalties:
- D1: $14 - 10 = 4$
- D2: $12 - 10 = 2$
- D3: $12 - 10 = 2$
- D4: $12 - 10 = 2$
- Dummy: $0 - 0 = 0$

**Step 2 — Highest penalty = 4 (tied at P4 row and D1 column). Choose P4 (or D1). Allocate to smallest cost cell in P4:**

Smallest cost in P4 = 0 (dummy) → allocate $\min(700, 200) = 200$ to (P4, Dummy). P4 supply = 500, Dummy demand = 0.

**Re-compute. Next highest penalty = 4 (D1 column).** Smallest cost in D1 = 10 (P1). Allocate $\min(700, 600) = 600$ to (P1, D1). P1 supply = 100, D1 demand = 0.

**Next highest penalty = 2 (multiple).** Choose any. Take P1 row: smallest = 0 (dummy, now full) → 12 (D2). Allocate $\min(100, 700) = 100$ to (P1, D2). P1 supply = 0, D2 demand = 600.

**Continue similarly for remaining rows/columns. Final VAM allocation:**

| Allocation | Units |
|---|---|
| P1 → D1 | 600 |
| P1 → D2 | 100 |
| P2 → D2 | 600 |
| P2 → D3 | 100 |
| P3 → D3 | 700 |
| P3 → D4 | 0 (next cell with demand) — actually allocate 0 |
| P3 → D4 | 0 |
| P4 → D3 | 0 |
| P4 → D4 | 500 |
| P4 → Dummy | 200 |

Let me re-allocate more carefully to balance all supplies and demands (3 marks reserved for valid VAM/MODI steps). A correct VAM-feasible solution is:

| Cell | Units | Cost | Subtotal |
|---|---|---|---|
| P1→D1 | 600 | 10 | 6000 |
| P1→D2 | 100 | 12 | 1200 |
| P2→D2 | 600 | 10 | 6000 |
| P2→D3 | 100 | 12 | 1200 |
| P3→D3 | 700 | 10 | 7000 |
| P4→D4 | 500 | 10 | 5000 |
| P4→Dummy | 200 | 0 | 0 |

**Total Cost Computation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC &= 600(10) + 100(12) + 600(10) + 100(12) + 700(10) + 500(10) + 200(0) \\
&= 6000 + 1200 + 6000 + 1200 + 7000 + 5000 + 0 \\
&= 26400 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply MODI (u_i + v_j = C_{i,j}) to verify optimality:**

With 7 allocations and m+n−1 = 4+5−1 = 8 needed, the solution above uses 7. One more independent allocation is needed (typically in a zero-cost dummy cell to close degeneracy). After running MODI:
- All opportunity costs $d_{i,j} = C_{i,j} - u_i - v_j \ge 0$ → solution is **optimal** (3 marks for optimality verification + final answer).

**Final Total Cost = ₹ 26,400.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative: LP Formulation + Strategy Discussion**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Style Question]**
>
> A company produces a single product. Forecast for next 3 months: 2000, 3000, 4000 units. Current workforce = 100 workers; each produces 25 units/month. Initial inventory = 500 units. Overtime capacity = 500 units/month. Hiring cost = ₹ 300/worker, Layoff cost = ₹ 500/worker, Regular cost = ₹ 50/unit, Overtime cost = ₹ 70/unit, Holding = ₹ 5/unit/month, Backorder = ₹ 10/unit/month.
>
> **(a) [7 marks]** Formulate this as a Linear Programming problem. Define decision variables clearly.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** Compare Chase vs. Level strategy for this problem and recommend which is better with justification.

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — LP Formulation**

**Decision Variables (1 mark):**
- $W_t$ = workforce in month $t$ (workers), $t = 1,2,3$
- $H_t$ = workers hired at start of month $t$
- $L_t$ = workers laid off at start of month $t$
- $R_t$ = regular-time production in month $t$ (units)
- $OT_t$ = overtime production in month $t$ (units)
- $I_t$ = inventory at end of month $t$ (units)
- $B_t$ = backorders at end of month $t$ (units)

**Objective Function (2 marks):** Minimize total cost
$$\min Z = \sum_{t=1}^{3}\big[50 R_t + 70 OT_t + 300 H_t + 500 L_t + 5 I_t + 10 B_t\big]$$

**Constraints (4 marks):**
1. **Demand Satisfaction:** $I_{t-1} + R_t + OT_t + B_{t-1} - B_t = F_t$, with $I_0 = 500$.
2. **Workforce Balance:** $W_t = W_{t-1} + H_t - L_t$, with $W_0 = 100$.
3. **Production Capacity:** $R_t = 25 W_t$ (or $R_t \le 25 W_t$).
4. **Overtime Limit:** $OT_t \le 500$ for all $t$.
5. **Non-Negativity:** $W_t, H_t, L_t, R_t, OT_t, I_t, B_t \ge 0$.
6. **Integer (optional):** $H_t, L_t$ integers.

This LP can be solved by the **Simplex Method** (covered in Module 2).

**Part (b) — Chase vs. Level Comparison**

| Aspect | Chase Strategy | Level Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| Workforce | $W_1=80, W_2=120, W_3=160$ (varies) | $W_1=W_2=W_3=120$ (constant) |
| Hiring cost (₹) | $0 + 300(40) + 300(40) = 24000$ | $300(20) = 6000$ |
| Layoff cost (₹) | $300(20) = 6000$ | 0 |
| Holding cost | Low (minimal inventory) | High (excess inventory 500 units) |
| Backorder | 0 | 0 |
| Total variable cost | Higher labor-change cost | Lower labor-change, higher holding |

**Recommendation (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Recommendation: Level Strategy is preferred for this problem.**
> 
> **Justification:** The hiring cost (₹ 300) is less than the layoff cost (₹ 500) and both are reasonable compared to the holding cost of only ₹ 5/unit/month. The level strategy avoids the high layoff cost incurred in the chase strategy in month 1 (₹ 6000). The chase strategy requires hiring 80 workers in months 2 and 3 — total labor change cost = ₹ 30,000, whereas level strategy total = ₹ 6,000 + holding costs. Hence **level strategy** yields a lower total cost.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make (and lose 2–4 marks each):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the cost-cell formula sign:** $C_{i,j} = C_R + (j-i)\cdot C_{HI}$ for $i \le j$, **not** $(i-j)$. Reversing the inequality leads to wrong cost matrix. (–2 marks)
>
> 2. **Not balancing the transportation problem:** If supply ≠ demand, students must add a **dummy row or column** with **zero cost**. Skipping this step makes the formulation infeasible. (–2 marks)
>
> 3. **Confusing Period vs. Time-unit:** KTU uses *periods* (months, quarters). Make sure holding cost is in ₹/unit/**period** and backorder cost in ₹/unit/**period** consistently.
>
> 4. **Ignoring degeneracy:** In transportation, if number of allocations < m + n − 1, place a tiny ε (epsilon) in a zero-cost cell to proceed with MODI.
>
> 5. **Wrong inventory balance equation:** $I_t = I_{t-1} + P_t - F_t$ is the **correct** sign convention. Some students write $I_t = I_{t-1} - P_t + F_t$ (reversed). (–1 mark)
>
> 6. **Forgetting the dummy for slack capacity:** If regular capacity > total demand, the difference must be assigned to a dummy demand (zero cost). Not doing this makes the plan infeasible. (–2 marks)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aggregate Planning — Rapid Revision (30-second glance):**
>
> - **Definition:** Medium-range (3–18 months) plan to match supply and demand at **minimum cost**.
> - **"Aggregate"** = combined product families, not individual SKUs.
> - **Seven cost components:** $C_R$, $C_{OT}$, $C_{UT}$, $C_H$, $C_L$, $C_{HI}$, $C_{BO}$ (+ $C_{SC}$).
> - **Inventory balance:** $I_t = I_{t-1} + P_t - F_t$.
> - **Workforce balance:** $W_t = W_{t-1} + H_t - L_t$.
> - **Total cost equation:** Sum of (production costs + workforce change costs + inventory/backorder costs).
>
> **Techniques covered in KTU Module 4:**
> 1. Graphical/Trial-and-Error
> 2. Transportation Method of Aggregate Planning (most important)
> 3. Linear Programming
> 4. Linear Decision Rule (Holt-Modigliani-Muth-Simon)
> 5. Management Coefficients Model
> 6. Simulation
> 7. Goal Programming
>
> **Transportation Method — Key Formulas:**
> - $C_{i,j} = C_R + (j-i)\cdot C_{HI}$ for $i \le j$
> - $C_{i,j} = C_R + (i-j)\cdot C_{BO}$ for $i > j$
> - Diagonal = $C_R$ only
> - Add **dummy row/column** with zero cost for slack capacity
>
> **Strategies:**
> - **Chase** → low inventory, high hiring/layoff
> - **Level** → high inventory, low labor change
> - **Mixed** → balances both
>
> **KTU board expects:**
> - Numerical problem solving via Transportation Method
> - Cost-matrix construction from forecast
> - VAM/MODI solution steps
> - Total cost computation
> - Short notes on LDR, Management Coefficients, Simulation
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Master Scheduling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET601 - OPERATIONS RESEARCH AND PRODUCTION MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Master Scheduling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Master Scheduling

**Master Scheduling** is the process of translating the aggregate business plan (sales and operations plan) into a detailed, time-phased plan for specific end-items or product families that drives material requirements planning, capacity planning, and shop-floor execution.

The central output of this process is the **Master Production Schedule (MPS)** — a statement of what end products will be produced, in what quantities, and when they will be available to fulfill anticipated demand.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** The Master Production Schedule is a time-phased plan that specifies the quantity and timing of finished products to be produced, taking into account customer orders, forecast demand, available inventory, and capacity constraints.

### 1.2 Key Components of an MPS Statement

The MPS explicitly specifies three critical elements for each time bucket:

1. **Quantity**: How many units of the end item to produce.
2. **Timing**: In which time period the units will be available.
3. **Units**: The specific part number / SKU (Stock Keeping Unit) being produced.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Master Schedule as a "Restaurant Meal Plan"

Imagine a popular restaurant manager planning the next 12 weeks:

- The **business plan** says: "We expect 10,000 meals next quarter."
- The **MPS** breaks it down: "In Week 3, produce 200 Biryani meals; in Week 4, produce 250 Pasta dishes."
- The **chef (production)** uses this to buy ingredients (raw materials).
- The **waiter (customer)** gets the food exactly when promised.

Just as the meal plan translates vague revenue goals into specific daily dishes, the **Master Schedule translates the abstract business plan into specific, actionable production orders**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** The MPS is NOT a forecast. A forecast is an input; the MPS is a *commitment* — a frozen plan once inside the **Demand Time Fence**.

### 1.4 Position of MPS in the Production Planning Hierarchy

Master Scheduling occupies the critical middle layer of the production planning hierarchy:

1. **Business Plan** (Top — Long range, 1-5 years)
2. **Sales & Operations Plan (S&OP)** (1-18 months)
3. **Master Production Schedule (MPS)** ← *Master Scheduling sits here*
4. **Material Requirements Planning (MRP)** ← Driven by MPS
5. **Shop Floor Execution** (Bottom — Daily)

### 1.5 Standard Metrics Used in Master Scheduling

The following industry-standard metrics are emphasized in KTU examinations:

- **Planning Horizon**: Typically **12 to 18 months** into the future.
- **Time Buckets**: Usually weekly for first 4-6 weeks, then monthly.
- **Safety Stock**: Buffer inventory to absorb demand variability. **Units: pieces**.
- **Lead Time**: Time required to produce or procure an item. **Units: days/weeks**.
- **Lot Size**: Quantity produced per production run. **Units: pieces/batch**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Objectives of Master Scheduling

The Master Schedule is designed to achieve the following objectives:

- **Translate aggregate plans** into specific end-item production quantities.
- **Balance demand and supply** by reconciling forecast with actual customer orders.
- **Drive MRP** by providing firm, time-phased requirements for parent items.
- **Serve as a visible commitment** to customers regarding delivery dates.
- **Identify capacity problems early** through Rough Cut Capacity Planning (RCCP).
- **Decouple the customer** from the shop floor — customers see only the MPS commitments, not internal disruptions.

### 2.2 Inputs to the Master Schedule

The MPS is built upon five primary inputs:

1. **End-item Forecast** — Statistical projection of expected demand.
2. **Customer Orders** — Actual booked orders with committed delivery dates.
3. **Current Inventory Status** — On-hand stock and work-in-process.
4. **Capacity Constraints** — What the factory can realistically produce.
5. **Business Policies** — Safety stock levels, lot-sizing rules, customer service priorities.

### 2.3 Outputs from the Master Schedule

1. **Planned Order Releases** — Authoritative production orders for the shop floor.
2. **Planned Order Receipts** — Expected completion dates and quantities.
3. **Available-to-Promise (ATP)** quantities — Uncommitted stock for new orders.
4. **Exception Reports** — Flagged items where demand exceeds supply.
5. **RCCP Output** — Capacity feasibility of the MPS.

### 2.4 Time Fences in Master Scheduling

A critical concept in KTU exams is the **Time Fence structure** within the planning horizon:

| Time Fence | Description | MPS Behavior | Allowed Changes |
|------------|-------------|--------------|-----------------|
| **Demand Time Fence (DTF)** | Inside DTF (typically cumulative lead time) | MPS is **FROZEN** — no changes allowed | Only quantity reductions to meet customer orders |
| **Planning Time Fence (PTF)** | Between DTF and PTF | MPS is **SLUSHY** — limited changes | Quantity and timing can be adjusted within ±10% |
| **Beyond PTF** | Far future horizon | MPS is **LIQUID** — freely changeable | Anything can be changed as new forecasts arrive |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always mention that the **Demand Time Fence equals the cumulative lead time** of the end item. This is a high-yield concept.

### 2.5 Available-to-Promise (ATP) — The Core MPS Calculation

**ATP** represents the uncommitted portion of planned production that can be promised to new customers.

The logic is:

$$\text{ATP} = \text{Next Planned Production Quantity} - \text{Committed Customer Orders Until Next Production}$$

ATP is recalculated each time a new order is booked, providing a **real-time commitment** capability.

### 2.6 Projected Available Balance (PAB)

The PAB tracks expected inventory at the end of each time bucket:

$$PAB_t = PAB_{t-1} + \text{Planned Receipts}_t - \text{Demand}_t$$

Where:
- $PAB_t$ = Projected Available Balance at end of period $t$
- $PAB_{t-1}$ = Projected Available Balance at end of period $t-1$
- $\text{Planned Receipts}_t$ = Quantity received from production in period $t$
- $\text{Demand}_t$ = Gross requirements (forecast + actual orders) in period $t$

If $PAB_t$ falls below the safety stock level, a new planned order must be released.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Description | When Used |
|---------|-------------|-----------|
| $PAB_t = PAB_{t-1} + R_t - D_t$ | Projected Available Balance | Every period inventory check |
| $ATP = MPS_q - \sum \text{Committed Orders}$ | Available-to-Promise | Customer order promising |
| $\text{Net Requirements} = \text{Gross Req.} - \text{On-hand} - \text{On-order}$ | MRP input calculation | Material planning |
| $\text{Cumulative Lead Time} = \sum \text{Component Lead Times}$ | Determines DTF | Time fence setup |
| $\text{Production Quantity} = \text{Lot Size (multiple of)}$ | Lot sizing rule | Order quantity determination |
| $\text{Safety Stock} = z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}$ | Statistical safety stock | Service level definition |
| $RCCP_{utilization} = \frac{\text{Hours Required}}{\text{Hours Available}}$ | Capacity check | Feasibility analysis |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Convention:** All quantities are in pieces/batches, time is in days/weeks, capacity is in standard hours.

### 2.8 Difference Between MPS and Detailed Scheduling

| Aspect | Master Schedule | Detailed (Shop Floor) Schedule |
|--------|----------------|--------------------------------|
| Horizon | Weeks to months | Hours to days |
| Item level | End products / key subassemblies | Components and operations |
| Detail | Weekly buckets, end-item quantities | Sequence, machine, operator |
| Stability | Frozen inside DTF | Continuously rescheduled |

### 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

Master Scheduling is critical in industries such as:
- **Automotive** (vehicle production sequencing)
- **Electronics** (smartphone assembly scheduling)
- **Aerospace** (aircraft build planning)
- **Process Industries** (batch chemical production)

It enables just-in-time (JIT) systems by providing firm forward visibility.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: ATP Calculation

**Problem Setup:**
A company produces industrial pumps. The MPS shows:
- Week 1: 0 production, 30 customer orders committed
- Week 2: 100 units planned production, 55 orders committed
- Week 3: 0 production, 20 orders committed
- Week 4: 150 units planned production, 110 orders committed

A new customer calls in Week 1 asking how many pumps can be promised for Week 4 delivery.

**Step 1 — Identify Next Production After Current Query Time:**

Query is in Week 1. The next production release is in Week 2 (100 units).

**Step 2 — Sum Committed Orders Between Week 2 and the Next Production After That:**

Commits between Week 2 and Week 4 (exclusive) = Week 3 orders = 20 units.

**Step 3 — Apply ATP Formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ATP &= (\text{Next Production Quantity}) - (\text{Committed Orders Until Following Production}) \\
ATP &= 100 - 20 \\
ATP &= 80 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

The company can promise up to **80 additional pumps** for delivery in the Week 2-3 window without disrupting the existing commitments.

### 3.2 Worked Example: Projected Available Balance Calculation

**Problem Setup:**
- Beginning inventory (PAB Week 0): **0 units**
- Safety stock policy: **20 units**
- Lot size: **lot-for-lot**

| Week | Forecast | Customer Orders | MPS Production |
|------|----------|-----------------|----------------|
| 1    | 50       | 45              | 60             |
| 2    | 50       | 55              | 0              |
| 3    | 50       | 60              | 70             |
| 4    | 50       | 40              | 0              |

**Step 1 — Determine Gross Requirements (use the larger of forecast or orders — depending on policy). Here we use Actual Orders when ≥ Forecast, else Forecast:**

Week 1: max(50, 45) = 50
Week 2: max(50, 55) = 55
Week 3: max(50, 60) = 60
Week 4: max(50, 40) = 50

**Step 2 — Calculate PAB for Each Week:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PAB_1 &= PAB_0 + \text{Production}_1 - \text{Demand}_1 \\
PAB_1 &= 0 + 60 - 50 \\
PAB_1 &= 10 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $PAB_1 = 10 < \text{Safety Stock} = 20$, **action needed** (but since production already occurred, this would trigger investigation).

$$
\begin{aligned}
PAB_2 &= PAB_1 + \text{Production}_2 - \text{Demand}_2 \\
PAB_2 &= 10 + 0 - 55 \\
PAB_2 &= -45 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

Negative PAB indicates a **stock-out** — the MPS is infeasible and must be revised.

$$
\begin{aligned}
PAB_3 &= PAB_2 + \text{Production}_3 - \text{Demand}_3 \\
PAB_3 &= -45 + 70 - 60 \\
PAB_3 &= -35 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
PAB_4 &= PAB_3 + \text{Production}_4 - \text{Demand}_4 \\
PAB_4 &= -35 + 0 - 50 \\
PAB_4 &= -85 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — MPS Revision Required:**

The Master Scheduler must release additional planned orders, e.g., add 50 units in Week 2 production to bring $PAB_2 = +5$, or move production earlier.

### 3.3 Master Scheduling Table (Standard KTU Format)

| Week | Forecast | Orders | Gross Requirements | Beginning Inventory | Production Receipts | Ending Inventory (PAB) | ATP |
|------|----------|--------|--------------------|--------------------|--------------------|-----------------------|-----|
| 0    | -        | -      | -                  | 30                 | -                  | 30                    | -   |
| 1    | 50       | 45     | 50                 | 30                 | 50                 | 30                    | 5   |
| 2    | 50       | 55     | 55                 | 30                 | 55                 | 30                    | 0   |
| 3    | 50       | 60     | 60                 | 30                 | 60                 | 30                    | 0   |
| 4    | 50       | 40     | 50                 | 30                 | 50                 | 30                    | 0   |

**ATP Calculation Logic (Week 1):**
ATP = Production Receipts (Week 1) − Committed Orders before next production = 50 − 45 = **5 units**.

### 3.4 Derivation of Time Fence Position

The **Demand Time Fence (DTF)** is mathematically defined as:

$$
DTF = \sum_{i=1}^{n} L_i
$$

Where:
- $L_i$ = Lead time of the $i^{th}$ level in the BOM (Bill of Materials)
- $n$ = Total number of levels from raw material to finished product

For a 3-level product with lead times of 3 days, 5 days, and 7 days respectively:

$$
DTF = 3 + 5 + 7 = 15 \text{ days}
$$

This means the MPS is frozen for the next **15 days** (typically rounded up to the next weekly bucket).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Scheduling Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Business Plan & S&OP"] --> nodeB["End Item Forecast"]
    nodeC["Customer Orders DB"] --> nodeD["Order Book Review"]
    nodeB --> nodeE["Master Scheduler Decision Engine"]
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeF["Inventory Status"] --> nodeE
    nodeG["Capacity Data"] --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeH["Draft MPS"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["RCCP Feasibility Check"]
    nodeI -->|"Infeasible"| nodeJ["Revise MPS"]
    nodeJ --> nodeH
    nodeI -->|"Feasible"| nodeK["Frozen MPS"]
    nodeK --> nodeL["MRP Processing"]
    nodeK --> nodeM["ATP Computation"]
    nodeK --> nodeN["Shop Floor Releases"]
```

### 4.2 Time Fence Structure Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA["TODAY"] -->|"Frozen Zone (DTF)"| nodeB["Demand Time Fence"]
    nodeB -->|"Slushy Zone (PTF)"| nodeC["Planning Time Fence"]
    nodeC -->|"Liquid Zone"| nodeD["Planning Horizon End"]
    nodeE["Customer Order Entry"] -->|"Allowed"| nodeA
    nodeE -->|"Limited Adjust"| nodeB
    nodeE -->|"Free Modification"| nodeC
    nodeE -->|"Free Modification"| nodeD
```

### 4.3 MPS Inputs-Outputs Block Architecture

```mermaid

    subgraph INPUTS
        nodeIn1["Aggregate Plan"]
        nodeIn2["Customer Orders"]
        nodeIn3["Forecast"]
        nodeIn4["Inventory Data"]

        nodeIn6["BOM & Routing"]
    end
    subgraph PROCESS
        nodeProc1["Master Scheduler"]
        nodeProc2["RCCP Engine"]
        nodeProc3["ATP Calculator"]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUTS
        nodeOut1["Planned Order Releases"]
        nodeOut2["ATP Quantities"]
        nodeOut3["Exception Reports"]
        nodeOut4["Capacity Utilization"]
        nodeOut5["MRP Input File"]
    end
    nodeIn1 --> nodeProc1
    nodeIn2 --> nodeProc1
    nodeIn3 --> nodeProc1
    nodeIn4 --> nodeProc1
    nodeIn5 --> nodeProc2
    nodeIn6 --> nodeProc1
    nodeProc1 --> nodeProc2
    nodeProc1 --> nodeProc3
    nodeProc2 --> nodeOut4
    nodeProc3 --> nodeOut2
    nodeProc1 --> nodeOut1
    nodeProc1 --> nodeOut3
    nodeOut1 --> nodeOut5
```

### 4.4 Hierarchical Planning Pyramid

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["Business Plan (1-5 years)"] --> nodeB["Sales & Operations Plan (1-18 months)"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Master Production Schedule (Weeks-Months)"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Material Requirements Plan (Days-Weeks)"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Shop Floor Schedule (Hours-Days)"]
    nodeC -.->|"Drives"| nodeF["Rough Cut Capacity Plan"]
    nodeD -.->|"Drives"| nodeG["Capacity Requirements Plan"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Define Master Production Schedule (MPS). List any four inputs of MPS.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):**
The Master Production Schedule (MPS) is a time-phased plan that specifies the quantity and timing of finished end-items to be produced, based on forecast demand, actual customer orders, current inventory, and production capacity. It serves as a bridge between the aggregate sales & operations plan and the detailed material requirements planning system.

**Four Inputs (1 Mark):**
1. End-item demand forecast
2. Actual customer orders
3. Current inventory status (on-hand + on-order)
4. Production capacity constraints

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Explain the concept of Available-to-Promise (ATP) in master scheduling.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
ATP (Available-to-Promise) is the quantity of a finished product that is currently uncommitted and can be promised to new customers without affecting existing orders. It is calculated as:

ATP = Planned Production Receipt − Committed Customer Orders until the next planned production.

ATP is recalculated dynamically whenever a new order is booked, allowing the master scheduler to give real-time delivery commitments to customers.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a) Explain the concept of Time Fences in Master Scheduling. Discuss the three zones of the planning horizon with their characteristics. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Concept of Time Fences (2 Marks):**
Time fences are demarcation points within the MPS planning horizon that define how freely the schedule can be changed. They protect the stability of near-term production while allowing flexibility for the distant future.

**Three Zones (5 Marks):**

**1. Frozen Zone (Inside Demand Time Fence — DTF):**
- The MPS is **completely frozen**.
- DTF = cumulative lead time of the end item.
- No changes allowed except reducing quantities to fulfill customer orders.
- This protects the shop floor from constant disruptions.

**2. Slushy Zone (Between DTF and PTF):**
- The MPS can be adjusted within defined limits (e.g., ±10%).
- Useful for accommodating minor forecast revisions.
- Requires master scheduler approval.

**3. Liquid Zone (Beyond Planning Time Fence):**
- The MPS is freely changeable.
- Forecasts can be updated entirely.
- Used for capacity planning and what-if analysis.

**Significance (0 Mark for memory, included for completeness):**
Time fences balance customer responsiveness with manufacturing stability.

---

**(b) The MPS for a product shows the following data. Calculate the Available-to-Promise (ATP) for Week 1 and Week 3. (7 Marks)**

| Week | Planned Production | Customer Orders |
|------|--------------------|-----------------|
| 1    | 100                | 60              |
| 2    | 0                  | 25              |
| 3    | 120                | 90              |
| 4    | 0                  | 15              |

**Model Solution:**

**ATP for Week 1 (3 Marks):**

The next production after Week 1 is in Week 3.
Committed orders between Week 1 and Week 3 (exclusive of Week 3) = Week 2 orders = 25.

$$
ATP_1 = \text{Production in Week 1} - \text{Committed Orders until next production}
$$

$$
ATP_1 = 100 - 25 = 75 \text{ units}
$$

**ATP for Week 3 (4 Marks):**

The next production after Week 3 is in Week 5 or beyond — we assume no production visible.
Committed orders beyond Week 3 (till next production) = 15.

$$
ATP_3 = 120 - 15 = 105 \text{ units}
$$

**Final Answers:** $ATP_1 = 75$ units, $ATP_3 = 105$ units.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a) Explain the position of Master Scheduling in the production planning hierarchy. How does MPS differ from a forecast? (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Position in Hierarchy (4 Marks):**
The Master Production Schedule sits between the aggregate Sales & Operations Plan (S&OP) and the detailed Material Requirements Planning (MRP). The hierarchy is:

1. Business Plan (long range)
2. Sales & Operations Plan (medium range)
3. **Master Production Schedule** ← detailed end-item plan
4. Material Requirements Plan (component-level plan)
5. Shop Floor Schedule (hour-by-hour execution)

The MPS translates the S&OP into specific end-item production quantities and timing.

**MPS vs Forecast (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Forecast | MPS |
|--------|----------|-----|
| Nature | Statistical estimate | Commitment |
| Subject | Demand projection | Production plan |
| Responsibility | Marketing/Sales | Master Scheduler |
| Stability | Continuously updated | Frozen inside DTF |
| Purpose | Input to MPS | Drives MRP & shop floor |

---

**(b) The following data relates to a manufacturing firm. Beginning inventory = 40 units, Safety stock = 30 units. Prepare the MPS table and identify any weeks requiring action. (7 Marks)**

| Week | Forecast | Actual Orders |
|------|----------|---------------|
| 1    | 60       | 55            |
| 2    | 60       | 70            |
| 3    | 60       | 50            |
| 4    | 60       | 65            |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Gross Requirements (use Actual Orders when ≥ Forecast):**

Week 1: 60, Week 2: 70, Week 3: 60, Week 4: 65.

**Step 2 — Compute PAB Week-by-Week (assume lot-for-lot MPS):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PAB_1 &= 40 + 60 - 60 = 40 \\
PAB_2 &= 40 + 70 - 70 = 40 \\
PAB_3 &= 40 + 60 - 60 = 40 \\
PAB_4 &= 40 + 65 - 65 = 40
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — MPS Table:**

| Week | Gross Req. | Beginning Inv. | Production | Ending Inv. | Safety Stock Met? |
|------|------------|----------------|------------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1    | 60         | 40             | 60         | 40          | Yes (≥ 30)        |
| 2    | 70         | 40             | 70         | 40          | Yes               |
| 3    | 60         | 40             | 60         | 40          | Yes               |
| 4    | 65         | 40             | 65         | 40          | Yes               |

**Step 4 — Action Required:**
No weeks require action because ending inventory stays at or above safety stock. The MPS is feasible.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Confusing forecast with orders** — Always use the *greater* of forecast and actual orders as gross requirements.
> 2. **Forgetting negative PAB** — Negative projected balance indicates stock-out; MPS must be revised.
> 3. **ATP formula errors** — ATP uses *next* production, not current period. Read the problem carefully.
> 4. **Time Fence naming** — Many students confuse Demand Time Fence with Planning Time Fence. Remember: DTF = inside = frozen.
> 5. **Lot size oversight** — Always mention if lot-for-lot or fixed quantity is used when computing production.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

**Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **MPS = time-phased production plan** for specific end items, not aggregate.
- ✅ **MPS is a commitment, not a forecast** — frozen inside Demand Time Fence.
- ✅ **DTF = cumulative lead time** of the end item.
- ✅ **Planning horizon** = 12-18 months, typically.
- ✅ **Time Fences** = Frozen (DTF), Slushy (PTF), Liquid (beyond PTF).
- ✅ **ATP = Production − Committed Orders until next production**.
- ✅ **PAB Formula**: $PAB_t = PAB_{t-1} + R_t - D_t$.
- ✅ **Gross Requirements** = max(Forecast, Actual Orders).
- ✅ **Safety Stock** must be maintained in every time bucket.
- ✅ **RCCP** checks capacity feasibility of MPS before freezing.
- ✅ **MPS drives MRP** — output is planned order releases for components.
- ✅ **ATP is dynamically recalculated** each time a customer order arrives.

**Key Numerical Conventions for KTU Exams:**
- Time expressed in **weeks** (most common).
- Quantities in **units/pieces**.
- Production quantities rounded to **lot-size multiples**.
- Negative PAB signals **infeasibility**.

**Memory Aid — "MR. FAT-C":**
- **M**aster schedule
- **R**eleases orders
- **F**orecast-driven
- **A**TP-protected
- **T**ime-fenced
- **C**apacity-checked

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inventory Management: Functions of Inventory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET601 - OPERATIONS RESEARCH AND PRODUCTION MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Inventory Management: Functions of Inventory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Inventory

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** framework (PCPET601 — Operations Research & Production Management), **Inventory** is formally defined as the stock of raw materials, components, work-in-process, finished goods, and other supplies held by an organization at any point in time to meet future demand and to support the smooth functioning of the production–distribution system.

Mathematically, inventory at any instant $t$ can be represented as:

$$
I(t) \;=\; I_0 \;+\; \int_{0}^{t} R(\tau)\,d\tau \;-\; \int_{0}^{t} D(\tau)\,d\tau
$$

Where:

- $I(t)$ = Inventory level at time $t$
- $I_0$ = Initial inventory at time zero
- $R(\tau)$ = Replenishment (inward) rate at time $\tau$
- $D(\tau)$ = Demand (outward) rate at time $\tau$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** Whenever a question asks "Define inventory," never write only "stock of goods." Always include **all four classical categories** (raw materials, WIP, finished goods, MRO) and tie it to the **time-gap between supply and demand**.

## 1.2 What Does "Functions of Inventory" Mean?

The word **"Functions"** here is NOT the mathematical sense. In the KTU Operations Management syllabus, it refers to the **purpose, role, or rationale** for which a firm deliberately chooses to hold inventory. In other words: *Why does a rational manager deliberately keep stocks when holding them costs money?*

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🍚

Imagine you run a small Kerala "Hotel" (restaurant). Every morning you estimate how many customers will arrive. But demand is unpredictable — some days you sell 50 dosas, some days 100.

If you keep **zero** rice-batter (inventory), you will run out on peak days and lose customers. If you keep **too much**, it spoils and you lose money. So you keep an **optimum stock** — that optimum stock has specific **jobs (functions)** it performs:

- It **saves you** when demand suddenly spikes.
- It **allows you** to cook in big batches economically.
- It **protects you** when your rice supplier is late.

In management language, these jobs are called the **Functions of Inventory**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU specifically tests whether a student can *list* the functions AND *explain with an industrial example* why each function is necessary. Pure listing without rationale fetches only **partial marks** (typically 1 out of 3 in Part A).

## 1.4 The 4 Physical Forms of Inventory (Mandatory to State)

| # | Inventory Type | Physical Form | Example |
|---|----------------|---------------|---------|
| 1 | **Raw Materials** | Inputs not yet processed | Steel coils, fabric rolls |
| 2 | **Work-in-Progress (WIP)** | Partially completed goods | Half-assembled car on a conveyor |
| 3 | **Finished Goods** | Completed goods awaiting sale | Packed laptops in warehouse |
| 4 | **MRO Supplies** | Maintenance, Repair, Operating items | Lubricants, gloves, screws |

These four forms exist *because* inventory performs multiple **functions**. Each function is tied to one or more of these forms.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📗 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 8 Major Functions of Inventory (KTU-Syllabus-Authoritative List)

The KTU 2024 syllabus (under Module 4 — Inventory Management) categorises the functions of inventory into **eight clearly distinguishable roles**. Each is explained below with its *industrial logic*:

### F1 — Decoupling Function (Buffer Between Stages)
If machine A feeds machine B, but A breaks down, B will starve. Placing a small inventory buffer between them **decouples** the two stages so that B can continue working for some time even if A stops.

> *Example:* A car assembly line keeps a 2-day buffer of dashboards between the dashboard sub-line and the main line.

### F2 — Meeting Demand (Satisfying Customer Demand)
This is the **most fundamental** function. Inventory exists so that customer orders can be fulfilled **on demand** without waiting for a fresh production cycle (which may take weeks).

### F3 — Buffering Against Demand Uncertainty (Safety Stock Function)
Since actual demand is never exactly equal to forecast demand, inventory acts as a **safety cushion** to absorb forecasting errors.

### F4 — Buffering Against Supply Uncertainty (Lead-Time Safety)
Suppliers may deliver late. Inventory held to cover **supplier lead time variability** is part of this function.

### F5 — Economies of Scale (Lot-Size Function)
Producing or ordering in **large batches** reduces per-unit ordering/setup cost. The larger batch creates **cycle stock** — inventory held purely because it is cheaper to do so.

The classic Economic Order Quantity formula expresses this:

$$
\text{EOQ} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2 \, D \, C_o}{C_h}}
$$

Where:
- $D$ = Annual demand (units/year)
- $C_o$ = Ordering cost per order (₹/order)
- $C_h$ = Holding cost per unit per year (₹/unit/year)

### F6 — Smoothing Production (Hedge Against Variable Production Rate)
Producing at a constant rate while demand fluctuates seasonally requires **anticipation inventory** — built up in off-season and consumed in peak-season.

### F7 — Hedging Against Price Changes (Speculative Function)
If the price of copper is expected to rise, the firm buys and stores extra copper. This **speculative inventory** protects against inflation, scarcity, or supplier price hikes.

### F8 — Pipeline / In-Transit Function
Goods physically moving between locations (factory → warehouse → retailer) are inventory. Even though not stored, they **belong to the firm** and represent tied-up capital.

$$
\text{Pipeline Inventory} \;=\; D_{\text{daily}} \times T_{\text{transit}}
$$

Where $T_{\text{transit}}$ is the average transit time in days.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Marking Pattern:** A 14-mark question on "Functions of Inventory" almost always awards **2 marks for each function** with an example. Listing 8 functions without example = ~6 marks. Listing 8 functions with example = full marks. **Never skip the example.**

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Purpose / When Used |
|-------------------|-------------------|--------------------|
| Inventory at time $t$ | $I(t) = I_0 + \int R\,d\tau - \int D\,d\tau$ | Models stock level over time |
| Economic Order Quantity | $\text{EOQ} = \sqrt{2DC_o / C_h}$ | Optimal order size (lot-size function) |
| Reorder Point | $\text{ROP} = d \times L + SS$ | When to place next order |
| Safety Stock | $SS = z \cdot \sigma_{dL}$ | Buffer against uncertainty |
| Pipeline Inventory | $P = D_{\text{daily}} \cdot T_{\text{transit}}$ | In-transit stock |
| Average Inventory (saw-tooth) | $I_{\text{avg}} = Q / 2$ | EOQ scenario |
| Total Inventory Cost | $TC = \frac{D}{Q}C_o + \frac{Q}{2}C_h$ | Minimising point → EOQ |

> **Note:** In the above table, vertical bars have been deliberately replaced with the textual form (e.g., "EOQ =") to comply with the markdown-table integrity rule. For inline math, use $\vert \cdot \vert$ or $\mid \cdot \mid$ notation.

## 2.3 Why These Functions Matter in Real Engineering Industries

- **Automotive (e.g., Maruti Suzuki):** Decoupling buffers between stamping, painting, and assembly lines prevent a single failure from halting the entire plant.
- **Aerospace (e.g., HAL):** Safety stock of mission-critical parts is non-negotiable because supplier lead-times are 6–12 months.
- **FMCG (e.g., Amul):** Finished-goods inventory in cold-storage allows the company to absorb seasonal milk-supply fluctuations.
- **Electronics (e.g., Apple):** Speculative inventory of microchips protected Apple during the 2020–22 semiconductor shortage.

## 2.4 Logical Flow Diagram (Conceptual Map of Functions)

The functions of inventory are **interdependent**, not isolated. They form a pyramid with **"meeting demand"** as the apex and the more specialised functions as supporting layers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📙 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Why Average Cycle Inventory Equals Q/2

In the classical **EOQ saw-tooth inventory profile**, each order of quantity $Q$ arrives instantaneously, and demand depletes it linearly until the next replenishment.

### Step-by-Step Logic

Let:
- $Q$ = order quantity (units per cycle)
- Demand rate be constant at $D$ units/year

The cycle time (time between two successive orders) is:

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} \;=\; \frac{Q}{D} \quad \text{(years per cycle)}
$$

Inventory at the **start** of cycle: $I = Q$.
Inventory at the **end** of cycle: $I = 0$.
Inventory decreases **linearly**, so the average value over one cycle is the arithmetic mean of the maximum and minimum:

$$
I_{\text{avg}} \;=\; \frac{Q + 0}{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{I_{\text{avg}} \;=\; \frac{Q}{2}}
$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** Holding cost per year = $I_{\text{avg}} \times C_h = (Q/2) \, C_h$. This is one of the two terms in the EOQ total-cost minimisation problem.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Reorder Point (ROP) with Safety Stock

Suppose:
- Average daily demand = $d$ units/day
- Average lead time = $L$ days
- Standard deviation of demand during lead time = $\sigma_{dL}$
- Desired service level corresponds to a $z$-score from standard normal table

Demand during lead time (DDLT) is random with mean $dL$. To protect against unexpected surges, we add **safety stock**:

$$
SS \;=\; z \cdot \sigma_{dL}
$$

The **Reorder Point** is therefore:

$$
\text{ROP} \;=\; (d \cdot L) \;+\; SS
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{ROP} \;=\; dL \;+\; z\sigma_{dL}}
$$

### Numerical Illustration (KTU-style 3-mark sub-part)

> A retailer sells 50 units/day, lead time is 4 days, $\sigma_{dL} = 30$ units, and the desired service level is 95% ($z = 1.65$). Find the ROP.

**Solution:**

$$
dL \;=\; 50 \times 4 \;=\; 200 \text{ units}
$$

$$
SS \;=\; 1.65 \times 30 \;=\; 49.5 \text{ units}
$$

$$
\text{ROP} \;=\; 200 + 49.5 \;=\; 249.5 \approx 250 \text{ units}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pipeline Inventory Verification

A truck carrying goods travels from Coimbatore to Kochi. Travel time = 3 days. Daily demand at receiving warehouse = 200 units.

At any instant, **all 3 days of demand** is sitting inside the truck:

$$
P \;=\; D_{\text{daily}} \times T_{\text{transit}}
$$

$$
P \;=\; 200 \times 3 \;=\; 600 \text{ units}
$$

This 600 units is **inventory owned by the firm**, even though it is not in any warehouse. KTU exam questions frequently use this fact to trick students into reporting inventory = 0.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Function vs Cost Implication

| Function | Inventory Type Created | Primary Cost Incurred | Managerial Trade-off |
|----------|------------------------|-----------------------|----------------------|
| Decoupling | Buffer stock | Carrying cost | More decoupling = more capital tied up |
| Meeting demand | Finished goods | Carrying cost + obsolescence | Under-stocking = lost sales |
| Demand uncertainty | Safety stock | Carrying cost | Higher service level → higher $z$ → more $SS$ |
| Supply uncertainty | Safety stock | Carrying cost | Longer/variable lead time → more $SS$ |
| Economies of scale | Cycle stock | Carrying cost | Large $Q$ reduces ordering cost but increases holding cost |
| Smoothing production | Anticipation stock | Carrying + obsolescence | Allows level production, but risks spoilage |
| Hedging price | Speculative stock | Carrying + price risk | Protects vs inflation, but capital locked |
| Pipeline | In-transit | In-transit insurance + financing | Cannot be eliminated, only reduced by faster logistics |

## 3.5 Decision Rule for KTU Numerical Problems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Rule:** If a question mentions "lot-sizing," use **EOQ**. If it mentions "service level" or "stockout," use **Safety Stock + ROP**. If it mentions "transit" or "pipeline," use $D_{\text{daily}} \cdot T_{\text{transit}}$. Mixing these is the **#1 reason students lose marks** in PCPET601 ESE.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📕 SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Block Diagram — Functions of Inventory

The following Mermaid block-diagram maps the **functions** (input rationale) to the **types** of inventory (output stock forms), and then to the **industrial beneficiary**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FUNCTIONS OF INVENTORY] --> B[Decoupling Function]
    A --> C[Demand Satisfaction]
    A --> D[Safety Against Uncertainty]
    A --> E[Economies of Scale]
    A --> F[Smoothing Production]
    A --> G[Hedging Price]
    A --> H[Pipeline In Transit]

    B --> I[Buffer Stock Between Stages]
    C --> J[Finished Goods Inventory]
    D --> K[Safety Stock]
    E --> L[Cycle Stock EOQ]
    F --> M[Anticipation Stock]
    G --> N[Speculative Stock]
    H --> O[In Transit Inventory]

    I --> P[Industrial Beneficiary Assembly Lines]
    J --> P
    K --> P
    L --> P
    M --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
```

**Reading the diagram:** Start at top — every function has a corresponding *physical form* of inventory. The aggregated stock supports the production–distribution pipeline of the firm.

## 4.2 Process Flow — Inventory Level Over Time (Saw-Tooth)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Order Placed Q units] --> S2[Inventory Jumps to Q]
    S2 --> S3[Demand Withdraws Stock Daily]
    S3 --> S4[Inventory Reaches ROP]
    S4 --> S5[Reorder Triggered]
    S5 --> S1
```

This is the **saw-tooth pattern** that all EOQ models assume. The visible area under the curve is the **average inventory = Q/2**, which is why holding cost is calculated on $Q/2$.

## 4.3 Block Architecture — Managerial Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Raw Materials] --> R2[WIP]
    R2 --> R3[Finished Goods]
    R3 --> R4[Customer]
    R1 -.- F1[Decoupling]
    R2 -.- F2[Buffer Uncertainty]
    R3 -.- F3[Meet Demand]
    R1 -.- F4[Speculative Hedging]
    R3 -.- F5[Anticipation Seasonal]
    R3 -.- F6[In Transit Pipeline]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each stage of the value chain (Raw → WIP → Finished → Customer) is governed by specific inventory functions. Students should be able to match the function to the stage during the exam.

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagrams here are **schematic architecture maps**, not physical drawings. They are intentionally designed to be exam-friendly and reproducible.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📒 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below strictly follow the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern**: Part A = 3 marks, Part B = 14 marks with internal choice. Bloom's levels (Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyse) are mapped. Model answers are exhaustive.

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]
**"List any six functions of inventory. Why is each function necessary in a manufacturing firm?"**

### **Model Answer (3 marks):**

The six major functions of inventory are:

1. **Decoupling Function** — Allows independent operation of sequential stages. *Example:* Buffer between machining and assembly.
2. **Meeting Demand** — Ensures finished goods are available when customers order. *Example:* Amazon keeps items in fulfilment centres.
3. **Buffering Uncertainty** — Safety stock cushions against forecast errors. *Example:* Pharmaceutical firms hold stock against demand spikes.
4. **Economies of Scale** — Allows production/ordering in economic lot sizes. *Example:* EOQ-based ordering in a steel plant.
5. **Hedging Price** — Protects against anticipated price increases. *Example:* Oil companies stockpile crude before OPEC hikes.
6. **Pipeline Function** — Goods in transit are inventory. *Example:* Coal being shipped from mine to power plant.

*(2 marks for listing + 1 mark for the "why" rationale — typical KTU scheme.)*

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]
**"Distinguish between cycle stock and safety stock. Why are both necessary?"**

### **Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Cycle Stock | Safety Stock |
|--------|-------------|--------------|
| Purpose | Arises from EOQ lot-sizing | Cushions against uncertainty |
| Triggered by | Economies of scale in ordering | Forecast errors + lead-time variability |
| Formula | $Q/2$ on average | $z \cdot \sigma_{dL}$ |
| Cost implication | Reduces ordering cost | Increases carrying cost |

**Why both?** Cycle stock alone meets *average* demand. Safety stock handles *deviations*. Without safety stock, stockouts occur. Without cycle stock, ordering cost becomes prohibitive. *(1 mark for distinction + 1 mark for "why" + 1 mark for example.)*

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice Provided)

### **QUESTION A — (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]**

**"Inventory is not a sign of inefficiency but a vital operational resource. Critically analyse the functions of inventory in a manufacturing organisation with reference to: (a) decoupling and lot-sizing functions, and (b) safety stock and pipeline functions."**

#### **Part (a) — Decoupling and Lot-Sizing Functions (7 marks)**

**Decoupling Function (3.5 marks):**

Decoupling means placing **buffer inventory between successive stages of production** so that the downtime of one stage does not halt the entire system.

- *Industrial example:* In a car assembly plant, the welding, painting, and final-assembly lines operate at different speeds. A buffer of 200 dashboards between paint and assembly allows paint to continue even if assembly stops.
- *Cost implication:* Carrying cost increases linearly with buffer size. Decision rule: place buffer where the cost of stoppage is highest.

**Lot-Sizing Function (3.5 marks):**

Lot-sizing exploits the **trade-off between ordering cost and carrying cost**. The EOQ formula gives the optimal quantity:

$$
\text{EOQ} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2DC_o}{C_h}}
$$

*Numerical example for full marks:*
A firm uses 10,000 units/year, ordering cost = ₹200/order, holding cost = ₹10/unit/year. Find EOQ and total cost.

$$
Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10000 \times 200}{10}} = \sqrt{400000} = 632.46 \text{ units}
$$

$$
\text{Ordering cost} = \frac{10000}{632.46} \times 200 \approx 3162 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
\text{Holding cost} = \frac{632.46}{2} \times 10 \approx 3162 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
\text{Total inventory cost} \approx 6324 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

---

#### **Part (b) — Safety Stock and Pipeline Functions (7 marks)**

**Safety Stock Function (3.5 marks):**

Safety stock is held to **protect against forecast errors and supplier delays**. The reorder point is:

$$
\text{ROP} \;=\; dL + z\sigma_{dL}
$$

*Numerical example:* $d = 50$ units/day, $L = 4$ days, $\sigma_{dL} = 30$, $z = 1.65$ (95% service).

$$
\text{ROP} = 50 \times 4 + 1.65 \times 30 = 200 + 49.5 = 249.5 \approx 250 \text{ units}
$$

**Pipeline Function (3.5 marks):**

Pipeline inventory is stock **in transit** — owned by the firm but not in any warehouse.

$$
P = D_{\text{daily}} \times T_{\text{transit}}
$$

*Example:* 200 units/day consumed at destination, transit = 3 days ⇒ $P = 600$ units. This represents working capital locked in transit.

**Conclusion (synthesis for full marks):** Decoupling + lot-sizing are *cost-driven* functions; safety stock + pipeline are *uncertainty-driven* functions. Together they justify inventory holding in any rational manufacturing system.

---

### **QUESTION B — (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**

**"Discuss the role of inventory as a hedge, smoother, and decoupler. How do these functions influence working capital and supply chain resilience in a manufacturing firm?"**

#### **Part (a) — Inventory as a Hedge (7 marks)**

The hedging (speculative) function involves **deliberate stockpiling to protect against expected price increases or supply scarcity**.

- *Example 1:* An FMCG firm buys extra palm oil when futures indicate a 15% price hike — saves ₹50 lakh in 6 months.
- *Example 2:* Government of India maintains strategic petroleum reserves — pure hedging inventory.
- *Working-capital impact:* Locked capital = $P_{\text{purchase}} \times Q_{\text{extra}}$.
- *Risk:* If price falls, firm bears a loss. KTU expects students to mention **this downside explicitly**.

#### **Part (b) — Inventory as a Smoother and Decoupler (7 marks)**

**Smoothing (Anticipation) Function:** Inventory built up in off-season to meet peak-season demand.

- *Example:* AC manufacturer producing in winter for summer demand.
- *Production rate:* Constant; demand rate: seasonal. Inventory differential = production − demand.

$$
I_{\text{anticipation}}(t) = \int_{0}^{t} [P(\tau) - D(\tau)]\, d\tau
$$

**Decoupling Function:** Already discussed in Question A.

- *Example:* Semiconductor fab decoupling photolithography from etching via WIP buffer of 500 wafers.

**Conclusion (synthesis):** Hedge + Smoother + Decoupler are the three strategic functions. Hedge protects against external price shocks; smoother handles internal seasonal cycles; decoupler protects against internal stoppages. Together they define the **resilience profile** of a supply chain.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Traps**
>
> 1. **Listing without example:** In Part A, students list 6 functions but write no industrial example → lose 1 mark automatically.
> 2. **Forgetting pipeline inventory:** Students report firm inventory = finished goods only → lose 1–2 marks because in-transit stock is *also* inventory.
> 3. **Mixing up cycle stock and safety stock:** Cycle stock comes from EOQ; safety stock comes from $z\sigma_{dL}$. Confusing the formulas is the #1 numerical error.
> 4. **Writing EOQ without units:** Always write $\text{units}$ or $\text{kg}$ after EOQ value. Missing units = -0.5 marks.
> 5. **Not mentioning the trade-off:** Holding inventory always involves the *trade-off between service level and cost*. A 14-mark answer without this trade-off discussion is considered incomplete.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Inventory** = stock of raw materials + WIP + finished goods + MRO supplies.
- ✅ **Eight major functions:** decoupling, meeting demand, demand-uncertainty buffer, supply-uncertainty buffer, economies of scale (lot-size), smoothing (anticipation), hedging (speculative), pipeline (in-transit).
- ✅ **Cycle stock** arises from EOQ lot-sizing; **Safety stock** arises from uncertainty.
- ✅ **Average cycle inventory = $Q/2$** under constant demand and instantaneous replenishment.
- ✅ **Reorder Point = $dL + z\sigma_{dL}$** — always state the service-level interpretation of $z$.
- ✅ **Pipeline inventory = $D_{\text{daily}} \times T_{\text{transit}}$** — do NOT ignore in-transit stock.
- ✅ **Every function has a cost implication** — always discuss trade-off between **service level** and **carrying cost** in 14-mark answers.
- ✅ **Use examples** — Kerala-based (Coir, Spices, Marine) or global (Apple, Maruti, Amul) examples fetch extra marks.
- ✅ **KTU weightage:** This topic typically carries 8–12 marks in ESE and 3–5 marks in internal assessments.
- ✅ **Don't confuse:** Functions of inventory (Module 4 part 1) ≠ Types/costs of inventory (Module 4 part 2) ≠ EOQ models (Module 4 part 3). Each part requires different treatment.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inventory Problem Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET601 - OPERATIONS RESEARCH AND PRODUCTION MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Inventory Problem Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inventory Problem Classification** is the systematic categorization of inventory control problems based on the nature of demand, lead time, review policy, number of items, replenishment rate, and the allowance of shortages. This classification enables the selection of an appropriate mathematical model that minimizes the **Total Inventory Cost (TIC)** while satisfying service level constraints.

In Operations Research (OR), an **inventory** refers to the stock of raw materials, work-in-progress (WIP), finished goods, or MRO (Maintenance, Repair, and Operating) items held by an organization at any point in time. An **inventory problem** arises when a decision-maker must determine:
- **How much** to order (Order Quantity, $Q$)
- **When** to order (Reorder Point, $ROP$)
- **How much** safety stock to maintain

The classification of these problems is the **first and most critical step** in inventory modeling, as the choice of classification directly dictates the governing equations, assumptions, and solution methodology.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a Tea Stall in Kerala ☕**
>
> Imagine a small tea stall owner in Thrissur who stocks sugar, milk, tea powder, and glasses. He faces a different "inventory problem" for each item:
> - **Sugar** — bought in bulk, used steadily, refilled quickly → *Deterministic, Single Item, No Shortage*
> - **Milk** — spoils if not used in a day → *Perishable Item, Frequent Review*
> - **Special Onam Combo Meals** — demand is uncertain → *Probabilistic Model*
> - **Tea + Coffee + Snacks** together — limited fridge space → *Multi-Item with Storage Constraint*
>
> Just as the tea stall cannot use the same restocking rule for milk and sugar, a factory cannot use the same model for screws and for imported electronic chips. **Classification is the art of mapping the right tool to the right problem.**

## 1.3 Why Classification Matters in KTU Examinations

In KTU's End Semester Examinations (ESE), a common pitfall is **jumping directly to formula application** without first identifying the problem class. The 2024 Scheme expects students to:
1. **Identify** the classification criteria present in the question stem.
2. **Map** the problem to the correct model family.
3. **Apply** the corresponding formula.
4. **Interpret** the result in business terms.

> [!TIP]
> **Always underline the classification keywords** in the question (e.g., "constant demand", "instantaneous replenishment", "shortages not permitted") — these are the examiner's hidden roadmap.

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (Bolded for Emphasis)

The following metrics are universally used across all inventory classifications:

- **$D$** = Annual demand (units/year) — deterministic or expected value
- **$Q$** = Order quantity (units/order)
- **$S$** or **$C_o$** = Ordering cost per order (Rs./order)
- **$H$** or **$C_h$** = Holding/carrying cost per unit per year (Rs./unit/year)
- **$L$** = Lead time (days or weeks)
- **$TCU$** = Total Cost per Unit time (Rs./year)
- **$N$** = Number of orders per year
- **$T$** = Cycle time (time between successive orders)
- **$SS$** = Safety stock (units)
- **$ROP$** = Reorder point (units)
- **$SS\%$** = Service level percentage
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Primary Classification Criteria

Inventory problems are classified along **six independent dimensions**. Each problem is mapped onto each dimension simultaneously, giving a unique "fingerprint" for model selection.

### 2.1.1 Classification by Demand Pattern

| Sub-Class | Description | Typical Model |
|---|---|---|
| **Deterministic – Static** | Demand is constant and known ($D = $ constant) | Classical EOQ (Wilson) Model |
| **Deterministic – Dynamic** | Demand varies with time, $D(t) = a + bt$ | Wagner-Whitin / Lot Sizing Model |
| **Probabilistic – Discrete** | Demand follows a discrete distribution (Poisson) | Newsboy / Single-Period Model |
| **Probabilistic – Continuous** | Demand follows a continuous distribution (Normal) | $(s, S)$ or $(Q, R)$ Model |

### 2.1.2 Classification by Lead Time

- **Deterministic Lead Time** — known with certainty; $ROP = \bar{d} \cdot L$
- **Stochastic Lead Time** — random variable; requires **safety lead time** or **safety stock**

### 2.1.3 Classification by Review Policy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most frequently tested classification** in KTU Module 4.

**A. Continuous Review System (Q-System or $(Q, R)$ System)**
- Inventory is monitored **continuously** (every transaction).
- When stock falls to reorder point $R$, an order of fixed size $Q$ is placed.
- Used in **high-value, critical items** (e.g., gold, medicines).

**B. Periodic Review System (P-System or $(s, S)$ System)**
- Inventory is reviewed at **fixed intervals** (weekly, monthly).
- Order quantity varies to raise stock up to level $S$.
- Used when **joint ordering** of multiple items is economical.

### 2.1.4 Classification by Number of Items

- **Single-Item Model** — decision for one SKU
- **Multi-Item Model with Storage Constraint** — limited warehouse space
- **Multi-Item Model with Budget Constraint** — limited working capital

### 2.1.5 Classification by Replenishment

- **Instantaneous Replenishment** — entire lot arrives at once (purchase model)
- **Finite Replenishment Rate** — production lot arrives gradually (Production Order Quantity / EPQ)

### 2.1.6 Classification by Shortage Handling

- **No Shortages Allowed** — must maintain stock at all times
- **Backorders Permitted** — unmet demand is fulfilled later
- **Lost Sales** — unmet demand is permanently lost

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute values and conditional expressions use $\vert$ instead of the pipe character to preserve table syntax.

| # | Model Class | Optimal Order Quantity $Q^*$ | Reorder Point $ROP$ | Total Cost $TCU$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Classical EOQ (No Shortage, Instantaneous)** | $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | $R = dL$ | $TCU = \sqrt{2DSH}$ |
| 2 | **Production Order Quantity (EPQ, Finite Rate $p > d$)** | $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{p}{p-d}}$ | $R = dL$ | $TCU = \sqrt{2DSH \cdot (1 - d/p)}$ |
| 3 | **EOQ with Backorders (Shortage Cost $C_s$)** | $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{H + C_s}{C_s}}$ | $R = dL$ | Includes backorder cost term |
| 4 | **Quantity Discount (All-Units)** | Find price break, compare $TCU$ | $R = dL$ | $TCU = D \cdot P + \text{order+hold}$ |
| 5 | **Probabilistic (Normal Demand)** | $Q = \bar{D} \cdot T + z \sigma_L \cdot \sqrt{T}$ | $R = \bar{d} \cdot L + z \sigma_L$ | Includes expected shortage cost |
| 6 | **Periodic Review $(s, S)$** | Order up to $S$ | Order at $s$ | $S = \bar{d}(T+L) + z\sigma_{T+L}$ |
| 7 | **Multi-Item with Space** | $Q_i^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2D_i S}{i \cdot H_i}}$ | Subject to $\sum a_i Q_i \leq A$ | Lagrangian solution |

Where:
- $d$ = average daily demand
- $L$ = lead time
- $\sigma_L$ = standard deviation of demand during lead time
- $z$ = standard normal variate for service level
- $p$ = production rate
- $C_s$ = shortage cost per unit per year
- $P$ = unit price

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Classification — Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Typical Classification Used |
|---|---|
| **Automotive Assembly** | Multi-item, finite rate, no shortage (JIT) |
| **Pharma Cold Chain** | Perishable, continuous review, probabilistic demand |
| **E-commerce Warehousing (Amazon)** | Multi-echelon, periodic review, $(s, S)$ policy |
| **Defense Spares** | Deterministic, no shortage, high $C_s$ |
| **Fashion Retail** | Single-period, probabilistic, salvage value considered |
| **Steel Manufacturing** | EPQ with finite production rate, backorders allowed |

> [!TIP]
> In KTU 14-mark questions, always **state the classification first**, then derive or apply the formula. Examiners award 1–2 marks for correct classification identification.

## 2.4 Decision Tree for Problem Classification (The KTU Compass)

Use this mental flowchart whenever you read a question:

1. Is demand **constant** or **variable**? → Determines Model Family 1, 2, 3 vs. Wagner-Whitin
2. Is replenishment **instantaneous** or **gradual**? → Determines EOQ vs. EPQ
3. Are **shortages permitted**? → Determines standard EOQ vs. backorder model
4. Is the item **single** or **multi**? → Determines single-variable optimization vs. Lagrangian
5. Is demand **certain** or **random**? → Determines deterministic vs. safety stock formula
6. Is review **continuous** or **periodic**? → Determines $Q$-system vs. $P$-system
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Classical EOQ (Wilson's Formula)

This is the **fundamental derivation** for the most basic classification: **Deterministic, Constant Demand, Instantaneous Replenishment, No Shortages, Single Item**.

### 3.1.1 Assumptions

1. Demand rate is constant: $D$ units per year.
2. Lead time is zero (or constant).
3. Order is received instantaneously.
4. No shortages are permitted.
5. Ordering cost $S$ is fixed per order.
6. Holding cost $H$ is constant per unit per year.
7. Unit purchase price $P$ is constant.

### 3.1.2 Cost Components

**Ordering Cost per year:**
$$OC = \frac{D}{Q} \cdot S$$

Number of orders per year is $D/Q$, and each costs $S$.

**Holding Cost per year:**
$$HC = \frac{Q}{2} \cdot H$$

The average inventory between two orders is $Q/2$ (triangular inventory profile: starts at $Q$, ends at 0).

**Total Cost per year (excluding purchase cost):**
$$TCU(Q) = \frac{DS}{Q} + \frac{HQ}{2}$$

### 3.1.3 Minimization — Calculus Approach

Take the first derivative of $TCU$ with respect to $Q$ and equate to zero:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d(TCU)}{dQ} &= -\frac{DS}{Q^2} + \frac{H}{2} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving for $Q$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{DS}{Q^2} &= \frac{H}{2} \\[4pt]
Q^2 &= \frac{2DS}{H} \\[4pt]
Q^* &= \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.4 Second-Order Verification (Concavity Check)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d^2(TCU)}{dQ^2} &= \frac{2DS}{Q^3} > 0
\end{aligned}
$$

The second derivative is **positive**, confirming that $TCU$ is **convex** and $Q^*$ is indeed a **minimum**.

### 3.1.5 Minimum Total Cost

Substitute $Q^*$ back into $TCU$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
TCU^* &= \frac{DS}{Q^*} + \frac{HQ^*}{2} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{DS}{\sqrt{2DS/H}} + \frac{H \cdot \sqrt{2DS/H}}{2} \\[4pt]
&= \sqrt{\frac{DS^2 \cdot H}{2DS}} + \frac{1}{2} \cdot \sqrt{2DSH} \\[4pt]
&= \sqrt{\frac{SH}{2}} + \sqrt{\frac{DSH}{2}} \\[4pt]
&= \sqrt{2DSH}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore: $\boxed{TCU_{\min} = \sqrt{2DSH}}$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Economic Production Quantity (EPQ)

Classification: **Deterministic demand, Finite Production Rate $p > d$, No Shortages**.

Here, the inventory builds up gradually during production at rate $(p - d)$ and depletes at rate $d$ during non-production.

### 3.2.1 Maximum and Average Inventory

The production run time is $t_1 = Q/p$.

Maximum inventory occurs at $t_1$:
$$I_{max} = (p - d) \cdot \frac{Q}{p} = Q \cdot \left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right)$$

Average inventory:
$$I_{avg} = \frac{I_{max}}{2} = \frac{Q}{2} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right)$$

### 3.2.2 Total Cost

$$
\begin{aligned}
TCU(Q) &= \frac{DS}{Q} + \frac{HQ}{2} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.3 Minimization

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d(TCU)}{dQ} &= -\frac{DS}{Q^2} + \frac{H}{2}\left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right) = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q^2 &= \frac{2DS}{H \cdot (1 - d/p)} \\[4pt]
Q^* &= \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{p}{p-d}}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation of EOQ with Backorders

Classification: **Deterministic, Backorders Permitted, Shortage Cost $C_s$**.

Let $S_{max}$ be the maximum backorder level. The inventory profile has two phases: from $-S_{max}$ to $0$ (backorder being filled), then to $I_{max} = Q - S_{max}$.

### 3.3.1 Cost Components

**Ordering Cost:** $DS/Q$ (unchanged)

**Holding Cost:** Applies only to positive inventory portion:
$$HC = \frac{H(Q - S_{max})^2}{2Q}$$

**Shortage Cost:** Applies to backorder portion:
$$SC = \frac{C_s \cdot S_{max}^2}{2Q}$$

### 3.3.2 Total Cost

$$
\begin{aligned}
TCU(Q, S_{max}) &= \frac{DS}{Q} + \frac{H(Q - S_{max})^2}{2Q} + \frac{C_s S_{max}^2}{2Q}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3.3 Partial Derivatives (Two Variables)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial TCU}{\partial Q} &= -\frac{DS}{Q^2} + \frac{H(Q-S_{max})}{Q^2} \cdot \left[\frac{Q - (Q-S_{max})}{Q}\right] + \frac{C_s S_{max}^2}{2Q^2} \cdot (-1) \cdot \dots \\[4pt]
\text{(After simplification using standard textbook manipulation):} \\[4pt]
Q^* &= \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{H + C_s}{C_s}} \\[4pt]
S_{max} &= Q^* \cdot \frac{H}{H + C_s}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Multi-Part Problem

> **Problem Statement (typical KTU-style):**
> A company uses **12,000 units** of a raw material annually. The **ordering cost is Rs. 200 per order** and the **holding cost is Rs. 5 per unit per year**. Lead time is **30 days** and the company operates **300 days per year**. Calculate:
> (i) Economic Order Quantity
> (ii) Number of orders per year
> (iii) Time between orders
> (iv) Total inventory cost per year
> (v) Reorder point

**Given:**
- $D = 12{,}000$ units/year
- $S = $ Rs. 200/order
- $H = $ Rs. 5/unit/year
- $L = 30$ days
- Working days = 300/year

**Step (i): EOQ**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q^* &= \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 12000 \times 200}{5}} = \sqrt{960000} = 979.8 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (ii): Number of Orders**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{D}{Q^*} = \frac{12000}{979.8} = 12.247 \text{ orders/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (iii): Time Between Orders**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{300 \text{ days}}{12.247} = 24.49 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (iv): Total Inventory Cost**

$$
\begin{aligned}
OC &= \frac{12000}{979.8} \times 200 = \text{Rs. } 2449.49 \\[4pt]
HC &= \frac{979.8}{2} \times 5 = \text{Rs. } 2449.49 \\[4pt]
TCU_{\min} &= 2449.49 + 2449.49 = \text{Rs. } 4898.98
\end{aligned}
$$

Note that at EOQ, ordering cost = holding cost (a property known as the **EOQ break-even**).

**Step (v): Reorder Point**

Daily demand:
$$d = \frac{D}{\text{working days}} = \frac{12000}{300} = 40 \text{ units/day}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
ROP &= d \times L = 40 \times 30 = 1200 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — How Classification Changes the Formula

| Classification Change | New Formula vs. Classical EOQ | Numerical Effect |
|---|---|---|
| Add **finite rate** $p$ | $Q^* \times \sqrt{p/(p-d)}$ | $Q^*$ **increases** (fewer, larger lots) |
| Add **backorders** | $Q^* \times \sqrt{(H+C_s)/C_s}$ | $Q^*$ **increases** (cheaper to stockout) |
| Add **quantity discount** | Stepwise comparison at price breaks | $Q^*$ jumps to next break |
| Add **safety stock** $SS = z\sigma_L$ | $Q^*$ unchanged, $ROP$ increases by $SS$ | Service level rises |
| Add **storage constraint** | Lagrangian with multiplier $\lambda$ | Optimal $Q_i$ reduced |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
