```mermaid
    startA[Start of period t for Item i]
    checkGR[Gross Requirement GR sub t known]
    calcNR[Compute Net Requirement NR sub t]
    decLot{Apply Lot Sizing Rule}
    allocPORc[Planned Order Receipt PORc sub t]
    bsched[Offset by Lead Time LT]
    allocPOR[Planned Order Release POR sub t-LT]
    updateOH[Update Projected On-Hand for t+1]
    nextPeriod[Advance to next period]
    done[All periods complete]

    startA --> checkGR
    checkGR --> calcNR
    calcNR --> decLot
    decLot --> allocPORc
    allocPORc --> bsched
    bsched --> allocPOR
    allocPOR --> updateOH
    updateOH --> nextPeriod
    nextPeriod --> checkGR
    nextPeriod --> done
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams for KTU:** In the exam, even if you cannot reproduce the exact Mermaid diagram, drawing a labelled block diagram with arrows from "MPS, BOM, ISF" to "MRP Processor" to "Outputs" earns full conceptual marks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**"Differentiate between independent and dependent demand with one manufacturing example each."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

- **Independent Demand** is demand for a finished product that is determined by external market forces and customer orders; it must be **forecasted** rather than calculated. *Example:* Demand for passenger cars at a Maruti Suzuki dealership.
- **Dependent Demand** is demand for components or sub-assemblies that is **derived mathematically** from the demand for their parent (higher-level) items using the BOM. *Example:* Demand for tyres, engines, and brake pads derived from car demand.
- The fundamental distinction is that dependent demand does **not require forecasting** — it is computed deterministically by MRP, whereas independent demand requires statistical forecasting techniques like moving averages or exponential smoothing. **(3 marks — 1 each for definition + example + distinction)**

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**"List the three primary inputs and three primary outputs of an MRP system."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

- **Inputs:** Master Production Schedule (MPS), Bill of Materials (BOM), Inventory Status File (ISF).
- **Outputs:** Planned Order Releases, Reschedule Notices, Exception Reports. **(3 marks — 1.5 each for inputs and outputs)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A. [KTU University Exam - Jul 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **structure of a Bill of Materials (BOM)** with a suitable example. Discuss **single-level** and **multi-level** BOMs. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A product **X** requires 2 units of sub-assembly **Y**. Sub-assembly Y in turn requires 3 units of component **Z** and 1 unit of component **W**. The MPS calls for **100 units of X to be completed in Week 8**. Component data are:

| Item | Lead Time (weeks) | Initial On-Hand | Safety Stock |
|------|-------------------|------------------|---------------|
| Y | 2 | 30 | 0 |
| Z | 1 | 100 | 0 |
| W | 1 | 50 | 0 |

Use **Lot-for-Lot** lot-sizing. Prepare MRP tables for Y, Z, and W and determine the **planned order release** for each. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — BOM Structure

A Bill of Materials (BOM) is a structured engineering list of all raw materials, sub-assemblies, and components required to manufacture **one unit** of a parent item, along with the **quantities** required. It represents the *recipe* of a manufactured product.

- **Single-Level BOM:** Lists only the *immediate* components of a product (one level down). Example: Product X's BOM lists only Y.
- **Multi-Level BOM (Indented BOM):** Lists *all* components at every level, indented to show hierarchy. Example: X → Y → Z, W.

The **lowest-level code** of an item is determined by its maximum distance from the end product. Items at the *same* logical level must be planned at the *same* time so that parent-child relationships are respected. This is essential because a component appearing in multiple parents must be netted only after all parent requirements are known. **(7 marks: 2 for definition, 2 for single vs multi-level distinction, 3 for example)**

#### Model Solution to (b) — MRP Tables

**Step 1 — Gross Requirement of Y (Week 8):**
$$ GR_{Y,8} = 100 \times 2 = 200 \text{ units} $$

**MRP Table for Y (LT = 2 weeks):**

| Y (OH=30, SS=0, LT=2) | Wk 6 | Wk 7 | Wk 8 |
|-------------------------|------|------|------|
| Gross Requirements | 0 | 0 | 200 |
| Scheduled Receipts | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| Projected On-Hand (30) | 30 | 30 | 30 |
| Net Requirements | 0 | 0 | 170 |
| Planned Order Receipts | 0 | 0 | 170 |
| **Planned Order Release** | 0 | **170** | 0 |

$$ NR_{Y,8} = 200 - 30 = 170 \quad ; \quad POR_{Y,6} \text{ (Week 8 - 2)} $$

**Step 2 — Since Y's POR is in Week 6 for 170 units, explode:**

$$ GR_{Z,6} = 170 \times 3 = 510 \quad ; \quad GR_{W,6} = 170 \times 1 = 170 $$

**MRP Table for Z (LT = 1 week):**

| Z (OH=100, SS=0, LT=1) | Wk 5 | Wk 6 | Wk 7 |
|-------------------------|------|------|------|
| Gross Requirements | 0 | 510 | 0 |
| Scheduled Receipts | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| Projected On-Hand (100) | 100 | 100 | 100 |
| Net Requirements | 0 | 410 | 0 |
| Planned Order Receipts | 0 | 410 | 0 |
| **Planned Order Release** | **410** | 0 | 0 |

$$ NR_{Z,6} = 510 - 100 = 410 \quad ; \quad POR_{Z,5} $$

**MRP Table for W (LT = 1 week):**

| W (OH=50, SS=0, LT=1) | Wk 5 | Wk 6 | Wk 7 |
|-------------------------|------|------|------|
| Gross Requirements | 0 | 170 | 0 |
| Scheduled Receipts | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| Projected On-Hand (50) | 50 | 50 | 50 |
| Net Requirements | 0 | 120 | 0 |
| Planned Order Receipts | 0 | 120 | 0 |
| **Planned Order Release** | **120** | 0 | 0 |

$$ NR_{W,6} = 170 - 50 = 120 \quad ; \quad POR_{W,5} $$

**Final Planned Order Release Schedule:**

| Item | Quantity | Release Week |
|------|----------|---------------|
| Y | 170 | Week 6 |
| Z | 410 | Week 5 |
| W | 120 | Week 5 |

**(7 marks — 1 for GR calculation, 2 for each of the three MRP tables, 2 for final summary)**

---

### Question B. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Describe the **structure of an MRP system** with a neat block diagram showing its three inputs, three outputs, and the processing logic. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **procedure of MRP** in detail. How does **netting, lot-sizing, and offsetting** work together? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — MRP System Structure

The MRP system is a closed-loop, computer-based information system comprising three primary **inputs** that feed into a central processing engine, generating three primary **outputs** that drive execution on the shop floor and in purchasing.

**The Three Inputs:**
1. **Master Production Schedule (MPS):** A time-phased declaration of finished goods to be produced, in specific quantities, by specific dates. It is the *authoritative demand signal* driving MRP.
2. **Bill of Materials (BOM):** A hierarchical engineering recipe defining the *quantity* of each component needed per unit of parent.
3. **Inventory Status File (ISF):** A dynamic record of current on-hand inventory, scheduled receipts, safety stock, lead times, and lot-sizing rules for every stocked item.

**The Processing Engine:** The MRP computer program iteratively applies netting, lot-sizing, and offsetting for each item across every time bucket.

**The Three Outputs:**
1. **Planned Order Releases** — Purchase or work orders to be issued.
2. **Reschedule Notices** — Recommendations to expedite/de-delay/cancel existing orders.
3. **Exception Reports** — Alerts on deviations (stockouts, late orders, scrap).

*(Block diagram as shown in Section 4.1 above should be drawn.)* **(7 marks: 2 each for inputs/outputs, 3 for engine + diagram)**

#### Model Solution to (b) — MRP Procedure

The MRP procedure executes **three nested algorithms** for every item, in every time bucket, in **lowest-level code order**:

**Procedure 1 — Netting (Demand Computation):**
For each period $t$:
$$ NR_{t} = \max\bigl( 0,\ GR_{t} + SR_{t} - OH_{t-1} - SS \bigr) $$
This determines *how much* is actually needed after accounting for current stock and inbound shipments.

**Procedure 2 — Lot-Sizing (Quantity Determination):**
Once $NR_t$ is known, a lot-sizing rule (L4L, EOQ, POQ, etc.) converts the net requirement into a discrete planned order receipt quantity $PORc_t$ that covers $NR_t$ (often in a quantity that minimizes total cost).

**Procedure 3 — Offsetting / Backscheduling (Timing):**
The planned order receipt $PORc_t$ is shifted backwards by the item's lead time $LT$:
$$ POR_{t-LT} = PORc_{t} $$
This determines *when* the order must be *released* so that it arrives exactly when needed, neither too early (excess inventory) nor too late (stockout).

The three procedures are **tightly coupled**: netting determines *what* is needed, lot-sizing decides *how much* to order, and offsetting determines *when* to release. Items are processed in **lowest-level code order** to ensure that components shared across multiple parents are not double-counted. **(7 marks: 2 each for netting, lot-sizing, offsetting + 1 for LLC importance)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting to backschedule:** Many students compute the *Planned Order Receipt* correctly but then forget to subtract lead time for the *Planned Order Release*. This alone costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Wrong low-level order:** If components are processed before their parent, planned orders will be wrong. Always explode **bottom-up**.
> 3. **Ignoring negative net requirements:** Students often write $-20$ instead of $0$. Convention: $NR_t = \max(0, \ldots)$.
> 4. **Units mismatch:** Always write units (units/week, units/order). Examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit.
> 5. **Confusing POR with PORc:** POR = Release date; PORc = Receipt date. Drawing arrows in the table is the safest way to avoid this.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Print Before Exam**

- [ ] MRP stands for **Material Requirements Planning**; it handles **dependent demand** only.
- [ ] Three inputs: **MPS, BOM, ISF**. Three outputs: **Planned Order Releases, Reschedule Notices, Exception Reports**.
- [ ] Three procedures: **Netting → Lot-Sizing → Offsetting (Backscheduling)**.
- [ ] Always process items in **Low-Level Code (LLC)** order — bottom-up.
- [ ] Core netting equation: $NR_t = GR_t + SR_t - OH_{t-1} - SS$ (with floor at 0).
- [ ] Lead time offset: $POR_{t-LT} = PORc_t$.
- [ ] Lot-for-Lot (L4L) is the simplest and most commonly tested lot-sizing rule in KTU.
- [ ] MRP-II (Manufacturing Resource Planning) extends MRP by integrating **Capacity Requirements Planning (CRP)** and **shop-floor feedback**.
- [ ] MRP does **not** consider capacity constraints — that is handled by **CRP** or **Rough-Cut Capacity Planning (RCCP)**.
- [ ] Differences to memorize: MRP vs. MRP-II vs. ERP; MRP vs. JIT; MRP vs. ROP (Reorder Point).
- [ ] KTU favorite numerical pattern: 2- or 3-level BOM with L4L, no safety stock, 1- or 2-week lead times, single period MPS demand.
- [ ] Common mistake trap: do not confuse *Scheduled Receipts* (incoming from existing orders) with *Planned Order Receipts* (from new orders).
- [ ] Safety stock only affects MRP if explicitly stated — when present, it raises net requirements and *must be replenished* every time on-hand dips below SS.
- [ ] Closed-loop MRP includes **feedback** from execution (actual vs. plan) into re-planning — often a 3-mark theory question.
- [ ] MRP assumes **perfect BOM accuracy** — the **Garbage In, Garbage Out (GIGO)** principle is a valid KTU exam point.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MRP Calculations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET601 - OPERATIONS RESEARCH AND PRODUCTION MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** MRP Calculations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Material Requirements Planning (MRP)** is a computer-based production planning and inventory control system used to manage dependent demand items. It translates the **Master Production Schedule (MPS)** into a detailed schedule for the procurement of raw materials, components, and sub-assemblies, taking into account the **Bill of Materials (BOM)** structure, lead times, and current inventory status.

**KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Syllabus Standard):**
MRP is a logical, calculative technique that determines *what* components are needed, *how many* are required, and *when* they must be ordered so that production schedules are met without inventory shortages or excessive carrying costs.

### 1.2 The Three Pillars of MRP (Inputs)

Every MRP run requires three foundational inputs:

| Input | Full Name | Role |
|---|---|---|
| **MPS** | Master Production Schedule | Dictates *what* end items must be produced and *when* |
| **BOM** | Bill of Materials | Defines *how* each end item is made (component recipe) |
| **IS** | Inventory Status File | Records current on-hand stock, scheduled receipts, and lead times |

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The Restaurant Kitchen Analogy**
> Imagine you are running a restaurant. Your *Master Schedule* says you must serve **50 biryani plates on Saturday** and **100 plates on Sunday**. You cannot just cook rice and spices — you need exact quantities of **basmati rice, chicken, onions, masala, oil, and yogurt**, and they must arrive in your kitchen at the right time.
> - **MPS** = The customer orders (50 on Saturday, 100 on Sunday)
> - **BOM** = The recipe card (1 kg rice per 10 plates, 0.5 kg chicken per 10 plates)
> - **Inventory Status** = What is already in your pantry
> - **MRP Calculations** = The shopping list with quantities and dates
> - **Planned Order Release** = The moment you call the supplier
> - **Planned Order Receipt** = When the goods land in your kitchen

Without MRP, you either run out of chicken on Saturday night (stockout) or buy so much that yogurt expires by Monday (overstock). MRP eliminates this chaos mathematically.

### 1.4 Key Metrics Used in MRP Calculations

- **Gross Requirements (GR)** – Total quantity needed in a period (ignoring on-hand).
- **Scheduled Receipts (SR)** – Confirmed incoming orders already in pipeline.
- **Projected On-Hand Inventory (POH)** – Expected inventory at end of period.
- **Net Requirements (NR)** – Quantity that *must* be procured after subtracting inventory.
- **Planned Order Receipts (POR)** – Quantity scheduled to arrive at the start of a period.
- **Planned Order Releases (PORel)** – The actual order placed (offset by **lead time**).
- **Lot Size (Q)** – Quantity ordered each time an order is placed.
- **Lead Time (LT)** – Time gap between order placement and receipt.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The MRP Calculation Logic – Step-by-Step Logic Tree

The MRP logic for each period $t$ follows a strict sequential flow:

**Step 1: Compute Projected On-Hand Inventory (POH)**
$$\text{POH}_t = \text{POH}_{t-1} + \text{SR}_t + \text{POR}_t - \text{GR}_t$$

**Step 2: Determine if Net Requirement Exists**
$$\text{NR}_t = \max(0, \, \text{GR}_t - \text{POH}_{t-1} - \text{SR}_t)$$

**Step 3: Decide Planned Order Receipt (Lot Sizing Applied)**
$$\text{POR}_t = f(\text{NR}_t, \, \text{Lot Sizing Rule})$$

**Step 4: Offset by Lead Time to get Planned Order Release**
$$\text{PORel}_t = \text{POR}_{t - LT}$$

### 2.2 Lot Sizing Techniques – KTU Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET — LOT SIZING RULES**
> The 5 lot sizing methods you *must* know for the 2024 scheme exam:

| # | Method | Logic / Formula | When Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Lot-for-Lot (L4L)** | $Q_t = \text{NR}_t$ | When holding cost is high; variable demand |
| 2 | **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)** | $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | Stable, uniform demand |
| 3 | **Economic Period (EP)** | $N^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2S}{H \cdot D_r}}$ | Discrete time periods |
| 4 | **Least Unit Cost (LUC)** | Choose lot that **minimises** $\dfrac{S + \sum H}{\text{Total Units}}$ | Heuristic, low computation |
| 5 | **Part Period Balancing (PPB)** | Order until $\sum(\text{Holding Cost} \times \text{Periods}) \approx S$ | Reduces total cost heuristically |

Where:
- $D$ = Annual demand (units/year)
- $D_r$ = Demand per period (units/period)
- $S$ = Ordering cost per order (**Rs./order**)
- $H$ = Holding/carrying cost per unit per period (**Rs./unit/period**)
- $N^*$ = Optimal number of periods between orders

### 2.3 Annualised Conversion Trick (Frequently Tested)

When the planner gives weekly data but formulas need annual data:

$$D_{\text{annual}} = D_{\text{weekly}} \times \frac{52 \text{ weeks}}{1 \text{ year}}$$

$$H_{\text{annual}} = H_{\text{weekly}} \times 52$$

### 2.4 BOM Explosion Logic

For each parent component $i$ with quantity $q_i$ per unit of child $j$:

$$\text{GR}_{\text{child}_j, \, t} = \sum_i \left( \text{PORel}_{i, \, t} \times q_{ij} \right)$$

The summation runs over all parent items that consume child $j$.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

MRP calculations are foundational in:
- **Automotive assembly lines** (Toyota Production System)
- **Pharmaceutical manufacturing** (batch tracking)
- **Aerospace component sourcing**
- **Electronics OEM (Foxconn, Apple supply chains)**
- **MRP-II and ERP systems** (SAP, Oracle)

> [!NOTE]
> Real-world engineers use MRP to compress **weeks of manual planning** into **minutes of computation**, while preventing **stockouts of 95%+** and reducing inventory carrying cost by **20–35%**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Complete Worked Example – End Item MRP using Lot-for-Lot

**Problem Statement:**
A KTU-2024 style question. Compute the MRP record for **Product P**.

**Given Data:**
- Beginning Inventory: **30 units**
- Lead Time: **LT = 2 weeks**
- No Scheduled Receipts
- Lot Sizing Rule: **Lot-for-Lot (L4L)**
- Planning Horizon: **6 weeks**

**Master Production Schedule (MPS):**

| Week $t$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| MPS (units) | 50 | 0 | 80 | 40 | 0 | 60 |

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step MRP Calculation

**Step A — Week 1 Calculation:**

Projected On-Hand at end of Week 1:
$$\text{POH}_1 = \text{POH}_0 + \text{SR}_1 + \text{POR}_1 - \text{GR}_1$$

Substituting:
$$\text{POH}_1 = 30 + 0 + \text{POR}_1 - 50 = \text{POR}_1 - 20$$

For POH $\geq 0$:
$$\text{POR}_1 - 20 \geq 0 \implies \text{POR}_1 \geq 20$$

With L4L rule: $\text{POR}_1 = 20$

$$\text{POH}_1 = 20 - 20 = 0 \text{ units}$$

**Step B — Week 2 Calculation:**
$$\text{POH}_2 = \text{POH}_1 + \text{SR}_2 + \text{POR}_2 - \text{GR}_2$$
$$\text{POH}_2 = 0 + 0 + 0 - 0 = 0 \text{ units}$$

No requirement, no order. **NR = 0, POR = 0**.

**Step C — Week 3 Calculation:**
$$\text{POH}_3 = \text{POH}_2 + \text{SR}_3 + \text{POR}_3 - \text{GR}_3$$
$$\text{POH}_3 = 0 + 0 + \text{POR}_3 - 80 = \text{POR}_3 - 80$$

With L4L: $\text{POR}_3 = 80$

$$\text{POH}_3 = 80 - 80 = 0 \text{ units}$$

**Step D — Week 4 Calculation:**
$$\text{POH}_4 = 0 + 0 + 0 - 40 = -40 \implies \text{POR}_4 = 40$$
$$\text{POH}_4 = 40 - 40 = 0 \text{ units}$$

**Step E — Week 5 Calculation:**
$$\text{POH}_5 = 0 + 0 + 0 - 0 = 0 \text{ units}$$

**Step F — Week 6 Calculation:**
$$\text{POH}_6 = 0 + 0 + \text{POR}_6 - 60 = \text{POR}_6 - 60$$

With L4L: $\text{POR}_6 = 60$

$$\text{POH}_6 = 60 - 60 = 0 \text{ units}$$

---

**Step G — Planned Order Releases (Offset by Lead Time = 2):**

| Planned Receipt Week | Release Week = Receipt $-$ 2 |
|---|---|
| Week 1 (20 units) | Week $-1$ (before period start) |
| Week 3 (80 units) | Week 1 |
| Week 4 (40 units) | Week 2 |
| Week 6 (60 units) | Week 4 |

---

### 3.3 Final MRP Record Table — Product P

| Item: P (LT = 2) | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Gross Requirements** | 50 | 0 | 80 | 40 | 0 | 60 |
| **Scheduled Receipts** | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| **Projected On-Hand (30)** | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| **Net Requirements** | 20 | 0 | 80 | 40 | 0 | 60 |
| **Planned Order Receipts** | 20 | 0 | 80 | 40 | 0 | 60 |
| **Planned Order Releases** | — | 20 | 40 | 0 | 60 | — |

> [!NOTE]
> The convention in KTU answer scripts: use a single horizontally-laid table per item, with the beginning inventory shown in brackets next to the POH row header.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Lot-Sizing Methods

For the same Product P data, suppose:
- Ordering Cost $S$ = **Rs. 100 per order**
- Holding Cost $H$ = **Rs. 2 per unit per week**
- Annualised demand: $D = 230 \text{ units} \times \frac{52}{6} \approx 1993 \text{ units/year}$
- Annualised holding: $H_a = 2 \times 52 = $ **Rs. 104/unit/year**

**EOQ Computation:**
$$\text{EOQ} = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H_a}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1993 \times 100}{104}}$$
$$\text{EOQ} = \sqrt{\frac{398600}{104}} = \sqrt{3832.7} \approx 61.91 \approx 62 \text{ units}$$

**Economic Period ($N^*$):**
$$N^* = \sqrt{\frac{2S}{H \cdot D_r}}$$

where $D_r = 230/6 \approx 38.33$ units/week

$$N^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 100}{2 \times 38.33}} = \sqrt{\frac{200}{76.66}} = \sqrt{2.61} \approx 1.61$$

Round to $N^* = 2$ periods. Order every 2 weeks.

**Comparison Table:**

| Method | Order Qty | Orders/Period | Total Cost Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lot-for-Lot | Matches NR exactly | 4 orders | Low holding, high ordering |
| EOQ (~62) | Fixed 62 | ~3 orders | Balanced |
| Economic Period (2) | Cover 2 weeks | 3 orders | Similar to EOQ |
| LUC | Heuristic (trial) | Variable | Cost-minimising heuristic |
| PPB | Heuristic (cumulative) | Variable | Cost-minimising heuristic |

---

### 3.5 BOM Explosion Example (Worked)

**BOM Structure for Product A:**

- **Level 0:** A
- **Level 1:** A requires **2 units of B** and **3 units of C**
- **Level 2:** B requires **4 units of D** and **2 units of E**
- **Level 2:** C requires **1 unit of D** and **3 units of E**

**MPS for A in Week 1 = 50 units**

**Explosion:**

| Level | Item | Calculation | Gross Req Week 1 |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | A | MPS | 50 |
| 1 | B | $50 \times 2$ | 100 |
| 1 | C | $50 \times 3$ | 150 |
| 2 | D (from B) | $100 \times 4$ | 400 |
| 2 | D (from C) | $150 \times 1$ | 150 |
| **2 Total** | **D** | $400 + 150$ | **550** |
| 2 | E (from B) | $100 \times 2$ | 200 |
| 2 | E (from C) | $150 \times 3$ | 450 |
| **2 Total** | **E** | $200 + 450$ | **650** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When a part appears under multiple parents, its gross requirements are **summed** at the explosion level.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 MRP System Block Diagram – Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputA["MPS - Master Production Schedule"]
    inputB["BOM - Bill of Materials"]
    inputC["Inventory Status File"]
    engine["MRP Logic Engine"]
    outA["Gross Requirements"]
    outB["Net Requirements"]
    outC["Planned Order Receipts"]
    outD["Planned Order Releases"]
    outE["Exception Reports"]
    inputA --> engine
    inputB --> engine
    inputC --> engine
    engine --> outA
    engine --> outB
    engine --> outC
    engine --> outD
    engine --> outE
```

### 4.2 BOM Tree Schematic – Indented Product Structure

```mermaid
graph TD
    prodA["Product A - Level 0"]
    compB["B - Qty 2 per A - Level 1"]
    compC["C - Qty 3 per A - Level 1"]
    partD["D - Qty 4 per B and Qty 1 per C - Level 2"]
    partE["E - Qty 2 per B and Qty 3 per C - Level 2"]
    prodA --> compB
    prodA --> compC
    compB --> partD
    compB --> partE
    compC --> partD
    compC --> partE
```

### 4.3 MRP Calculation Algorithm Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Start MRP Calculation for Item I"]
    readInv["Read Beginning Inventory BI"]
    readLT["Read Lead Time LT"]
    loopStart["FOR each period t = 1 to T"]
    readGR["Read Gross Requirement GR sub t"]
    readSR["Read Scheduled Receipt SR sub t"]
    computePOH["POH sub t = POH sub t-1 plus SR sub t plus POR sub t minus GR sub t"]
    checkNet{"Is GR sub t greater than POH sub t-1 plus SR sub t"}
    computeNR["Net Req NR sub t = GR sub t minus POH sub t-1 minus SR sub t"]
    applyLot["Apply Lot Sizing Rule to get POR sub t"]
    offsetRel["PORel sub t-LT = POR sub t"]
    endLoop["END FOR"]
    output["Output MRP Table"]
    start --> readInv
    readInv --> readLT
    readLT --> loopStart
    loopStart --> readGR
    readGR --> readSR
    readSR --> computePOH
    computePOH --> checkNet
    checkNet -->|"Yes"| computeNR
    checkNet -->|"No"| loopStart
    computeNR --> applyLot
    applyLot --> offsetRel
    offsetRel --> loopStart
    loopStart -->|"All weeks done"| endLoop
    endLoop --> output
```

### 4.4 Lot Sizing Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    req["Net Requirement NR arises in Week t"]
    q1{"Lot Sizing Rule"}
    l4l["L4L - POR = NR"]
    eoq["EOQ - POR = round up NR divided by EOQ times EOQ"]
    ep["Economic Period - Cover N* periods"]
    luc["LUC - Iterate combinations pick minimum cost per unit"]
    ppb["PPB - Accumulate economic value until PP equals S"]
    final["Planned Order Receipt POR fixed"]
    req --> q1
    q1 -->|"L4L"| l4l
    q1 -->|"EOQ"| eoq
    q1 -->|"EP"| ep
    q1 -->|"LUC"| luc
    q1 -->|"PPB"| ppb
    l4l --> final
    eoq --> final
    ep --> final
    luc --> final
    ppb --> final
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> Define MRP. List any **four** inputs required for MRP calculations.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Material Requirements Planning (MRP) is a computer-based production planning and inventory control system that translates the Master Production Schedule into a detailed time-phased plan for the procurement of components and raw materials, accounting for lead times, BOM structure, and current inventory.

**Four Inputs (1 mark – ¼ each):**
1. **Master Production Schedule (MPS)** – What end items are needed and when.
2. **Bill of Materials (BOM)** – The product structure / recipe.
3. **Inventory Status File** – Current on-hand and scheduled receipts.
4. **Lead Time Information** – For each component.

*(Alternative valid 4th input: Lot-Sizing Rules or Outstanding Orders.)*

---

**Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> Distinguish between **Gross Requirements** and **Net Requirements** in MRP.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Gross Requirements | Net Requirements |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Total quantity needed regardless of stock | Quantity needed *after* using existing inventory |
| Formula | Comes directly from MPS × BOM | $NR_t = \max(0, \, GR_t - POH_{t-1} - SR_t)$ |
| Dependency | Independent of on-hand stock | Fully dependent on projected stock |
| Role | **Demand input** | **Order trigger** |

Gross Requirement is the **uncorrected** demand signal; Net Requirement is the **actionable** order quantity after offsetting available inventory.

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> The following data relates to **Product X**:
> - Beginning Inventory: **40 units**
> - Lead Time: **3 weeks**
> - No scheduled receipts
> - Lot Sizing Rule: **Lot-for-Lot**
> - Planning Horizon: **6 weeks**
>
> **MPS:** Week 1 = 30, Week 2 = 50, Week 3 = 0, Week 4 = 60, Week 5 = 40, Week 6 = 0
>
> **(a)** Compute the complete MRP record for Product X and tabulate the planned order releases. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Product X requires **2 units of Y** and **3 units of Z**. Y requires **1 unit of W** and Z requires **2 units of W**. If the lead time for Y is 1 week and for Z is 2 weeks, compute the gross requirements of W in Week 1 using BOM explosion. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question A:**

**(a) MRP Record for Product X:**

Apply the MRP logic $\text{POH}_t = \text{POH}_{t-1} + \text{SR}_t + \text{POR}_t - \text{GR}_t$ week by week.

- **Week 1:** $40 + 0 + \text{POR}_1 - 30 \geq 0 \implies \text{POR}_1 = 0$; POH = 10
- **Week 2:** $10 + 0 + \text{POR}_2 - 50 \geq 0 \implies \text{POR}_2 = 40$; POH = 0
- **Week 3:** $0 + 0 + 0 - 0 = 0$; POH = 0
- **Week 4:** $0 + 0 + \text{POR}_4 - 60 \geq 0 \implies \text{POR}_4 = 60$; POH = 0
- **Week 5:** $0 + 0 + \text{POR}_5 - 40 \geq 0 \implies \text{POR}_5 = 40$; POH = 0
- **Week 6:** $0 + 0 + 0 - 0 = 0$; POH = 0

**Final MRP Table (7 marks):**

| Item: X (BI = 40, LT = 3) | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gross Requirements | 30 | 50 | 0 | 60 | 40 | 0 |
| Scheduled Receipts | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| Projected On-Hand | 10 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| Net Requirements | 0 | 40 | 0 | 60 | 40 | 0 |
| Planned Order Receipts | 0 | 40 | 0 | 60 | 40 | 0 |
| Planned Order Releases | 40 (W1) | 0 | 60 (W3) | 40 (W4) | — | — |

**(b) BOM Explosion for W in Week 1:**

Step 1 — Gross requirement of Y in Week 1:
$$\text{GR}_Y = 30 \times 2 = 60 \text{ units (from Week 1 release of X)}$$

Step 2 — Gross requirement of Z in Week 1:
$$\text{GR}_Z = 30 \times 3 = 90 \text{ units}$$

Step 3 — W from Y: $60 \times 1 = 60$ units
Step 4 — W from Z: $90 \times 2 = 180$ units

**Total Gross Requirement of W in Week 1 (7 marks):**
$$\text{GR}_W = 60 + 180 = \boxed{240 \text{ units}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> For **Component C**, the following data is available:
> - Beginning Inventory: **20 units**
> - Lead Time: **2 weeks**
> - Scheduled Receipts: Week 3 = **50 units**
> - Lot Sizing Rule: **Economic Order Quantity**
> - Annual Demand $D$ = **5000 units/year**, Ordering Cost $S$ = **Rs. 200/order**, Holding Cost $H$ = **Rs. 5/unit/year**
>
> **MPS Gross Requirements:** Week 1 = 100, Week 2 = 200, Week 3 = 150, Week 4 = 300, Week 5 = 100, Week 6 = 50
>
> **(a)** Compute the EOQ and prepare the MRP record using EOQ lot sizing. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain how the MRP record would change if Lot-for-Lot rule was used instead. Compare the ordering cost and holding cost components qualitatively. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question B:**

**(a) EOQ Computation:**
$$\text{EOQ} = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 5000 \times 200}{5}}$$
$$\text{EOQ} = \sqrt{\frac{2000000}{5}} = \sqrt{400000} = \boxed{632.46 \approx 633 \text{ units}}$$

**MRP Record using EOQ (rounded order quantities):**

- Week 1: POH = 20 + 0 + POR − 100. Need 80. Order 633 → POH = 553
- Week 2: POH = 553 − 200 = 353. No order.
- Week 3: POH = 353 + 50 (SR) − 150 = 253. No order.
- Week 4: POH = 253 − 300 = −47. Order 633 → POH = 586
- Week 5: POH = 586 − 100 = 486. No order.
- Week 6: POH = 486 − 50 = 436. No order.

| Item: C (BI=20, LT=2, SR=50 in W3) | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gross Requirements | 100 | 200 | 150 | 300 | 100 | 50 |
| Scheduled Receipts | 0 | 0 | 50 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| Projected On-Hand | 553 | 353 | 253 | 586 | 486 | 436 |
| Net Requirements | 80 | 0 | 0 | 47 | 0 | 0 |
| Planned Order Receipts | 633 | 0 | 0 | 633 | 0 | 0 |
| Planned Order Releases | — | 633 (W2) | — | 633 (W4) | — | — |

**(b) Lot-for-Lot Comparison:**

Under L4L:
- POR in Week 1 = 80
- POR in Week 4 = 47

**Qualitative Cost Comparison (7 marks):**

| Cost Component | EOQ (633) | Lot-for-Lot (variable) |
|---|---|---|
| Number of Orders | 2 | 2 |
| Ordering Cost | $2 \times 200 = $ Rs. 400 | $2 \times 200 = $ Rs. 400 |
| Holding Cost | High (large leftover stock) | Low (matches demand) |
| Inventory Investment | ~Rs. 5000 average | ~Rs. 200 average |
| Suitability | Stable demand | Variable / lumpy demand |

**Conclusion:** EOQ minimises **total annual cost** but inflates inventory; L4L minimises **holding cost** but creates more orders (if many small NRs exist).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting Lead-Time Offset:** Many students write POR = Planned Order Release. They are different by LT periods. Always shift backwards. **(−2 marks)**
> 2. **Initial Inventory:** Writing GR but ignoring the **beginning inventory** leads to a wrong NR. Always subtract previous POH first. **(−2 marks)**
> 3. **BOM Explosion:** Common mistake — using *gross* requirement of parent instead of *planned order release* of parent. MRP is driven by planned releases, not raw MPS. **(−3 marks)**
> 4. **Annual vs Weekly Conversion:** Forgetting to multiply weekly $H$ by 52 or $D_r$ by 52/periods leads to wrong EOQ. **(−2 marks)**
> 5. **Negative POH:** Never allow POH to be negative. Always trigger a POR that makes POH $\geq 0$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Things to Remember — MRP Calculations**

- **MRP = Time-phased, dependent-demand explosion** of MPS via BOM.
- The **core equation** is $\text{POH}_t = \text{POH}_{t-1} + \text{SR}_t + \text{POR}_t - \text{GR}_t$.
- **Net Requirement = GR − (Previous POH + SR)**, never negative (use max with 0).
- **Planned Order Release = Planned Order Receipt shifted left by Lead Time.**
- **Lot-for-Lot:** $Q = NR$ (simplest, zero theoretical excess).
- **EOQ:** $Q^* = \sqrt{2DS/H}$ (best when demand is steady).
- **Economic Period:** $N^* = \sqrt{2S / (H \cdot D_r)}$ (integer rounds).
- **LUC:** Heuristic — pick lot with minimum $(S + \sum H)/\text{Total Units}$.
- **PPB:** Accumulate economic periods until $\sum(H \times \text{weeks}) \approx S$.
- **BOM Explosion:** GR of child = $\sum$ (PORel of parent × quantity per).
- **Common parts under multiple parents** must have their GRs **summed**.
- **MRP outputs:** Planned order releases + exception reports + reschedule notices.
- **MRP-II evolution** integrates MRP with capacity planning, shop floor control, and finance.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MRP-II
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET601 - OPERATIONS RESEARCH AND PRODUCTION MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** MRP-II

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Manufacturing Resource Planning (MRP-II)** is an integrated, computer-based information system designed specifically for planning and controlling all manufacturing resources (materials, capacity, labor, machinery, and tooling) of a manufacturing enterprise in an optimal and synchronized manner to achieve the **Master Production Schedule (MPS)**. MRP-II extends the closed-loop **Material Requirements Planning (MRP)** system by incorporating additional planning modules such as **Capacity Requirements Planning (CRP)**, **Shop Floor Control (SFC)**, and **Financial Planning**, thereby forming a comprehensive closed-loop production planning and control framework.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> MRP-II is a unified system that simultaneously plans *all* resources — not just materials. It is the **"what-if" simulation engine** of the factory floor, aligning production with demand, capacity, and finances in real-time.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are organizing a large Indian wedding banquet for **500 guests**:
- **MRP (Material Requirements Planning)** is like creating a *shopping list* — how much rice, dal, paneer, oil, and spices are needed based on the menu.
- **MRP-II (Manufacturing Resource Planning)** is like running the *entire kitchen operation* — you also account for:
  - How many cooks and waiters are needed (labor)
  - How many burners, tandoors, and freezers are available (capacity/machinery)
  - The cash flow to pay vendors (financial planning)
  - The exact timeline of when each dish must be cooked (scheduling)

If the menu (Master Production Schedule) changes — say, you now need biryani instead of pulao — MRP-II recalculates **everything** in one go: ingredients, cooks, burners, and budget.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exam:**
> Always remember the evolutionary lineage: **MRP → MRP-I (Closed-Loop MRP) → MRP-II → ERP (Enterprise Resource Planning)**. MRP-II is the *manufacturing-focused* precursor to modern ERP systems like SAP, Oracle, and Microsoft Dynamics.

### 1.3 Core Terminology (KTU High-Yield Vocabulary)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **BOM (Bill of Materials)** | A structured list of all raw materials, components, and sub-assemblies required to manufacture one unit of a finished product. |
| **MPS (Master Production Schedule)** | A time-phased plan specifying *what* finished products will be made, *how many*, and *when*. |
| **CRP (Capacity Requirements Planning)** | The process of determining the required machine and labor capacity to execute the MPS. |
| **SFC (Shop Floor Control)** | A real-time system that monitors and controls the actual progress of work orders on the shop floor. |
| **PAC (Planned Order Releases)** | Firm, time-phased orders generated by MRP-II that authorize production or procurement. |
| **PEG (Pegging)** | The ability to trace a demand for a finished good back to its component requirements and orders. |

### 1.4 Objectives of MRP-II (KTU Frequently Asked)

The principal objectives of an MRP-II system are:

1. **To ensure material availability** — components and raw materials arrive *just-in-time* to support the MPS.
2. **To maintain minimum inventory levels** — reduce carrying costs and working capital lock-up.
3. **To plan manufacturing activities and capacity utilization** — balance load against available resources.
4. **To plan and control shop floor activities** — execute the plan with real-time feedback.
5. **To provide financial planning integration** — translate operational plans into monetary terms.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architecture of MRP-II — The Three-Layer Closed Loop

MRP-II operates as a **closed-loop hierarchical planning system** with three integrated planning horizons:

#### **Layer 1: Long-Range Planning (12–24 months)**
- **Tool Used:** Aggregate Production Planning (APP) / Rough-Cut Capacity Planning (RCCP).
- **Output:** Production Plan in broad product families (e.g., "10,000 SUVs next quarter").
- **Granularity:** Product family level, by month.

#### **Layer 2: Medium-Range Planning (3–12 months)**
- **Tools Used:** Master Production Scheduling (MPS) → Material Requirements Planning (MRP) → Capacity Requirements Planning (CRP).
- **Output:** Detailed time-phased orders for specific SKUs and components.
- **Granularity:** End-item level, by week.

#### **Layer 3: Short-Range / Execution (Days to weeks)**
- **Tools Used:** Shop Floor Control (SFC), Purchasing, Final Assembly Scheduling.
- **Output:** Real-time WIP tracking, dispatch lists, and exception reports.
- **Granularity:** Operation/Work-center level, by hour/shift.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Closed-Loop Feedback Mechanism:**
> If CRP detects that the load exceeds capacity at any work center, this information is **fed back to the MPS** so planners can either reschedule, shift capacity (overtime/subcontracting), or revise the plan. This is the *closed-loop* feedback that distinguishes MRP-II from the open-loop MRP.

### 2.2 Inputs, Processing Engine, and Outputs of MRP-II

The MRP-II logic engine executes the following procedural sequence:

| Stage | Input | Process | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Forecast & Orders | Demand Management | Planned Orders |
| 2 | MPS + BOM + Inventory | MRP Computation | Planned Order Releases |
| 3 | Routing + Work Centers | CRP | Capacity Load Profile |
| 4 | CRP Feedback + Plan Adjustments | Capacity Balancing | Feasible MPS |
| 5 | Cost Data + Production Plan | Financial Planning | Budget & Cash Flow |

### 2.3 MRP vs MRP-II — Critical Comparison Table

| Parameter | MRP (Material Requirements Planning) | MRP-II (Manufacturing Resource Planning) |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Materials only | All manufacturing resources |
| **Modules** | BOM, Inventory, MPS | Adds CRP, SFC, Financial Planning |
| **Capacity** | Not considered | Fully integrated (CRP) |
| **Financial View** | Absent | Built-in cost rollup |
| **Decision Support** | "What to order and when" | "What if we change the plan" |
| **Data Integration** | Single database for materials | Unified database for entire operations |
| **Feedback Loop** | Open loop | Closed loop |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **MRP-II Core Calculations — Use these in every numerical KTU question.**

**Formula 1: Gross Requirements (Planned Order Release Schedule)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
GR_t &= D_t + SS_t - OH_{t-1} - SR_t
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $GR_t$ = Gross Requirement of a component in period $t$ (units)
- $D_t$ = Independent (external) demand in period $t$
- $SS_t$ = Safety Stock requirement in period $t$
- $OH_{t-1}$ = On-Hand Inventory at end of period $t-1$
- $SR_t$ = Scheduled Receipts already on order in period $t$

**Formula 2: Net Requirements**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NR_t &= \max(0,\; GR_t - OH_{t-1})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Formula 3: Planned Order Receipt (PORc)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PORc_t &= NR_t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Formula 4: Planned Order Release (PORl) — Time-Phased Backwards by Lead Time**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PORl_t &= PORc_{(t+LT)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $LT$ is the **Lead Time** (in periods) for that component.

**Formula 5: Capacity Load at a Work Center**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_j &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( \frac{Q_i \times Std_i}{Eff_i} \right) \quad \text{[Hours per period]}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $L_j$ = Total load on work center $j$ in the planning period
- $Q_i$ = Planned production quantity of item $i$ (units)
- $Std_i$ = Standard time required for item $i$ (hours/unit)
- $Eff_i$ = Efficiency factor at the work center ($0 < Eff_i \leq 1$)

**Formula 6: Capacity Utilization Ratio**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_j &= \frac{L_j}{A_j} \times 100 \quad [\%]
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $A_j$ = Available Capacity at work center $j$ (hours per period).

**Formula 7: Capacity Variance / Overload**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta_j &= L_j - A_j
\end{aligned}
$$

- If $\Delta_j > 0$ → **Overload** (must reschedule or add capacity)
- If $\Delta_j < 0$ → **Underload** (idle capacity available)
- If $\Delta_j = 0$ → **Perfect balance**

**Formula 8: Effective Capacity (after efficiency adjustment)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_j &= (N_{shifts} \times H_{shift} \times N_{days}) - \text{Maintenance Downtime} - \text{Setup Time}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Formula 9: Total Cost of a Plan**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC &= C_{setup} + C_{holding} + C_{backorder} + C_{labor} + C_{overtime}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Formula 10: Lead Time Offset for Multi-Level BOMs**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LT_{cumulative} &= \sum_{k=1}^{m} LT_k \quad \text{[Periods]}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $LT_k$ is the lead time of item at level $k$ in the BOM tree.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the era of **Industry 4.0**, MRP-II principles are embedded within:
- **Automotive Industry:** Toyota Production System, Ford assembly plants
- **Aerospace:** Boeing and Airbus supply chain planning
- **Electronics Manufacturing:** Foxconn, Apple supply chains
- **Process Industries:** Steel plants, petrochemical refineries

Modern **ERP systems** (SAP S/4HANA, Oracle Manufacturing Cloud) are direct descendants of MRP-II, with added CRM, SCM, and analytics layers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: Multi-Period MRP-II Computation

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
A manufacturer of ceiling fans produces a parent assembly **"Fan-100"**. Each Fan-100 requires **2 units of Motor (M-50)** and **1 unit of Blade-Set (BS-30)**. The Motor itself requires **1 unit of Copper Coil (CC-10)**.

- **Master Production Schedule (MPS) for Fan-100:** Week 1 = 100 units, Week 2 = 150 units, Week 3 = 200 units.
- **Lead Times:** Fan-100 = 1 week, Motor = 2 weeks, Blade-Set = 1 week, Copper Coil = 3 weeks.
- **Beginning Inventory:** Fan-100 = 50, Motor = 30, Blade-Set = 100, Copper Coil = 60.
- **Safety Stock:** Fan-100 = 0, Motor = 20, Blade-Set = 0, Copper Coil = 50.

**Compute the MRP-II Planned Order Release Schedule for all four items.**

---

#### **Step 1: MRP for Fan-100 (Level 0 — End Item)**

Using Formula 1: $GR_t = D_t + SS_t - OH_{t-1} - SR_t$

- Initial On-Hand = 50 (given as available)
- Demand Week 1 = 100, Week 2 = 150, Week 3 = 200
- Safety Stock = 0

$$
\begin{aligned}
GR_1 &= 100 + 0 - 50 - 0 = 50 \text{ units} \\
GR_2 &= 150 + 0 - 0 - 0 = 150 \text{ units} \\
GR_3 &= 200 + 0 - 0 - 0 = 200 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

Planned Order Receipts (PORc) = Gross Requirements (no safety stock to maintain):

$$
PORc_1 = 50,\quad PORc_2 = 150,\quad PORc_3 = 200
$$

Planned Order Releases (PORl) offset by Lead Time = 1 week:

$$
PORl_0 = 50,\quad PORl_1 = 150,\quad PORl_2 = 200
$$

---

#### **Step 2: MRP for Motor M-50 (Level 1)**

Each Fan-100 uses 2 Motors. So dependent demand for Motor = $2 \times$ (Fan-100 PORc in that week).

| Week | -1 | 1 | 2 | 3 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gross Req (2×PORc) | 0 | 100 | 300 | 400 |
| Scheduled Receipts | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| On-Hand (start = 30) | 30 | 30 | 0 | 0 |
| Net Req (after SS) | - | 0 | 290 | 420 |
| Planned Order Receipt | - | 0 | 290 | 420 |
| Planned Order Release (LT=2) | 290 | 420 | - | - |

**Detailed calculation trace:**

Week 1: $OH_0 = 30$, $GR = 100$, $SS = 20$

$$
NR_1 = \max(0,\; 100 + 20 - 30) = 90 \text{ units}
$$

PORc_1 = 90, PORl_{-1} = 90 (offset by 2 weeks → release before Week 1).

Week 2: $OH_1 = 30 - 100 = -70 \Rightarrow$ need replenishment.

$$
NR_2 = 290 \text{ units (calculated similarly)}
$$

Week 3: $NR_3 = 420$ units.

**So the PORl for Motor is: Week -1 = 90, Week 0 = 0, Week 1 = 290, Week 2 = 420.**

---

#### **Step 3: MRP for Blade-Set BS-30 (Level 1)**

Each Fan-100 uses 1 Blade-Set. Demand = $1 \times$ Fan-100 PORc.

| Week | 1 | 2 | 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gross Req | 50 | 150 | 200 |
| On-Hand (start = 100) | 100 | 50 | 0 |
| Net Req | 0 | 100 | 200 |
| Planned Order Receipt | 0 | 100 | 200 |
| Planned Order Release (LT=1) | 100 | 200 | - |

---

#### **Step 4: MRP for Copper Coil CC-10 (Level 2)**

Each Motor uses 1 Copper Coil. Demand for CC = $1 \times$ Motor PORc.

Motor PORc schedule (delayed to align with assembly) effectively drives CC requirements. The calculation follows the same pattern with LT = 3 weeks and safety stock = 50.

---

#### **Step 5: Capacity Requirements Planning (CRP) Check**

Suppose Motor production requires **0.5 hours per unit** at Work Center WC-M, which operates **160 hours/week** with **85% efficiency**.

Using Formula 5: $L_j = \dfrac{Q_i \times Std_i}{Eff_i}$

For Week 1 Motor load:

$$
L_{WC-M,1} = \frac{90 \times 0.5}{0.85} = 52.94 \text{ hours}
$$

Available capacity $A_j = 160$ hours/week.

Using Formula 6: Utilization

$$
U_j = \frac{52.94}{160} \times 100 = 33.09\%
$$

**Interpretation:** Week 1 has 66.9% spare capacity → **under-loaded, plan is feasible**.

For Week 2 (Motor load = 290 units):

$$
L_{WC-M,2} = \frac{290 \times 0.5}{0.85} = 170.59 \text{ hours}
$$

Since $170.59 > 160$, we have **overload** of:

$$
\Delta_j = 170.59 - 160 = 10.59 \text{ hours overload}
$$

**MRP-II Feedback Action:** Either authorize overtime (+10.59 hrs), subcontract, or shift Week 2 production partially to Week 3. This is the **closed-loop** in action.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 MRP-II Closed-Loop Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the closed-loop architecture of MRP-II, showing how information flows from the Master Production Schedule through MRP, CRP, and back to the MPS via the feedback mechanism.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Forecast and Customer Orders] --> A2[Demand Management]
    A2 --> A3[Master Production Schedule MPS]
    A3 --> A4[Bill of Materials BOM]
    A3 --> A5[Inventory Status File]
    A4 --> A6[Material Requirements Planning MRP]
    A5 --> A6
    A6 --> A7[Planned Order Releases]
    A7 --> A8[Capacity Requirements Planning CRP]
    A8 --> A9{Rough Cut Capacity Check}
    A9 -->|Overload Detected| A10[Capacity Adjustment Overtime or Subcontract]
    A10 --> A3
    A9 -->|Feasible Plan| A11[Shop Floor Control SFC]
    A11 --> A12[Purchasing and Vendor Management]
    A11 --> A13[Production Activity Control]
    A13 --> A14[Feedback to MRP and MPS]
    A14 --> A3
    A13 --> A15[Financial Planning Module]
    A15 --> A16[Cost and Budget Reports]
```

### 4.2 MRP-II Module Hierarchy — Block Architecture

This diagram maps the hierarchical planning blocks in MRP-II, from strategic aggregate planning down to execution-level shop floor control.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[Top Level: Strategic Planning 12 to 24 months] --> B2[Aggregate Production Plan APP]
    B2 --> B3[Rough Cut Capacity Plan RCCP]
    B3 --> B4[Middle Level: Tactical Planning 3 to 12 months]
    B4 --> B5[Master Production Schedule MPS]
    B5 --> B6[Material Requirements Planning MRP]
    B6 --> B7[Capacity Requirements Planning CRP]
    B7 --> B8[Bottom Level: Operational Execution Days to Weeks]
    B8 --> B9[Shop Floor Control SFC]
    B9 --> B10[Production Activity Control PAC]
    B10 --> B11[Feedback to MPS via Closed Loop]
    B11 --> B5
    B6 --> B12[Financial Planning Module]
    B12 --> B13[Cost Rollup and Budget Output]
```

### 4.3 BOM Tree Explosion Example

This diagram shows how the Bill of Materials is "exploded" through the planning levels to generate component-level planned orders — a core visual concept for KTU problems.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Level 0: Fan 100 End Item] --> C2[Level 1a: Motor M 50 Quantity 2 per Fan]
    C1 --> C3[Level 1b: Blade Set BS 30 Quantity 1 per Fan]
    C2 --> C4[Level 2a: Copper Coil CC 10 Quantity 1 per Motor]
    C2 --> C5[Level 2b: Stator Core SC 20 Quantity 1 per Motor]
    C3 --> C6[Level 2c: Blade Aluminum BA 15 Quantity 3 per Blade Set]
    C3 --> C7[Level 2d: Hub Cap HC 05 Quantity 1 per Blade Set]
```

### 4.4 MRP-II vs MRP vs ERP — Evolutionary Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D1[1960s: Basic Inventory Control] --> D2[1970s: MRP Material Requirements Planning]
    D2 --> D3[1980s: Closed Loop MRP with CRP]
    D3 --> D4[1980s 1990s: MRP II All Manufacturing Resources]
    D4 --> D5[1990s 2000s: ERP Enterprise Wide Integration]
    D5 --> D6[2020s: Cloud ERP and Industry 4.0 MES Integration]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip for KTU:**
> Always explain the *closed-loop* nature in your diagrams. Examiners frequently award 2 marks explicitly for identifying the feedback arrow from CRP back to MPS.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
*Define MRP-II. How is it different from a basic MRP system?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **MRP-II (Manufacturing Resource Planning)** is an integrated information system that plans and controls *all* manufacturing resources — materials, capacity, labor, tooling, and finances — to achieve the Master Production Schedule (1.5 marks).
- **Difference from MRP:** While MRP focuses only on *what materials* are needed and *when*, MRP-II extends this by adding **Capacity Requirements Planning (CRP)**, **Shop Floor Control (SFC)**, and **Financial Planning** modules, forming a *closed-loop* system with feedback to the MPS (1.5 marks).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*List the three major planning levels in MRP-II and briefly state their planning horizons.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Long-Range Planning** — Horizon of 12 to 24 months, uses Aggregate Production Plan and Rough-Cut Capacity Plan (1 mark).
2. **Medium-Range Planning** — Horizon of 3 to 12 months, uses MPS, MRP, and CRP (1 mark).
3. **Short-Range / Execution Planning** — Horizon of days to weeks, uses Shop Floor Control and Production Activity Control (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

**Question A. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** Explain the architecture of an MRP-II system with a neat block diagram. Discuss the inputs, processing, and outputs at each planning level. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A product *P* requires 2 units of component *C* and 3 units of component *D*. Component *C* further requires 1 unit of sub-component *S*. The following data is given:

| Item | Lead Time (weeks) | On-Hand Inventory | Safety Stock |
|---|---|---|---|
| P | 1 | 30 | 0 |
| C | 2 | 50 | 20 |
| D | 1 | 100 | 0 |
| S | 3 | 40 | 10 |

The MPS for *P* is: Week 1 = 100 units, Week 2 = 150 units, Week 3 = 200 units. Prepare the MRP schedule for items *P*, *C*, *D*, and *S*. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) MRP-II Architecture (7 Marks):**

The MRP-II system operates as a **hierarchical, closed-loop planning system** with three integrated levels:

**Level 1 — Strategic / Long-Range (12–24 months):**
- Input: Long-term demand forecast, aggregate capacity
- Process: **Aggregate Production Planning (APP)** translates business plan into product-family-level targets.
- Output: Production Plan in broad categories.

**Level 2 — Tactical / Medium-Range (3–12 months):**
- Input: APP, Forecast orders, BOM, Inventory, Routing
- Process: **MPS → MRP → CRP**
- Output: Time-phased planned orders for specific end-items and components.

**Level 3 — Operational / Short-Range (Days–Weeks):**
- Input: Released orders from Level 2
- Process: **Shop Floor Control (SFC)** and **Production Activity Control (PAC)**
- Output: Real-time WIP tracking, dispatch lists.

**Closed-Loop Feedback:** If CRP detects capacity overload, the load profile is fed back to MPS, triggering plan adjustments (reschedule, overtime, subcontract). *(Full credit with diagram. Use the Block diagram from Section 4.2 of these notes.)*

**(b) MRP Schedule (7 Marks):**

**Item P (End-Item, LT=1, OH=30, SS=0):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
GR_1 &= 100 + 0 - 30 - 0 = 70 \text{ units} \\
GR_2 &= 150 + 0 - 0 - 0 = 150 \text{ units} \\
GR_3 &= 200 + 0 - 0 - 0 = 200 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

PORc: Week 1 = 70, Week 2 = 150, Week 3 = 200.
PORl (offset by 1 week): Week 0 = 70, Week 1 = 150, Week 2 = 200.

**Item C (LT=2, OH=50, SS=20):** Demand = $2 \times$ PORc of P.

| Week | 1 | 2 | 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gross Req | 140 | 300 | 400 |
| Scheduled Recv | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| OH (start 50) | 50 | 0 | 0 |
| NR (with SS=20) | 110 | 320 | 420 |
| PORc | 110 | 320 | 420 |
| PORl (LT=2) | 320 (Week 0) | 420 (Week 1) | — |

**Item D (LT=1, OH=100, SS=0):** Demand = $3 \times$ PORc of P.

| Week | 1 | 2 | 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gross Req | 210 | 450 | 600 |
| OH (start 100) | 100 | 0 | 0 |
| NR | 110 | 450 | 600 |
| PORc | 110 | 450 | 600 |
| PORl (LT=1) | 450 (Week 1) | 600 (Week 2) | — |

**Item S (LT=3, OH=40, SS=10):** Demand = $1 \times$ PORc of C.

| Week | 1 | 2 | 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gross Req (PORc of C) | 110 | 320 | 420 |
| OH (start 40) | 40 | 0 | 0 |
| NR (with SS=10) | 80 | 330 | 430 |
| PORc | 80 | 330 | 430 |
| PORl (LT=3) | — (offsets to earlier weeks) | — | — |

---

**Question B. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice]**
**(a)** Discuss in detail the evolution from MRP to MRP-II. Explain the role of Capacity Requirements Planning (CRP) and Shop Floor Control (SFC) within an MRP-II framework. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A work center has the following planned production loads in the next 4 weeks:

| Week | Planned Load (hours) |
|---|---|
| 1 | 380 |
| 2 | 420 |
| 3 | 350 |
| 4 | 400 |

The available capacity is **400 hours/week**, operating at **85% efficiency**, with **20 hours/week reserved for setup**. Determine the **capacity utilization**, **overload/underload**, and recommend corrective actions using the MRP-II closed-loop feedback mechanism. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Evolution from MRP to MRP-II (7 Marks):**

1. **MRP (1970s):** Open-loop system focusing solely on material requirements using BOM, MPS, and inventory data. No feedback or capacity check. *(1.5 marks)*
2. **Closed-Loop MRP (Late 1970s):** Added CRP and feedback from execution to MPS. Capacity is checked against the plan. *(1.5 marks)*
3. **MRP-II (1980s):** Expanded scope to *all* manufacturing resources — labor, tooling, machines, finance. Added SFC, Purchasing, and Financial Planning modules. **(1.5 marks)**
4. **ERP (1990s onward):** Extended beyond manufacturing to include HR, CRM, SCM across the enterprise. *(1 mark)*
5. **Role of CRP:** Determines detailed hour-by-hour capacity needed at each work center using the routing file. If overload is detected, feedback is sent to MPS for rescheduling, overtime, subcontracting, or lot-splitting. *(1 mark)*
6. **Role of SFC:** Real-time execution module that tracks WIP, releases orders, monitors progress, and reports exceptions back to planning. *(0.5 mark)*

**(b) Capacity Analysis (7 Marks):**

**Effective Available Capacity (per Formula 8):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= 400 \times 0.85 - 20 = 340 - 20 = 320 \text{ hours/week}
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — the problem states "available capacity is 400 hours/week, operating at 85% efficiency, with 20 hours/week reserved for setup." This implies:

**Step 1: Adjust for efficiency and setup:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{effective} &= (400 \times 0.85) - 20 \\
A_{effective} &= 340 - 20 = 320 \text{ hours/week}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Load, Utilization, and Variance for each week (per Formulas 5, 6, 7):**

| Week | Load (hrs) | $A_{eff}$ (hrs) | Utilization $U$ | Variance $\Delta$ | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 380 | 320 | $\frac{380}{320} \times 100 = 118.75\%$ | $+60$ | **Overload** |
| 2 | 420 | 320 | $\frac{420}{320} \times 100 = 131.25\%$ | $+100$ | **Overload** |
| 3 | 350 | 320 | $\frac{350}{320} \times 100 = 109.38\%$ | $+30$ | **Overload** |
| 4 | 400 | 320 | $\frac{400}{320} \times 100 = 125.00\%$ | $+80$ | **Overload** |

**Step 3: Total overload across 4 weeks:**

$$
\Delta_{total} = 60 + 100 + 30 + 80 = 270 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 4: MRP-II Closed-Loop Corrective Actions:**

Since every week shows overload, the MRP-II feedback mechanism would recommend:

1. **Lot Splitting:** Split Week 2's 420-hour load into Week 2 + Week 3 by overlapping operations (saves ~30 hours).
2. **Subcontracting:** Outsource ~100 hours of Week 2 work to a vendor.
3. **Overtime Authorization:** Add 30 hours overtime in Week 1 + Week 4 to absorb the remaining load.
4. **MPS Rescheduling:** Shift Week 2 demand (the heaviest) partially to Week 5 (next planning period).

**Recommended Combined Plan:** Subcontract 100 hrs + 60 hrs overtime across weeks = clears 270 hrs of overload. New utilization = 100%.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Deduction Pitfalls in MRP-II Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting Safety Stock (SS):** Many students compute Net Requirements as $GR - OH$ only. SS must be added to $GR$ before subtracting $OH$. Loses **2 marks**.
> 2. **Wrong Lead Time Offset Direction:** PORl must be **offset backward in time** by LT, not forward. A forward offset is a conceptual error — loses **2–3 marks**.
> 3. **Ignoring the Closed-Loop Feedback:** When asked about CRP overload, students often stop at "overload detected." Always mention the **feedback arrow** to MPS and the **corrective action**. This alone is worth **2 marks** in KTU valuations.
> 4. **Confusing PORc with PORl:** PORc = when material is *needed*; PORl = when order must be *released* = PORc shifted by LT. Confusing these costs **2 marks**.
> 5. **Not Adjusting for Efficiency:** In CRP calculations, raw hours ≠ effective hours. Always apply the efficiency factor and subtract setup time before computing utilization.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for MRP-II:**

- **Definition:** MRP-II = integrated planning of *all* manufacturing resources (materials + capacity + labor + finance) to achieve the MPS.
- **Evolution:** Basic Inventory → MRP → Closed-Loop MRP → MRP-II → ERP → Industry 4.0 MES.
- **Three Planning Levels:** Strategic (months–years) / Tactical (weeks–months) / Operational (days–weeks).
- **Closed-Loop Feedback:** CRP overload → feedback to MPS → reschedule / overtime / subcontract.
- **Core MRP-II Formulas to Memorize:**
  - $GR_t = D_t + SS_t - OH_{t-1} - SR_t$
  - $NR_t = \max(0, GR_t - OH_{t-1})$
  - $PORl_t = PORc_{(t+LT)}$ (offset backward by lead time)
  - $L_j = \sum (Q_i \times Std_i) / Eff_i$
  - $U_j = (L_j / A_j) \times 100\%$
  - $\Delta_j = L_j - A_j$ (positive = overload)
- **Key Distinction (must-state in every answer):** MRP plans *materials*; MRP-II plans *materials + capacity + finances*.
- **Capacity Decision Rule:** If $U_j > 100\%$ → overload; if $U_j < 85\%$ → idle capacity; optimal band is **85%–95%**.
- **Safety Stock Rule:** Always include SS in $GR$ calculation but exclude it from $NR$ when computing PORc (SS is *maintained*, not *replenished on demand*).
- **Modern Context:** SAP, Oracle ERP, Microsoft Dynamics — all descend from MRP-II architecture.
- **Industry 4.0 Link:** Today's MES (Manufacturing Execution Systems) and IIoT dashboards are real-time digital twins of the MRP-II execution layer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: JIT and Lean Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET601 - OPERATIONS RESEARCH AND PRODUCTION MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** JIT and Lean Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Just-in-Time (JIT): Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Just-in-Time (JIT)** is a pull-based production philosophy and inventory management strategy that aims to produce and deliver the right items, in the right quantity, at the right time — thereby eliminating all forms of waste (muda) from the production system. It is rooted in the **Toyota Production System (TPS)** pioneered by **Taiichi Ohno** in post-WWII Japan.

In the formal KTU 2024 syllabus context, JIT is defined as:
> *"A production strategy that aligns raw-material orders from suppliers and production schedules directly with demand, minimizing work-in-process (WIP) inventory and associated carrying costs."*

The **Five Zero Objectives** of JIT (a high-yield KTU expectation) are:

| # | Zero Objective | Meaning |
|---|----------------|---------|
| 1 | Zero Defects | Perfect quality at first attempt |
| 2 | Zero Lot Size | One-piece flow |
| 3 | Zero Setups | SMED (Single Minute Exchange of Dies) |
| 4 | Zero Breakdowns | TPM (Total Productive Maintenance) |
| 5 | Zero Handling | Elimination of non-value-adding motion |

## 1.2 Lean Operations: Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Lean Operations** is the broader, Western-coined term (popularized by *Womack & Jones* in **"The Machine That Changed the World"**, MIT, 1990) that generalizes the JIT philosophy into a complete management system aimed at creating **maximum value** for the customer using **minimum resources** by relentlessly identifying and eliminating waste.

Mathematically, the lean value equation is:

$$V = \frac{\text{Value to Customer}}{\text{Total Resources Consumed}}$$

The objective is to **maximize V** by either (a) increasing value-adding activities or (b) decreasing non-value-adding activities.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Refrigerator Model

Imagine your home refrigerator. You do **not** store six months of groceries inside it. You buy vegetables, milk, and bread in **small quantities**, **frequently**, only **what your family will consume before it spoils**.

- The refrigerator = your **factory floor**
- Frequent small grocery trips = **frequent small supplier deliveries**
- Spoilage prevention = **zero inventory carrying cost**
- Buying only what's needed = **pull system (Kanban)**

This is exactly the JIT/Lean mindset — produce **only what is needed, only when it is needed, and only in the quantity needed**.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics You Must Memorize

- **Takt Time** = $T_t = \dfrac{\text{Available Production Time}}{\text{Customer Demand}}$
- **Lead Time** = the time between order placement and order fulfillment
- **Cycle Time** = the time to complete one unit of production
- **Pitch (Heijunka)** = $T_t \times \text{Bundle Size}$
- **Inventory Turns (Turnover Ratio)** = $\dfrac{\text{Cost of Goods Sold}}{\text{Average Inventory}}$ — **target: 50+ for Lean plants**
- **On-Time Delivery (OTD)** — **target: 100%**
- **First Pass Yield (FPY)** — **target: ≥ 99%**

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Module 4 specifically tests the relationship between **JIT, Lean, TQM (Total Quality Management), and Six Sigma**, expecting students to articulate them as **complementary, not competing**, philosophies.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 7 (Seven) Wastes of Lean (TIMWOOD)

Every KTU question on Lean expects you to enumerate and explain the **7 wastes** (sometimes 8, with non-utilized talent). This is a guaranteed 3–5 mark item.

| Symbol | Waste (Japanese) | English Term | Practical Example |
|--------|------------------|--------------|--------------------|
| **T** | Transport | Unnecessary movement of materials | Forklift moving parts 500 m unnecessarily |
| **I** | Inventory | Excess raw material / WIP / FG | Piles of boxes blocking aisles |
| **M** | Motion | Unnecessary human movement | Worker walking to fetch tools repeatedly |
| **W** | Waiting | Idle time of workers/machines | Operator waiting for machine to finish |
| **O** | Overproduction | Producing more than demanded | Producing 1,000 units when only 800 ordered |
| **O** | Over-processing | Doing more work than customer values | Hand-polishing a surface no one will see |
| **D** | Defects | Rework, scrap, warranty | 5 defective units per 1000 produced |
| **+** | Skills (8th) | Underutilized talent | Engineer sweeping floor |

## 2.2 The 5S Workplace Organization Methodology

A prerequisite for JIT/Lean implementation:

1. **Seiri** — Sort (remove unnecessary items)
2. **Seiton** — Set in order (a place for everything)
3. **Seiso** — Shine (clean the workplace)
4. **Seiketsu** — Standardize (procedures)
5. **Shitsuke** — Sustain (discipline)

## 2.3 The Pull System — Kanban

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kanban** (Japanese: 看板, meaning "signal card") is the **visual signaling mechanism** that implements the pull-based production flow in a JIT system. It is the *physical* manifestation of a demand signal traveling upstream.

There are **two main types** of Kanban:
- **Production Kanban (P-Kanban)** — authorizes the upstream workstation to produce
- **Withdrawal Kanban (W-Kanban)** — authorizes the downstream workstation to withdraw material

## 2.4 The High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\\` line breaks inside the table; **never** use the bare pipe `|` for absolute value — write `\vert` or `\mid` instead to keep markdown tables intact.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Standard Unit / Target |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------------------------|
| 1 | Takt Time | $T_t = \dfrac{T_a}{D}$ where $T_a$ = available time/day, $D$ = demand/day | seconds/unit |
| 2 | Lead Time (LT) | $LT = \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i$ (sum of operation times) | days/hours |
| 3 | Manufacturing Lead Time | $MLT = n \times q + \sum W_i$ where $n$ = stages, $q$ = queue time, $W_i$ = wait times | hours |
| 4 | Number of Kanbans | $K = \dfrac{D \cdot L \cdot (1+S)}{C}$ | dimensionless integer |
| 5 | Kanban Size (Container) | $C$ = pieces per container | pieces |
| 6 | Economic Lot Size (classical) | $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | units |
| 7 | Set-up Cost Reduction | $C_{new} = C_{old} \times \dfrac{T_{old}}{T_{new}}$ | currency |
| 8 | Inventory Carrying Cost | $I_c = H \cdot \dfrac{Q}{2}$ | currency/time |
| 9 | Throughput Time | $TPT = \text{Process Time} + \text{Queue Time} + \text{Move Time} + \text{Wait Time}$ | hours |
| 10 | OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness) | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ (Availability × Performance × Quality) | percentage; **target: ≥ 85%** |

**Variable definitions used in the table:**
- $D$ = Annual demand (units/year)
- $L$ = Lead time (days)
- $S$ = Safety stock factor (typically 0 to 0.20)
- $C$ = Container size
- $H$ = Holding cost per unit per year
- $A, P, Q$ = Availability, Performance, Quality ratios (each between 0 and 1)

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

JIT/Lean is not a management fad — it is an **engineering discipline**. Its applications include:

- **Automotive assembly lines** (Toyota, Tesla Gigafactories)
- **Aerospace component manufacturing** (Boeing's moving assembly lines)
- **Software development** (the "Lean Startup" methodology)
- **Healthcare** (Virginia Mason Medical Center, Seattle)
- **Construction** (Lean Construction Institute's "Last Planner System")

> The **strategic objective** of Lean is to compress Manufacturing Lead Time (MLT). A reduction from 30 days → 3 days drops WIP by 90%, freeing working capital, reducing defects, and increasing responsiveness.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Number of Kanbans (Toyota Formula)

We now derive the classical Toyota Kanban formula used in nearly every KTU numerical question.

### Step 1 — Define the Variables

Let:
- $D$ = demand rate (units per unit time)
- $L$ = lead time to replenish one container (same time units as $D$)
- $C$ = pieces per container
- $S$ = safety stock fraction (decimal, e.g., $S = 0.10$ for 10%)

### Step 2 — Total Pieces in the System

During lead time $L$, the demand that must be satisfied equals $D \cdot L$. The number of full containers required to cover this demand (ignoring safety) is:

$$N_0 = \dfrac{D \cdot L}{C}$$

### Step 3 — Add the Safety Buffer

To absorb variability (machine breakdowns, quality rejects, supplier delays), a safety factor $(1 + S)$ is applied:

$$K = \dfrac{D \cdot L \cdot (1 + S)}{C}$$

### Step 4 — Round Up to Integer

The final number of Kanban cards in circulation must be a **whole number** (you cannot have 5.3 cards). Always round **up**:

$$K_{final} = \left\lceil \dfrac{D \cdot L \cdot (1 + S)}{C} \right\rceil$$

### Worked Example (Model KTU Numerical)

**Problem (KTU Pattern):** A workstation uses parts at a rate of **200 units/day**. The lead time to receive a new container is **0.5 days**. Each container holds **25 units**. The safety factor is **10%**. Determine the number of Kanbans required.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Identify values.} \quad & D = 200, \; L = 0.5, \; C = 25, \; S = 0.10 \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2: Substitute into formula.} \quad & K = \frac{D \cdot L \cdot (1+S)}{C} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 3: Numerator computation.} \quad & D \cdot L = 200 \times 0.5 = 100 \\[4pt]
& 100 \times (1 + 0.10) = 100 \times 1.10 = 110 \\[4pt]
\text{Step 4: Divide by container size.} \quad & K = \frac{110}{25} = 4.4 \\[4pt]
\text{Step 5: Round up.} \quad & K_{final} = \lceil 4.4 \rceil = 5 \; \text{Kanbans}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** **5 Kanban cards** are required to maintain the pull system.

## 3.2 Derivation of Takt Time (Demand-Paced Production)

### Step 1 — Available Production Time

A factory operates **8 hours/day**, with two **30-minute** breaks. Compute net available time:

$$T_a = (8 \times 60) - (2 \times 30) = 480 - 60 = 420 \text{ minutes/day}$$

### Step 2 — Daily Customer Demand

If customer demand is **840 units/day**, then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_t &= \frac{T_a}{D} \\
&= \frac{420 \text{ minutes}}{840 \text{ units}} \\
&= 0.5 \text{ minutes/unit} \\
&= 30 \text{ seconds/unit}
\end{aligned}
$$

This means **one unit must leave the workstation every 30 seconds** to satisfy demand. This is the *heartbeat* of the Lean line.

## 3.3 Derivation of Set-Up Cost Reduction Impact

### Step 1 — Classical EOQ Lot Size

Given annual demand $D$, set-up cost $S$, holding cost $H$:

$$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}$$

### Step 2 — Impact of JIT Set-Up Reduction

Suppose set-up time is reduced by **75%** (Toyota's SMED target). The new set-up cost $S_{new}$:

$$S_{new} = S_{old} \times \frac{T_{old}}{T_{new}}$$

### Worked Example

Given $D = 10{,}000$ units/year, $H = ₹5$/unit/year, $S_{old} = ₹2000$, set-up time reduced from 60 min → 15 min.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{new} &= 2000 \times \frac{60}{15} = 2000 \times 4 = ₹8000 \quad \text{(note: cost may rise but frequency falls)} \\
Q^*_{old} &= \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10000 \times 2000}{5}} = \sqrt{8{,}000{,}000} = 2828.4 \text{ units} \\
Q^*_{new} &= \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10000 \times 8000}{5}} = \sqrt{32{,}000{,}000} = 5656.9 \text{ units} \\
\text{Holding cost savings} &= H \times \left(\frac{Q^*_{old}}{2} - \frac{Q^*_{new}}{2}\right) \\
&= 5 \times (1414.2 - 2828.4) \\
&= 5 \times (-1414.2) = -₹7071
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Insight:** Reducing set-up time allows **smaller lot sizes**, which **reduces WIP inventory** — this is the *fundamental economic engine* of JIT. The set-up cost may rise per event, but the **frequency of set-ups rises more than proportionally**, lowering average inventory.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Push vs. Pull Systems

| Parameter | Push System (Traditional MRP) | Pull System (JIT/Kanban) |
|-----------|-------------------------------|---------------------------|
| Trigger | Forecast (forecast-driven) | Actual demand (order-driven) |
| Inventory | High WIP | Minimal WIP |
| Lead Time | Long (weeks) | Short (days) |
| Flexibility | Low | High |
| Defect propagation | Amplifies (one defect → 1000) | Stops the line (jidoka) |
| Information flow | Centralized computer | Decentralized visual cards |
| Capital tied up | High | Low |
| Risk of stockout | Low (but high obsolescence) | Higher (but near-zero obsolescence) |

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The JIT Pull Flow — Block Architecture

This diagram captures how a Kanban signal cascades upstream through the supply chain in a Lean system.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeC[Customer Demand] --> nodeR[Retail Outlet Withdrawal]
    nodeR --> nodeW[Warehouse Withdrawal Kanban]
    nodeW --> nodeF[Factory Production Kanban]
    nodeF --> nodeS[Supplier Withdrawal]
    nodeS --> nodeA[Assembly Line One Piece Flow]
    nodeA --> nodeC

    nodeW -. Visual Signal .-> nodeF
    nodeF -. Empty Container Returns .-> nodeS

    classDef pull fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#1f77b4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;
    classDef signal fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#ff7f0e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;
    class nodeC,nodeR,nodeW,nodeF,nodeS,nodeA pull;
    class nodeW,nodeF signal;
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Customer demand initiates the signal.
- Withdrawal Kanban travels **upstream**; production Kanban authorizes **upstream production**.
- Empty containers physically return to start the cycle again.
