# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: Distinguish between Push and Pull production systems. Which is preferred under JIT and why?**

**Model Answer:**
A **push system** produces goods based on **forecasts** and pushes them down the supply chain, irrespective of actual demand. Information flows **downstream** while material flows **downstream**.

A **pull system** produces goods based on **actual customer demand**, triggered by a **Kanban signal** that flows **upstream**. Only what is consumed is replenished.

**JIT prefers a pull system** because it (i) eliminates overproduction, (ii) minimizes WIP inventory, (iii) exposes defects immediately (jidoka), and (iv) drastically reduces lead time. The system is demand-driven, ensuring zero obsolescence.

*(Word count target: ~100 words — fits 3-mark schema.)*

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: List the Seven Wastes (TIMWOOD) of Lean with one engineering example each.**

**Model Answer:**
- **T**ransport — Unnecessary forklift movement of parts across the factory
- **I**nventory — Pile-up of WIP between two workstations
- **M**otion — Operator walking to fetch a tool repeatedly
- **W**aiting — CNC machine idle while operator loads the next part
- **O**verproduction — Producing 1,000 units when order is for 800
- **O**ver-processing — Hand-polishing a surface that will be painted over
- **D**efects — 5 defective pistons per 1,000 requiring rework

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — *JIT Kanban & Calculations*

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q: A manufacturing cell consumes components at the rate of 800 units/day. The lead time to receive a replenished container is 0.4 days. Each container holds 50 units. Safety factor is 12%. Determine:**
**(i) Number of Kanbans required.**
**(ii) Total WIP at any time.**
**(iii) Comment on what happens if lead time increases to 0.8 days.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Number of Kanbans:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K &= \frac{D \cdot L \cdot (1+S)}{C} \\
&= \frac{800 \times 0.4 \times (1+0.12)}{50} \\
&= \frac{800 \times 0.4 \times 1.12}{50} \\
&= \frac{358.4}{50} \\
&= 7.168 \\
K_{final} &= \lceil 7.168 \rceil = 8 \; \text{Kanbans}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Total WIP in the system:**

$$WIP = K_{final} \times C = 8 \times 50 = 400 \text{ units}$$

**(iii) If lead time doubles to 0.8 days:**

$$
K_{new} = \frac{800 \times 0.8 \times 1.12}{50} = \frac{716.8}{50} = 14.336 \Rightarrow 15 \text{ Kanbans}
$$

$$WIP_{new} = 15 \times 50 = 750 \text{ units}$$

**Comment:** Doubling lead time **doubles the WIP**, negating Lean benefits. This is why **supplier proximity** and **reliable logistics** are critical for JIT success.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q: Explain the role of SMED and Heijunka in a JIT system. How do they reduce waste?**

**Model Solution:**

**SMED (Single Minute Exchange of Dies):**
- A set-up reduction methodology developed by **Shigeo Shingo** at Toyota.
- Converts **internal set-up activities** (done while machine is stopped) into **external set-up activities** (done while machine is running).
- Target: set-up time **under 10 minutes** ("single digit minutes").
- **Waste reduced:** Waiting, Overproduction (via smaller lot sizes), Inventory.

**Heijunka (Production Leveling):**
- Levels the **mix and volume** of production across the planning horizon to avoid peaks and troughs.
- Uses the **pitch formula** to produce small, repeated batches of varied models (e.g., 2 red cars + 2 blue cars, not 100 red then 100 blue).
- **Waste reduced:** Inventory (WIP), Waiting (level workload), Overproduction (matches demand).

**Integration with JIT:** Both SMED and Heijunka are **prerequisites** to a functional Kanban system. Without fast set-ups (SMED), small-lot production is uneconomic; without leveled production (Heijunka), Kanban fluctuates erratically.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Lean Philosophy & Waste Elimination*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Module 4]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q: Define Lean Operations. Explain with examples how the 7 wastes (TIMWOOD) can be eliminated in an Indian automotive component manufacturing plant.**

**Model Solution:**

**Lean Operations Definition:**
Lean Operations is a management philosophy focused on **maximizing customer value** while **minimizing waste** (muda), unevenness (mura), and overburden (muri).

**TIMWOOD Elimination Examples in an Indian Auto Plant:**

| Waste | Indian Plant Scenario | Lean Countermeasure |
|-------|------------------------|----------------------|
| Transport | Forklifts moving parts 300 m across shop floor | Re-layout using cellular manufacturing |
| Inventory | 45 days of WIP buffers between machines | One-piece flow + Kanban |
| Motion | Workers walking 2 km/shift for tools | 5S + shadow boards at point-of-use |
| Waiting | CNC machines idle for 30 min/cycle | TPM, SMED, load leveling |
| Overproduction | Producing 1,200 units when order is 900 | Takt-time-based pull system |
| Over-processing | Hand-deburring after automated machining | Poka-Yoke (mistake-proofing) fixtures |
| Defects | 3% rejection at customer end | Jidoka (autonomation) + Andon system |

**Outcome:** Lead time reduction **from 20 days → 5 days**, WIP reduction **70%**, defect reduction **85%**.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q: Discuss the relationship between JIT, TQM, and Six Sigma. Why are these three treated as complementary rather than competing philosophies in modern production management?**

**Model Solution:**

The three philosophies form a **complementary triad**:

- **JIT** addresses the **flow dimension** — eliminates waste, reduces inventory, compresses lead time. It answers: *"Are we making only what is needed?"*
- **TQM** addresses the **quality dimension** — engages all employees in continuous improvement, customer focus, and process ownership. It answers: *"Are we doing things right the first time?"*
- **Six Sigma** addresses the **variation dimension** — uses DMAIC (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control) and statistical tools to drive defects below **3.4 DPMO**. It answers: *"How predictable and stable are our processes?"*

**Why Complementary?**
- A JIT system **without** quality = defective parts stop the entire line → chaos.
- A TQM system **without** flow = perfect parts produced inefficiently → high cost.
- A Six Sigma program **without** JIT = optimized process making the wrong quantities.

**Synergy:** JIT creates the *demand*, TQM provides the *quality foundation*, and Six Sigma provides the *analytical rigor*. Together, they form the **"Lean Six Sigma"** methodology, now the gold standard in KTU-aligned industry practice.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to round up** Kanban values — students write `K = 4.4` and lose 1 mark. Always write $K_{final} = \lceil \cdot \rceil = 5$.
> 2. **Confusing Takt Time with Cycle Time** — Takt time is set by **demand**, cycle time is set by **process capability**. They are equal only in a perfectly balanced line.
> 3. **Mishandling absolute value symbols** in equations inside answer sheets — write `|x|` clearly outside table cells.
> 4. **Listing only 6 wastes** instead of 7 (TIMWOOD) — Japanese tutors count the "+Skills" or list "DOWNSMIT" to remember all 8.
> 5. **Stating "JIT = zero inventory"** — this is incorrect. JIT aims for **minimal inventory**, not zero; zero inventory is the theoretical asymptote.
> 6. **Confusing P-Kanban with W-Kanban** — P-Kanban authorizes *production*, W-Kanban authorizes *withdrawal*. This distinction appears in KTU 2-mark sub-questions.
> 7. **Skipping the safety factor** $(1+S)$ in Kanban formulas — a recurring 1-mark penalty.
> 8. **Writing TPS instead of TPS** for Toyota Production System — both are accepted but maintain consistency.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

**Core Philosophy**
- JIT = Pull-based, demand-driven, zero-waste philosophy (Taiichi Ohno, Toyota, 1950s–70s)
- Lean = Western umbrella term (Womack & Jones, MIT, 1990)
- Three enemies of Lean: **Muda** (waste), **Mura** (unevenness), **Muri** (overburden)

**The 7+1 Wastes — TIMWOOD + Skills**
- Transport, Inventory, Motion, Waiting, Overproduction, Over-processing, Defects, [Skills]
- Remember the mnemonic: **"Transport Involves Moving Workers Over Obvious Distances"**

**The 5S Housekeeping**
- Seiri (Sort) → Seiton (Set) → Seiso (Shine) → Seiketsu (Standardize) → Shitsuke (Sustain)

**Key Japanese Terms (must-know glossary)**
- **Kanban** = visual signal card (pull trigger)
- **Jidoka** = autonomation (stop-the-line on defect)
- **Andon** = visual signal board
- **Poka-Yoke** = mistake-proofing device
- **Kaizen** = continuous incremental improvement
- **Kaikaku** = radical breakthrough improvement
- **Heijunka** = production leveling
- **SMED** = single minute exchange of dies (Shigeo Shingo)
- **TPM** = total productive maintenance
- **Takt** = German "rhythm"; pace-of-production time

**Critical Formulas (must memorize)**
- $T_t = \dfrac{T_a}{D}$ — Takt Time
- $K = \left\lceil \dfrac{D \cdot L \cdot (1+S)}{C} \right\rceil$ — Number of Kanbans
- $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ — Economic Lot Size
- $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ — Overall Equipment Effectiveness
- $MLT = n \cdot q + \sum W_i$ — Manufacturing Lead Time

**Lean Six Sigma Synergy**
- JIT = Flow focus → Lean
- TQM = Quality culture → TQM
- Six Sigma = Variation control → Six Sigma
- Combined = **Lean Six Sigma** (modern industry standard)

**Five Zero Objectives (KTU favorite)**
- Zero Defects, Zero Lot Size (one-piece flow), Zero Setups, Zero Breakdowns, Zero Handling
